Gaming and AI chips use different types of memory, but both are made of the same raw materials coming from one of three main suppliers: Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, and Micron Technologies.
turns.zh: 上周，苹果CEO蒂姆·库克表示，内存芯片价格上涨将影响公司三月份的季度利润率。他还提到在台积电获得产线时间方面有些困难。因此，整个科技市场都存在许多瓶颈。他暗示未来影响会更大，并指出：“我们确实看到内存市场价格持续大幅上涨。一如既往，我们会考虑各种应对方案。”游戏和AI芯片使用不同类型的内存，但两者都由来自三星电子、SK海力士和美光科技这三大主要供应商的相同原材料制成。

turns.en: Well, without further ado, our first guest of the show, Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI. He's in the green room waiting. Welcome to the show, Sam. How are you doing?
turns.zh: 那么，话不多说，我们节目的第一位嘉宾，OpenAI的CEO山姆·奥特曼。他正在休息室等候。欢迎来到节目，山姆。你好吗？

turns.en: Welcome back.
turns.zh: 欢迎回来。

turns.en: Good. Thank you guys for having me back.
turns.zh: 很好。谢谢你们再次邀请我。

turns.en: Thanks so much. Big day.
turns.zh: 非常感谢。重要的一天。

turns.en: Big day.
turns.zh: 重要的一天。

turns.en: Kick us off. Where should we start? Should we start with the model or Frontier?
turns.zh: 开始吧。我们从哪里开始？从模型开始还是从Frontier开始？

turns.en: Can we start with the model? Just because I'm having fun with it.
turns.zh: 我们能从模型开始吗？因为我玩得很开心。

turns.en: Yeah, absolutely. Break it down. What did you launch?
turns.zh: 当然可以。详细说说。你们发布了什么？

turns.en: We launched 5.3 Codex. It is, I think, the best coding model in the world. We took a lot of the feedback that people had about 5.2 Codex and got it into one model. It is much smarter at programming, but it's also way faster. You can interact with it mid-turn. I think it's got a much better personality. It's really good at computer use. So it feels like a very big step forward. It was funny as we were deploying it this morning, a couple of extremely expert users of these models noticed and said, "Man, something's really different with Codex." And they caught it mid-deploy. So I think you can really feel it quickly.
turns.zh: 我们发布了5.3 Codex。我认为它是世界上最好的编程模型。我们收集了人们对5.2 Codex的大量反馈，并将其整合到一个模型中。它在编程方面更聪明，但也更快。你可以中途与它交互。我觉得它的个性更好了。它非常擅长计算机使用。所以感觉像是向前迈出了一大步。有趣的是，今天早上我们部署它的时候，几个非常精通这些模型的专家用户注意到了，并说：“天哪，Codex真的不一样了。”他们在部署过程中就发现了。所以我认为你很快就能感受到。

turns.en: That's great. Oh, you're saying people outside of OpenAI, just everyday use?
turns.zh: 太棒了。你是说OpenAI之外的人，日常使用？

turns.en: Yeah, like the start of an hour that we put it out before we...
turns.zh: 是的，就像我们发布后的一小时内，在我们……

turns.en: Talk about interacting mid-turn. How does that work? Why is that important? What does it unlock?
turns.zh: 谈谈中途交互。这是如何工作的？为什么重要？它解锁了什么？

turns.en: So people are starting to use these tools for very long pieces of work at one time, you know, multi-hour tasks. And sometimes you don't specify it correctly, right? Sometimes something's not set up right. Something just screws up. The ability to steer them along the way is crucial. They can do amazing things with no steering, but they can do much more amazing things if you steer them along the way. So this has been one of the things I felt most new about this model.
turns.zh: 人们开始一次性使用这些工具处理非常长的工作，比如多小时的任務。有时你指定得不对，对吧？有时某些东西设置不对。有些东西就是搞砸了。沿途引导它们的能力至关重要。它们可以在没有引导的情况下做出惊人的事情，但如果你沿途引导它们，它们可以做出更惊人的事情。所以这是我觉得这个模型最新颖的地方之一。

turns.en: So talk about orchestration, how this fits into Frontier, because I imagine...
turns.zh: 那么谈谈编排，这如何融入Frontier，因为我想象……

turns.en: One second. It's notable: if you see a coworker making a mistake and you don't interrupt them, that's rude, right? It's deeply inefficient.
turns.zh: 等一下。值得注意的是：如果你看到同事犯错而不打断他们，那很粗鲁，对吧？这非常低效。

turns.en: It is incredible what these models can do without any feedback. Like if you think about a new coworker, especially, you train them and give them a lot of feedback early on, they learn the job, you correct them, they get practice. The models will soon do that, but right now they don't. So we just rely on either they get it right one shot or we correct them along the way.
turns.zh: 这些模型在没有反馈的情况下能做的事情令人难以置信。就像如果你想到一个新同事，尤其是，你训练他们，早期给他们很多反馈，他们学习工作，你纠正他们，他们得到练习。模型很快也会做到这一点，但现在它们还做不到。所以我们只能依赖要么它们一次做对，要么我们沿途纠正它们。

turns.en: Yeah. So there are a lot of people running multiple agents and multiple tabs. They're starting to think about orchestration. Frontier feels like a piece of that. But if you're interacting with a model that's running mid-turn, how does the user experience change for developers with 5.3? And then what will it look like in the Frontier world?
turns.zh: 是的。所以有很多人在运行多个智能体和多个标签。他们开始考虑编排。Frontier似乎是其中的一部分。但如果你与一个中途运行的模型交互，对于开发者来说，5.3的用户体验如何改变？然后在Frontier世界中会是什么样子？

turns.en: I think we will be heading towards a workflow where a lot of people just feel like they're managing a team of agents. As the agents get better, they'll keep operating at a higher and higher level of abstraction. At least watching what's happening so far, that is a jump that people are going to make pretty well. The models are so good now. There's such a capability overhang that building better tools to let people do that—which the Codex app we launched on Monday was a great step forward for—will be very important. But you will be managing very complex workflows. The agents will keep getting better, so you'll keep working at the maximum of your management bandwidth or cognitive ability to keep track of all the stuff. And the tools to make that easy to do will matter, I think, more than intelligence for a little while, because there's such an intelligence overhang already.
turns.zh: 我认为我们将走向一个工作流程，很多人感觉就像在管理一个智能体团队。随着智能体变得更好，它们将在越来越高的抽象层次上运作。至少从目前观察到的情况来看，人们会很好地实现这一跳跃。模型现在非常出色。能力过剩如此严重，以至于构建更好的工具让人们做到这一点——我们周一发布的Codex应用在这方面迈出了一大步——将非常重要。但你将管理非常复杂的工作流程。智能体会不断改进，所以你将一直以你的管理带宽或认知能力的上限工作，以跟踪所有事情。而让这变得容易的工具，我认为在一段时间内会比智能更重要，因为智能已经过剩了。

turns.en: What's the role of a forward deployed engineer today at OpenAI towards the end of the year? Capability overhang feels like raw meat for a forward deployed engineer. It's like they solve that problem, right?
turns.zh: 到今年年底，OpenAI的前沿部署工程师的角色是什么？能力过剩对前沿部署工程师来说就像生肉。他们就是解决那个问题的，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah, I mean, look, eventually the models will get so good that they'll help companies deploy themselves, and the forward deployed engineers will again get to work at a higher level of abstraction. But for now, you go into a company that is not AI native and say, "Okay, you've said you want to deploy AI." They really are not sure what to do: how do I hook this up to my systems? Do I need to fine-tune a model on my codebase? How do I think about orchestrating agents and using things from different companies? Most of all, or at least what we hear most frequently, is how do I think about security of my data and how do I know that these AI coworking agents are not going to go access a bunch of information and share it in ways they shouldn't, or get a context exploit or something like that. So the forward engineers take this incredible new technology and a platform like Frontier and say, "We will connect your company to an AI platform so that you can use all these agents and workflows and everything else you want."
turns.zh: 是的。是的，我的意思是，看，最终模型会变得非常好，它们会帮助公司自我部署，而前沿部署工程师将再次在更高的抽象层次上工作。但就目前而言，你进入一家非AI原生公司，说：“好吧，你说你想部署AI。”他们真的不确定该怎么做：如何将它连接到我的系统？我需要对代码库进行微调吗？如何考虑编排智能体和使用不同公司的产品？最重要的是，或者至少我们最常听到的是，如何考虑数据安全，以及如何知道这些AI协作智能体不会访问大量信息并以不应该的方式共享，或者遭受上下文利用之类的事情。所以前沿部署工程师利用这项令人难以置信的新技术和像Frontier这样的平台，说：“我们将把你的公司连接到一个AI平台，这样你就可以使用所有这些智能体、工作流程以及你想要的任何其他东西。”

turns.en: How important are these metaphors or how temporary are they? I was very interested in reading about Gas Town and you have these pole cats and it's this whole Mad Max world. That feels like maybe just a temporary aberration where you're setting up agents for specific tasks, but also that could be incredibly valuable in explaining to a large corporation how they're going to integrate AI across the whole organization.
turns.zh: 这些比喻有多重要，或者它们有多临时？我对读到关于Gas Town的内容非常感兴趣，你们有这些pole cats，整个疯狂的麦克斯世界。这感觉可能只是一个暂时的异常，你为特定任务设置智能体，但这也可能在向大公司解释他们将如何在整个组织中整合AI时非常有价值。

turns.en: Yeah, I suspect like everything else that's happening when an industry is moving so fast, all of this is somewhat temporary on a long enough time scale. As these models become more capable and these agents are operating on very long time horizons with the ability to just kind of figure it out, and our trust and their robustness keeps going up, then maybe you don't need a lot of the abstractions we need today.
turns.zh: 是的，我怀疑就像在一个快速发展的行业中发生的一切一样，所有这些在足够长的时间尺度上都是暂时的。随着这些模型变得更有能力，这些智能体在很长的时间范围内运作，能够自己解决问题，我们的信任和它们的稳健性不断提高，那么也许你就不需要我们今天需要的很多抽象了。

transcript.secZh: 内存芯片价格与市场瓶颈

turns.en: You know, maybe you just have a single AI bot that runs at your company and you can say, 'Hey, I want to launch this new product' and it does everything an ambitious person would do. But that's not where we are today. So today we have to put in a little more work to get the pieces put together.
turns.zh: 你知道，也许你公司里只有一个AI机器人，你可以说，‘嘿，我想推出这个新产品’，然后它就会完成一个雄心勃勃的人会做的一切。但我们现在还做不到。所以今天我们必须多花点功夫把各个部分拼凑起来。

turns.en: Yeah. How are you thinking about the meter benchmark for long task horizon? You're at the top of the charts. At the same time, it feels like we might need a new chart if we're talking about agent swarms because they'll be able to do things that go for weeks, but they will subdivide the work. There's some subdivision that happens within a reasoning model, but it doesn't truly parallelize, at least that I'm aware of. So what does it look like in a world where you go to a model, but now it's spinning up a whole bunch of different models underneath?
turns.zh: 是的。你怎么看待长任务时间跨度的基准测试？你们在排行榜上名列前茅。同时，如果我们谈论智能体集群，感觉可能需要一个新的排行榜，因为它们能处理持续数周的任务，但会细分工作。推理模型内部有一些细分，但并没有真正的并行化，至少据我所知是这样。那么，在一个你调用一个模型，但它却在底层启动一大堆不同模型的世界里，会是什么样子？

turns.en: I think there are two key insights of the whole field in this question. Number one, the implication that no chart in AI lasts more than a few years is right. And this one, we'll see how much longer it's really useful.
turns.zh: 我认为这个问题包含了整个领域的两个关键见解。第一，AI领域的任何排行榜都不会持续超过几年，这个暗示是对的。至于这个排行榜，我们看看它还能有用多久。

turns.en: The second is a lot of people thought that we're going to need a super long task and a super long task horizon, so we need a super long context. And definitely what people have already seen with coding agents is by agents breaking up work, orchestrating it well, farming it off to sub agents, even with the current limitations of the technology, we can do something which should not be surprising because it's similar to how people do things and get amazing amounts of work done. So that's been cool to watch. I think that will keep going.
turns.zh: 第二点是很多人认为我们需要一个超长任务和超长任务时间跨度，所以我们需要超长上下文。但人们已经在编码智能体上看到，通过智能体分解工作、良好编排、分派给子智能体，即使有当前技术的限制，我们也能做到一些事情——这并不令人惊讶，因为这与人类做事并完成大量工作的方式类似。所以看着很酷。我认为这将继续下去。

turns.en: A joke that some people at OpenAI make is that soon the chart that matters is just going to be GDP impact.
turns.zh: OpenAI的一些人开玩笑说，很快重要的排行榜就只是GDP影响了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And then the question is what's the one that comes after that? But everything else, these proxy metrics, there's now so much economic value in what the models are doing.
turns.zh: 然后问题是什么是GDP之后的下一个？但其他所有代理指标，模型所做的工作已经产生了巨大的经济价值。

turns.en: What do you think could come after?
turns.zh: 你认为之后会是什么？

turns.en: I have no idea. Do you have an opinion?
turns.zh: 我不知道。你有想法吗？

turns.en: Happiness.
turns.zh: 幸福感。

turns.en: I don't know.
turns.zh: 我不知道。

transcript.secZh: 当前限制与智能体集群

turns.en: When you look back at some of your blog posts from 10 years ago, your predictions were usually pretty on point.
turns.zh: 当你回顾10年前的一些博客文章时，你的预测通常相当准确。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Maybe it's hard to predict.
turns.zh: 也许很难预测。

turns.en: Thank you. Thank you.
turns.zh: 谢谢。谢谢。

turns.en: Specifically the merge, basically there's a bunch that are good. Like right now we're getting thousands of messages in the chat about 40 and you predicted in 2016 that people would become very attached to a chatbot.
turns.zh: 特别是合并，基本上有很多好的预测。比如现在我们收到成千上万条关于40的聊天消息，而你在2016年就预测人们会对聊天机器人产生强烈依恋。

turns.en: Yeah. I'm working on a big prediction blog post for the next 10 years seems too far, but the next five. But because it's like I'm sure a lot of it'll be wrong and it's still fun to try.
turns.zh: 是的。我正在写一篇大的预测博客文章，未来10年似乎太远了，但未来5年可以。不过因为我很确定很多预测会是错的，但尝试仍然很有趣。

turns.en: The sort of relationships with chatbots clearly that's something now we got to worry about more and is no longer an abstract concept. Even the question of what comes after GDP, one reason I think that's interesting is the way we measure GDP now could start going down even though quality of life goes way up.
turns.zh: 与聊天机器人的关系显然是我们现在需要更多担忧的事情，不再是一个抽象概念。甚至GDP之后是什么这个问题，我认为有趣的一个原因是，我们现在衡量GDP的方式可能会开始下降，即使生活质量大幅上升。

turns.en: Sure.
turns.zh: 当然。

turns.en: And we don't have a lot of practice with things like that. But the massively deflationary...
turns.zh: 而且我们对此类事情没有太多经验。但大规模通缩……

turns.en: Not just Europe, we want quality of life going up too, not just going down, you know.
turns.zh: 不只是欧洲，我们也希望生活质量上升，而不仅仅是下降，你知道的。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

transcript.sec: New research labs and industry research

turns.en: Switching gears, what do you think about the work that the Neol boom, the research efforts that are happening all over Silicon Valley? It feels like there's an acknowledgment that there's research breakthroughs that need to happen and everyone's taking different shots at those. Do you think that those companies will just find a breakthrough and join a lab or launch their own products?
turns.zh: 换个话题，你怎么看待Neol boom以及整个硅谷正在进行的研究工作？感觉大家都承认需要研究突破，每个人都在尝试不同的方向。你认为这些公司会找到突破然后加入一个实验室，还是推出自己的产品？

turns.en: I think it's fantastic. First of all, one of the meta things that we wanted to do when we started OpenAI is that there had been a period where the technology industry in Silicon Valley in particular was amazing at new research labs or just doing new research in industry in general, and then it kind of fell apart and there hadn't been a good one in a while. And part of what we hoped to show, and this was not only us, like a lot of people were excited about new research labs, is that industry could do research again.
turns.zh: 我认为这太棒了。首先，我们创办OpenAI时想做的元事情之一是，曾经有一段时间，特别是硅谷的科技行业在创建新研究实验室或进行行业新研究方面非常出色，然后它有点崩溃了，好一阵子没有好的实验室。我们希望展示的一部分——而且不只是我们，很多对新研究实验室感到兴奋的人——是工业界可以再次做研究。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: So seeing that now become fashionable in all of these new labs, I think it's totally awesome. Some will succeed, some will fail, some will go into some other effort, but having industry support research in startup style, I think it's wonderful.
turns.zh: 所以看到现在这在所有新实验室中变得流行，我觉得非常棒。有些会成功，有些会失败，有些会转向其他努力，但让工业界以创业风格支持研究，我认为这很美妙。

turns.en: Over the next two years, would you expect to acquire more individual product companies or more research labs?
turns.zh: 未来两年，你预计会收购更多独立产品公司还是更多研究实验室？

turns.en: Good question. I don't have a strong opinion there. I would bet the very best ones will often look like a mixture of both. The one that I have in mind right now is something that very much looks like a mixture of both. So maybe that's the shape of things to come: the truly extraordinary product work will have more and more research component, and it'll be kind of a more hybrid thing.
turns.zh: 好问题。我没有强烈的意见。我打赌最优秀的那些往往看起来是两者的混合。我现在想到的一个例子就非常像两者的混合。所以也许未来的形态就是：真正卓越的产品工作将包含越来越多的研究成分，它会变得更混合。

transcript.secZh: 新研究实验室与行业研究

transcript.sec: Data as the new oil and scaling

turns.en: Is data the new oil? Is there value? We were joking a couple days ago that they have a bunch of data, but they don't understand AI. They don't know how to monetize.
turns.zh: 数据是新的石油吗？有价值吗？几天前我们开玩笑说，他们有一堆数据，但他们不懂AI。他们不知道如何变现。

turns.en: Well, and that phrase was effectively wasted a decade ago.
turns.zh: 嗯，那个短语十年前实际上就被浪费了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And so to say it now sounds really silly, but it feels like it could be more true now than ever.
turns.zh: 所以现在说它听起来很傻，但感觉现在可能比以往任何时候都更真实。

turns.en: Yeah. They really did waste it a decade ago. I was just thinking of the kind of people that used to say that and came out of it.
turns.zh: 是的。他们十年前确实浪费了它。我只是在想那些曾经这么说的人，然后他们从中走了出来。

turns.en: TED talks, you're not supposed to call them out.
turns.zh: TED演讲，你不应该点名批评他们。

turns.en: They're nice people over at TED.
turns.zh: TED的人很好。

turns.en: You know, definitely the magic relationship of this last eight years, whatever you want to call it, has been that we can put in more and more resources, compute, data, new ideas, whatever into creating an artifact and it gets like the log of it gets better.
turns.zh: 你知道，过去八年的神奇关系，不管你怎么称呼它，就是我们投入越来越多的资源、算力、数据、新想法等来创造一个产物，然后它的对数会变得更好。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: So you can throw in, that's why we have this huge exponential increase in resources, but we keep getting better and better models. And for all of the concern people have about it's going to top out or it's slowing down or whatever, no one's been right about that. I mean, sometimes it looked like they were for a couple of months as we digested a new model or came to a new form factor, but it has been an incredibly smooth last six or eight years of this. What those resources are.
turns.zh: 所以你可以投入，这就是为什么我们有这种巨大的指数级资源增长，但我们不断得到越来越好的模型。尽管人们担心它会达到顶峰或放缓等等，但没有人说对过。我的意思是，有时看起来他们对了几个月，当我们消化一个新模型或进入新形态时，但过去六八年里这非常平稳。那些资源是什么。

transcript.secZh: 数据是新石油与规模化

turns.en: There can be some trade-off between, you know, sometimes it's better to spend your money on better data, sometimes on more compute, sometimes something else. On the whole, compute power is the new oil is the statement that feels closest to true to me, but there will be other parts too.
turns.zh: 这中间会有一些权衡，你知道，有时候把钱花在更好的数据上更划算，有时候花在更多算力上，有时候是别的东西。总的来说，'算力是新石油'这句话对我来说最接近真相，但也会有其他方面。

turns.en: Makes sense. Is software dead?
turns.zh: 有道理。软件死了吗？

turns.en: It's different. It's definitely not dead. But what software like how you create it, how you're going to use it, how much you're going to have written for you each time you need it versus how much you'll want sort of a consistent UX. That's all going to change. There have been a number of these like big sell-offs of SaaS stocks over the last few years as these models have rolled out. I expect there will continue to be more. I expect there will be big booms in software. I think it's just going to be volatile for a while.
turns.zh: 情况不同了。它绝对没有死。但软件——比如你如何创建它、如何使用它、每次需要时有多少代码会为你自动生成，以及你希望有多少一致的用户体验——这一切都将改变。过去几年，随着这些模型的推出，SaaS股票出现了多次大规模抛售。我预计还会有更多。我也预计软件会出现大繁荣。我认为它只会在一段时间内波动。

turns.en: Mhm. As people figure out what this looks like, the statement that someone said to me that has stuck in my mind most these last couple of weeks is that every company is an API company now whether they want to be or not.
turns.zh: 嗯。随着人们弄清楚这到底是什么样子，过去几周有句话一直印在我脑海里，有人对我说：现在每家公司都是API公司，不管他们愿不愿意。

turns.en: Oh yeah.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。

turns.en: Because agents are just going to be able to write a CS.
turns.zh: 因为智能体将能够编写CS。

turns.en: Yeah. We had D from Uber on yesterday and he had a pretty refreshing kind of approach. We were asking about integrating agents with Uber and he recognized that yeah, the ad business could potentially be threatened if you can order an Uber in ChatGPT, but he basically said like you have to think of the consumer. The consumer wants to order an Uber via their preferred agent, you should let them, otherwise you're going to have other problems.
turns.zh: 是的。昨天我们请来了Uber的D，他的态度相当令人耳目一新。我们问起将智能体与Uber集成的问题，他承认，如果你能在ChatGPT里叫Uber，广告业务确实可能受到威胁，但他基本上说，你必须考虑消费者。消费者想通过他们偏好的智能体叫Uber，你就应该让他们这么做，否则你会遇到其他问题。

turns.en: Yeah, that is the right take for sure. And I don't or I think so at least.
turns.zh: 是的，这肯定是正确的看法。至少我是这么认为的。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And you know, we've been through platform shifts like this before where you I mean Uber wouldn't have existed without one. It wasn't until the iPhone where you could like have it make sense to order an Uber to ride where you were as you were out in the world. So I think there will be totally new things that happen. Other things you'll use in new ways. But definitely as I've started using Codex, my excitement about having agents go off and do things for me and still use other services, pay other services. I'm sure we'll need to figure out new business models and how revenue gets shared around, but that will happen.
turns.zh: 而且你知道，我们以前也经历过这样的平台转变，我的意思是，没有iPhone，Uber就不会存在。直到iPhone出现，你才能在外出时合理地叫一辆Uber到你所在的地方。所以我认为会有全新的事情发生。其他东西你会以新的方式使用。但自从我开始使用Codex，我对让智能体去为我做事、同时仍然使用其他服务、支付其他服务感到兴奋。我相信我们需要找出新的商业模式和收入分配方式，但这会发生。

turns.en: Yeah. Talk more about Codex Desktop.
turns.zh: 是的。再多谈谈Codex Desktop。

turns.en: One more question on SaaS. Have any public market SaaS companies tried to get a soft landing with OpenAI? And do you think there's any value?
turns.zh: 再问一个关于SaaS的问题。有没有公开市场的SaaS公司试图与OpenAI达成软着陆？你认为这有价值吗？

turns.en: Not that I'm no one has no public SaaS companies that I'm aware of have tried that with OpenAI. Look, some of them will certainly be durable and are on sale right now and potentially just need new energy and need kind of a new entirely new approach and maybe OpenAI could provide that.
turns.zh: 据我所知，没有公开的SaaS公司尝试过与OpenAI这样做。你看，其中一些公司肯定会是持久的，现在正在打折，可能只需要新的能量和全新的方法，也许OpenAI可以提供。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Yeah. I think some will be incredibly valuable. Some do feel like a thinner layer now. But I don't know like I was talking recently to a bunch of SaaS companies and they do not feel unexcited like they're like we're going to go through a big transformation here and you know yeah sure other people can instantly write software now but so can we and we got a great system of record and seems reasonable. Some won't make it of course.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为有些会非常有价值。有些现在确实感觉像一层更薄的层。但我不确定，我最近和一群SaaS公司聊过，他们并不感到不兴奋，他们说我们要经历一场大转型，你知道，是的，其他人现在可以即时编写软件，但我们也可以，而且我们有一个很棒的记录系统，这似乎合理。当然，有些会失败。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. Talk more about the Codex Desktop rollout. It feels like a successful amount of downloads but a key shift for people who are maybe lightly technical but don't have time to set up an IDE and configure an environment to actually start writing software. I want to know about plans to integrate to the phone. That was a big moment I think for a lot of people with the Claude bot, the ChatGPT bot, the open thing was like oh I can text something and it will go and write code and that's valuable and that unlocks a new agentic experience. Where do you see the Codex Desktop ecosystem going?
turns.zh: 是的。是的。再多谈谈Codex Desktop的发布。感觉下载量很成功，但对那些可能有点技术背景但没时间设置IDE和配置环境来真正开始写软件的人来说，这是一个关键转变。我想知道整合到手机的计划。我认为对很多人来说，Claude bot、ChatGPT bot、开放的东西是一个重大时刻，就像哦，我可以发个短信，它就会去写代码，这很有价值，解锁了一种新的智能体式体验。你认为Codex Desktop生态系统会走向何方？

turns.en: I am so Codex Desktop has been a somewhat of a surprise to me in terms of how much people love it including how much I love it myself. You know, I think it's a great example of 10% of polish of the experience of using these models, especially when there's so much capability overhang goes an extremely long way to what you can build and how you interact with this stuff. Of course, we should have an ability to kick off new tasks from mobile and we'll do that. I mean really what you want is like your single AI that's working for you on a unified back end, access to all of your data and your ideas and your stuff and your memory and your all the context and the ability to work across a lot of surfaces and often you'll be at your desktop. Often you'll be on your phone and you just want to add something in. But it is a pretty profound shift in my own workflow, not just for coding tasks, but more general purpose tasks. It's still kind of hard to use if you're not at least reasonably technical. But obviously, we'll find a version of this product that can do other knowledge work tasks and control your computer and things like that where you don't have to be and we'll and it'll bring the magic of building stuff really to a lot of people because even if you never look at code, you'll be able to build something reasonably sophisticated. One of the things that I have built when I was playing around with the new Codex app is this thing I had always wanted just like this magic autocompleting to-do list.
turns.zh: Codex Desktop让我有点意外，人们如此喜欢它，包括我自己也非常喜欢。你知道，我认为这是一个很好的例子，说明对使用这些模型的体验进行10%的打磨，尤其是在能力过剩的情况下，对你能够构建什么以及如何与这些东西互动有极大的帮助。当然，我们应该有能力从手机端启动新任务，我们会做到的。我的意思是，你真正想要的是你的单一AI，它在统一的后端为你工作，访问你所有的数据、想法、东西、记忆和所有上下文，并且能够在多个界面上工作，而你通常会在桌面端。你通常会在手机上，只想添加一些东西。但这对我自己的工作流程来说是一个相当深刻的转变，不仅仅是编码任务，还有更通用的任务。如果你至少不是相当技术性的话，它仍然有点难用。但显然，我们会找到这个产品的一个版本，它可以做其他知识工作任务，控制你的电脑等等，你不需要具备技术背景，它将把构建东西的魔力带给很多人，因为即使你从不看代码，你也能够构建相当复杂的东西。我在玩新的Codex应用时构建的东西之一，就是我一直想要的这个神奇的自动完成待办事项列表。

turns.en: Oh yeah. I really work with to-do lists and this idea that I could just put tasks in and it would try to go do them. If it could complete them, it could complete them. If it needed questions, it would ask me questions. If it needed, you know, if I had to do something, I could still do it the old-fashioned way. But an interface like that where, you know, all the stuff you want to do, you just sort of explain to a computer or your AI and it tries to go off and do them. And sure, if you're on your phone, you're going to just add a task on your phone or, you know, if you want to easily import something from email, you're going to do that. It felt really good. So, I'm excited about all of the ways that this will just become a general knowledge work agent.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。我真的使用待办事项列表，这个想法是，我只需把任务放进去，它就会尝试去完成它们。如果它能完成，它就完成。如果需要问题，它会问我问题。如果需要，你知道，如果我必须做某事，我仍然可以用老方法做。但这样的界面，你知道，所有你想做的事情，你只需向电脑或你的AI解释，它就会尝试去完成它们。当然，如果你在手机上，你只需在手机上添加一个任务，或者，你知道，如果你想轻松地从邮件中导入一些东西，你也会这样做。感觉真的很好。所以，我对所有它将成为一个通用知识工作智能体的方式感到兴奋。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Were you unsurprised to see a product like OpenClaw come from open source? Because that kind of user experience is something I imagine you knew would be a thing, and yet part of the magic of OpenClaw is that it would be very difficult for a large tech company to make. Peter didn't make many phone calls to hyperscalers to say, 'Hey, I'm going to be integrating your API.' It just went. And when I think back on the Sky acquisition, this kind of experience was probably top of mind and things you were working towards internally.
turns.zh: 看到像 OpenClaw 这样的产品来自开源，你感到意外吗？因为那种用户体验，我想你早就知道会出现，但 OpenClaw 的魔力在于，大型科技公司很难做到。Peter 没怎么给超大规模云服务商打电话说‘嘿，我要集成你的 API’，它就这么成了。回想 Sky 收购案时，这种体验可能正是你心中所想，也是你们内部在努力的方向。

turns.en: I love the spirit of everything about OpenClaw.
turns.zh: 我喜欢 OpenClaw 的一切精神。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: And you are totally right that it's much easier to imagine a one-person open source project doing something like that than a company that is going to be afraid of lawsuits, data privacy, and everything else. I think this is kind of how innovation works. Something like that starts. It's clearly amazing. There will be a way to make a mass market version of that product, but letting the builders build, the equivalent of the Homebrew Computer Club spirit, is so important.
turns.zh: 你说得完全对，一个单人开源项目做这种事，比一个害怕诉讼、数据隐私等问题的公司要容易得多。我认为这就是创新的方式。这样的东西开始出现，显然很了不起。会有办法做出那个产品的大众市场版本，但让创造者去创造，那种家酿计算机俱乐部的精神，非常重要。

turns.en: Yeah, totally.
turns.zh: 完全同意。

turns.en: Can we switch to social? I feel like if I Google Sam Altman social, I get pure AI and Sora, and also demand or predictions about a humanoid social network. Where do you see social going broadly? How do you want to integrate it with AI and power it in the future? The Moldbook thing was a very interesting social experiment to watch, and I think it points to agents interacting in some sort of social space, hopefully on behalf of people at least to some degree, which could be quite interesting. I don't think we know what to do there yet, but it feels like social is going to change a lot. And I am interested in the space of what a social experience can look like when your agent is talking to my agent and coming up with new stuff. Clearly, putting a lot of AI bots on existing social platforms is just making everyone crazy and not that fun. So that's not the right answer.
turns.zh: 我们能聊聊社交吗？我觉得如果我在谷歌上搜 Sam Altman 社交，出来的全是 AI 和 Sora，还有关于人形社交网络的需求或预测。你大体上认为社交会走向何方？你希望如何将 AI 融入其中并赋能未来？Moldbook 那件事是一个很有趣的社交实验，我认为它指向了智能体在某种社交空间中互动，希望至少在某种程度上代表人类，这可能相当有趣。我觉得我们还没想好怎么做，但感觉社交将发生很大变化。我对这样的社交体验很感兴趣：当你的智能体和我的智能体对话并产生新东西时。显然，在现有社交平台上放大量 AI 机器人只会让所有人抓狂，并不好玩。所以那不是正确答案。

turns.en: But I think we can design something new for what this technology is capable of that will feel good and useful.
turns.zh: 但我认为我们可以为这项技术的能力设计一些新的东西，让它感觉良好且有用。

turns.en: Yeah. Is there a solution to the bot problem that's just all the labs sort of integrating with all the other platforms? And even if you can't detect it's AI generated, you can literally say, 'We just generated those tokens, those exact tokens are in our database.'
turns.zh: 是啊。有没有解决机器人问题的办法，就是所有实验室都与其他平台整合？即使你无法检测出是 AI 生成的，你也可以直接说，‘我们刚刚生成了那些 token，那些确切的 token 就在我们的数据库里。’

turns.en: Can't do that because open source models are good enough to write at this point.
turns.zh: 做不到，因为开源模型现在已经足够好了，能写出来。

turns.en: Yeah. I am excited about sort of assertion of humanity instead of trying to detect AI as a thing here.
turns.zh: 是啊。我对于某种‘人性声明’感到兴奋，而不是试图检测 AI。

turns.en: I don't know if the social platforms have an interest in solving this. It is because it's creating, at least in the short term, a lot of engagement and increased usage. So I believe they could solve it if they wanted to. I'm not sure it's in their interest. I'm actually not even—I don't like it, but some people do seem to enjoy it.
turns.zh: 我不知道社交平台是否有兴趣解决这个问题。因为至少在短期内，它带来了大量参与度和使用量的增长。所以我相信如果他们想解决，是可以做到的。但我不确定这符合他们的利益。实际上我甚至不——我不喜欢这样，但有些人似乎确实乐在其中。

turns.en: Yeah. Can you talk a little bit more about where Sora as a video generation model is going? It feels like tool use is maybe underdiscussed. Adding reasoning, it's not just the diffusion model. It's giving these models the ability to make linear cuts and overlay motion graphics. And when I scroll through Instagram reels, I see vibe reels with cutouts, it flips negative, and it's all color graded. It's stuff that you could probably diffuse it all, but it's pretty cool just to teach a model to also use After Effects or whatever video motion graphics suite you want to use. Is that an interesting unlock? What do you see going on?
turns.zh: 是啊。你能多谈谈 Sora 作为视频生成模型的未来方向吗？感觉工具使用可能被低估了。加入推理，不仅仅是扩散模型。它赋予这些模型进行线性剪辑和叠加动态图形的能力。当我刷 Instagram Reels 时，我看到氛围短片有剪影、负片翻转，还有调色。这些你或许都能用扩散生成，但教模型使用 After Effects 或任何动态图形套件也很酷。这是一个有趣的解锁吗？你怎么看？

turns.en: So all of that stuff will happen and I agree with you. The models will get really great at doing that. People love generating videos. I would say people, we have not yet found a way that people really love watching other people's videos. This is true for a lot of other AI—like they love talking to ChatGPT or whatever, but it's not that compelling for most people to read other people's generations. So I think there is something.
turns.zh: 所以所有这些都会发生，我同意你的看法。模型会变得非常擅长做这些。人们喜欢生成视频。但我要说，我们还没有找到让人们真正喜欢看别人视频的方式。这对很多其他 AI 来说也是如此——人们喜欢和 ChatGPT 之类的东西聊天，但对大多数人来说，看别人生成的东西并没有那么吸引人。所以我认为这里面有点问题。

turns.en: But isn't that for all writing and all video?
turns.zh: 但这对所有写作和所有视频不都一样吗？

turns.en: Yeah, it seems stronger to me in this case than the general case, but maybe you're right.
turns.zh: 是啊，在我看来这种情况比一般情况更明显，但也许你是对的。

turns.en: Yeah, but if somebody says, 'Hey, I generated a 15-minute video, I'm really excited for you to watch it,' and you watch the first 10 seconds and you're not that captured by it, I don't care that it was human.
turns.zh: 是啊，但如果有人说，‘嘿，我生成了一个 15 分钟的视频，我很兴奋想让你看’，你看了前 10 秒并不觉得吸引人，那我才不管它是不是人做的。

turns.en: Yeah, maybe you're right and this is not a special case.
turns.zh: 是啊，也许你是对的，这并非特例。

turns.en: Do you see that in the data with Sora downloads? Because I've noticed that I'll generate stuff on Sora, download it, and share it to a group chat, and then it's this little in-joke that me and five other people get. And we see this like family group chats of 'Oh, it's our dog and our kids,' but there's not really like, 'Okay, yeah, this is a business, everyone likes this.'
turns.zh: 你在 Sora 下载数据中看到这一点了吗？因为我注意到，我会在 Sora 上生成东西，下载下来分享到群聊，然后这成了我和其他五个人之间的小玩笑。我们看到家庭群聊里说‘哦，这是我们的狗和我们的孩子’，但并没有那种‘好吧，这是个生意，大家都喜欢这个’的感觉。

turns.en: Absolutely. I would say that the most common use case is something like that. You know, memes on group chats is a real killer use case of sorts.
turns.zh: 绝对是的。我会说最常见的用例就是那样。你知道，群聊里的表情包算是一种杀手级用例。

turns.en: How is the Disney rollout going? I was super excited about it. Jordy was extremely bullish on it from a business strategy perspective.
turns.zh: 迪士尼的推广进展如何？我对此非常兴奋。Jordy 从商业策略角度非常看好它。

turns.en: Yeah. When you look at how image models have grown various LLMs historically, and now you're going to have an image and video model that can do something that no other LLM can do, at least legally.
turns.zh: 是啊。当你回顾历史上图像模型如何推动各种大语言模型发展时，现在你将拥有一个图像和视频模型，能做其他大语言模型做不到的事，至少在法律上如此。

turns.en: Yeah. And is Bob Iger joining OpenAI? Bob, I love it. No credit for that.
turns.zh: 是啊。Bob Iger 要加入 OpenAI 吗？Bob，我喜欢这个。不邀功。

turns.en: He's going to be looking for a job and a free agent here. He's a free agent. Pick him up.
turns.zh: 他马上要找工作了，是个自由人。签下他。

turns.en: Hit him up for us. You know, do some recruiting. That'd be great.
turns.zh: 替我们联系他。你知道，做点招聘。那太好了。

turns.en: I think that generating characters in images and videos is going to be very important to people and they really like that. Otherwise, as we were saying, I don't think many people want to watch me and some Star Wars character doing something together, but I might think it's cool.
turns.zh: 我认为在图像和视频中生成角色对人们来说会非常重要，他们真的很喜欢。否则，就像我们说的，我不认为很多人想看我和某个星球大战角色一起做点什么，但我可能会觉得这很酷。

turns.en: There's a real trend going on right now with ChatGPT where it's 'Make a caricature of me and my job based off everything you know about me.' And those kinds of things people actually do like looking at other people's media.
turns.zh: 现在 ChatGPT 上有一个真正的趋势：‘根据你对我的所有了解，给我和我的工作画一幅漫画。’那种东西人们确实喜欢看别人的。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: It's almost like a face filter or something. There's enough of the human still in it that it's not—yours is not the same as mine. So it's still personalized.
turns.zh: 这几乎就像个面部滤镜之类的。里面仍然有足够多的人性，所以你的和我的不一样。它仍然是个性化的。

transcript.secZh: 迪士尼合作与角色生成

transcript.sec: Viral Image Generation and Rollout Strategy

turns.en: It's personalized and it says something about you. And a lot of these things that have gone viral before with image gen, I think it's like if you can make people look a little bit more attractive or cool than they are in real life.
turns.zh: 它很个性化，能体现你的特点。之前图像生成领域很多爆款，我觉得就是能让人看起来比现实中更吸引人或更酷。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: Without having to ask for that.
turns.zh: 不用特意要求。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: How are you thinking about the actual rollout? We were debating between like open the floodgates, you can generate any Disney property versus like it's Spider-Man week and everyone's posting Spider-Man and then it's Mickey Mouse week and there's another viral moment.
turns.zh: 你如何看待实际推广策略？我们之前讨论过，是全面放开，允许生成任何迪士尼角色，还是像蜘蛛侠周那样，大家发蜘蛛侠，然后米老鼠周再来一波病毒式传播。

turns.en: I'm not sure what the team is planning there. I know Disney's had some different opinions about what they want to do and we try to be a good partner there, but I'd be excited to open the floodgates personally.
turns.zh: 我不确定团队的具体计划。我知道迪士尼对合作方式有一些不同意见，我们努力做好合作伙伴，但个人而言，我很期待全面放开。

turns.en: Oh, that'd be fun. Cool. Talk about your first speaking video, your first Super Bowl ad. It felt like not generated, lots of motion graphics, the black dots coming together. What was the goal with that ad? Who were you trying to speak to? It didn't feel like a direct response QR code, download the app. What was the mission?
turns.zh: 哦，那会很有趣。酷。聊聊你的第一个演讲视频，第一个超级碗广告。感觉不像生成的，有很多动态图形，黑点汇聚在一起。那个广告的目标是什么？你想对谁说话？它不像直接回应的二维码，下载应用。使命是什么？

turns.en: I love that ad. I think it was such a cool one. It was clearly not meant to be a mass market or direct response ad.
turns.zh: 我喜欢那个广告。我觉得它很酷。它显然不是面向大众市场或直接回应的广告。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: But it was speaking to the people who are at the center of this revolution and just trying to celebrate everything that has come before and everything that will come after.
turns.zh: 但它是在对这场革命的核心人物说话，试图庆祝过去的一切和未来的一切。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: We didn't hear a lot about it from average users of ChatGPT, but we heard a lot from researchers in the field and there was a lot of resonance there. It was definitely not generated. It was done the old fashioned way. And a lot of people loved it, a lot of people hated it, and many in the middle didn't get it, and I felt okay about that.
turns.zh: 普通ChatGPT用户对此反响不大，但领域内的研究者反响强烈，产生了共鸣。它绝对不是生成的，是用传统方式制作的。很多人喜欢，很多人讨厌，中间很多人不理解，我觉得这没问题。

turns.en: It's a great encapsulate.
turns.zh: 总结得很好。

turns.en: I like our ad for Sunday.
turns.zh: 我喜欢我们周日的广告。

turns.en: Okay. It's about Codex, no surprise. But talk about the evolution of the advertising to be more clear about the actual use case, the value. What are you trying to say with your advertising strategy now as it relates to video?
turns.zh: 好的。是关于Codex，不意外。但谈谈广告的演变，如何更清晰地传达实际用例和价值。你现在对视频的广告策略想表达什么？

turns.en: The thing I would most like us to say, and I think this is a new challenge given where the models are, is to teach people what they can go do with AI. I think AI is now unbelievably capable in most of the world. It's still like asking basic questions on ChatGPT. Everyone can go build amazing things now. Everyone can go do all kinds of work. Scientists are going to make new discoveries. And I'd like to, to the degree that advertising we do can teach people how to use this, I think that'd be awesome.
turns.zh: 我最希望我们传达的，也是鉴于模型现状的新挑战，是教会人们能用AI做什么。我认为AI现在在大多数领域已经非常强大，但人们还只是像在ChatGPT上问基本问题。现在每个人都能构建令人惊叹的东西，每个人都能做各种工作。科学家将做出新发现。如果我们的广告能在一定程度上教会人们如何使用它，那就太棒了。

turns.en: So the KPI is like reduce the capability overhang broadly.
turns.zh: 所以关键绩效指标是广泛减少能力过剩。

turns.en: I think that should be a general KPI for us, not just of our ads.
turns.zh: 我认为这应该是我们的总体关键绩效指标，不仅仅是广告。

turns.en: The products that we build, how we teach people to use those products, that feels very important.
turns.zh: 我们构建的产品，以及如何教人们使用这些产品，这感觉非常重要。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

transcript.secZh: 病毒式图像生成与发布策略

transcript.sec: Anthropic's Super Bowl Ads and Criticism

turns.en: Anthropic also had a bunch of ads in the Super Bowl. Seems like they ran a ton. What do you think they got wrong about their characterization of how ads will roll out in chat apps?
turns.zh: Anthropic也在超级碗投放了大量广告。你认为他们对聊天应用广告推广方式的描述哪里错了？

turns.en: Well, it's just wrong. The main thing I think is we are not stupid. We respect our users. We understand that if we did something like what those ads depict, people would rightfully stop using the product. No one, our first principle with ads is that we're not going to put stuff into the LLM stream. That would feel crazy dystopic, like a bad sci-fi movie. So the main thing that's wrong with the ads is using a deceptive ad to criticize deceptive ads. I don't know, something doesn't sit right with me about that. I asked Claude what the definition of playing dirty is, and it said, 'Misleading others about your intentions, hiding information, or creating false impressions.'
turns.zh: 嗯，就是错了。我认为主要问题是，我们不傻。我们尊重用户。我们明白，如果我们做了那些广告所描绘的事情，用户会理所当然地停止使用产品。没有人，我们的首要原则是不把广告塞进大语言模型的输出流中。那会显得疯狂反乌托邦，像糟糕的科幻电影。所以这些广告的主要问题是用欺骗性广告来批评欺骗性广告。我不知道，我对此感到不舒服。我问Claude什么是玩脏的，它说：‘误导他人关于你的意图，隐藏信息，或制造虚假印象。’

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: I thought it was a little dirty. I thought it was well played, but it was well played for sure. And it was a funny ad. And the stuff about the ChatGPT personality that most annoys me, which we'll fix very soon, I thought they nailed in the ad. So that part was funny.
turns.zh: 我觉得有点脏。我觉得它玩得漂亮，确实漂亮。而且是个有趣的广告。关于ChatGPT个性中最让我恼火的部分，我们很快会修复，我觉得他们在广告中抓住了这一点。所以那部分很有趣。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: But I don't know, I also think it's great for them not to do ads. We have a different shaped business. I did notice that they said in their thing like 'we may later revise this decision and we'll explain why.' So, yeah, the blog post kind of did a good job of disarming the pro-ad people and gave themselves an out in the future.
turns.zh: 但我不确定，我也觉得他们不做广告挺好的。我们的业务形态不同。我注意到他们在声明中说‘我们可能稍后修订这一决定，并会解释原因。’所以，那篇博客文章确实很好地安抚了支持广告的人，并给自己留了后路。

turns.en: Do you think they care?
turns.zh: 你觉得他们在意吗？

turns.en: I do matter. Like I think it's a sideshow. People are excited for a food fight between companies, but the amazing capabilities of these models, the product, the groundswell of excitement around Codex, that feels way more important to me.
turns.zh: 我在意。我觉得这只是个插曲。人们热衷于公司间的争斗，但这些模型的惊人能力、产品、围绕Codex的兴奋浪潮，对我来说重要得多。

turns.en: How do you stop the pausing that happens in voice mode? Do you need new hardware for that or is it a model capability thing?
turns.zh: 如何解决语音模式中的停顿问题？需要新硬件还是模型能力问题？

turns.en: We need a new model. We may need some new hardware too, but mostly we just need a new model. I think we'll have a great voice mode by the end of this year.
turns.zh: 我们需要一个新模型。可能也需要一些新硬件，但主要是新模型。我认为今年年底前我们会有一个很棒的语音模式。

turns.en: What's the bigger bottleneck? Energy or chips?
turns.zh: 更大的瓶颈是什么？能源还是芯片？

turns.en: It goes back and forth. Right now again it's chips.
turns.zh: 两者交替。现在又是芯片。

turns.en: Chips. Is there anything society or America should be doing more aggressively to increase the supply of fabs?
turns.zh: 芯片。社会或美国是否应该更积极地增加晶圆厂供应？

turns.en: I think it may get solved on its own. Normal capitalism may solve it. But I think somehow deciding as a society that we are going to increase the wafer capacity of the world and fund that, and get the whole supply chain and talented people we need to make that happen, would be a very good thing to do.
turns.zh: 我认为它可能会自行解决。正常的资本主义可能解决。但我认为，如果社会决定增加全球晶圆产能并为此提供资金，获得所需的整个供应链和人才，那将是一件非常好的事情。

turns.en: Do you think there's an upper bound on model IQ? The race right now is you're smart, but you're not smart for days. You're smart for hours. Can you go much farther and get much smarter?
turns.zh: 你认为模型智商有上限吗？目前的竞赛是，你很聪明，但聪明不了几天，只能聪明几小时。能走得更远，变得更聪明吗？

turns.en: It seems certain. Upper bound, I don't know. I don't know how to think about that question yet.
turns.zh: 似乎必然。上限，我不知道。我还不知道如何思考这个问题。

turns.en: I can't even reason about what 2,000 IQ looks like. I don't know what that means.
turns.zh: 我甚至无法推理2000智商是什么样子。我不知道那意味着什么。

turns.en: It's funny you say. I mean, I can't reason about what it means to think about a problem for like 10,000 human years.
turns.zh: 你这么说很有趣。我的意思是，我无法推理思考一个问题一万人类年意味着什么。

transcript.sec: Initial banter and space data centers

turns.en: That's another good one. Yeah, that's crazy. But maybe IQ is going to feel even weirder. I don't know. I feel like somehow this isn't going to feel as strange as it sounds. For a bunch of reasons, we're so focused on other people, our own lives. We have such a human-centric nature. So okay, this thing is really smart. It's inventing new science for us, running companies, doing all this stuff. That sounds like it should be impossibly weird, and I think it'll just be very weird.
turns.zh: 这又是一个好问题。是啊，太疯狂了。但也许智商（IQ）会感觉更奇怪。我不知道。我总觉得这不会像听起来那么奇怪。原因有很多，我们太关注别人了，太关注自己的生活了。我们太以人类为中心了。所以，好吧，这个东西真的很聪明。它为我们发明新科学，运营公司，做所有这些事情。这听起来应该会非常奇怪，我想它确实会非常奇怪。

turns.en: Do you think space data centers will provide a meaningful amount of compute for OpenAI in the next two to three years, five years?
turns.zh: 你认为太空数据中心会在未来两三年、五年内为 OpenAI 提供有意义的算力吗？

turns.en: No.
turns.zh: 不会。

turns.en: 10 years?
turns.zh: 十年呢？

turns.en: You just keep going. 10,000 years. I wish you luck.
turns.zh: 你继续问吧。一万年。祝你好运。

transcript.secZh: 开场闲聊与太空数据中心

turns.en: The funny thing about the whole back and forth about ads is that in our world, the criticism is that you didn't launch ads early enough. Is there a world where you wish you launched earlier? How is the actual rollout going? Are advertisers happy? Do you have a really long roadmap, or do you think you'll be faster at catching up to what's frontier in ads?
turns.zh: 关于广告的整个争论，有趣的一点是，在我们这个领域，批评是你没有尽早推出广告。有没有一个世界你希望自己更早推出？实际推广进展如何？广告商满意吗？你有一个很长的路线图，还是你认为你会更快地赶上广告的前沿？

turns.en: We haven't even started to test yet. We start to test soon. But it's going to take us some number of iterations to figure out the right ad unit, the right way this all works. Do I wish we had started earlier? We have gone from not a company, three years and three months ago we were a research lab, and now we are a pretty big company with a lot of products. So there are many things I wish we had done faster. I think we were correct on the trade-off here of how we balance things that we need to do. I wish we had launched this very cool enterprise platform this morning. Wish we had done that earlier too, but had to deal with the monstrous growth of ChatGPT and Codex and all that other stuff. Good problems to have.
turns.zh: 我们甚至还没有开始测试。我们很快就要开始测试了。但我们需要几次迭代才能找到合适的广告单元和正确的方式。我是否希望我们更早开始？我们从三年前三个月前还不是一家公司，只是一个研究实验室，到现在是一家拥有很多产品的大公司。所以我希望很多事情能做得更快。我认为我们在权衡需要做的事情上是正确的。我希望我们今天早上推出了这个很酷的企业平台。也希望更早做，但必须应对 ChatGPT 和 Codex 等产品的巨大增长。这是好问题。

transcript.secZh: 广告发布与优先级排序

turns.en: Last question for me. What happened to that internal writing model that you used to write the essay? That feels like something that was really cool, but we never really saw the light of day.
turns.zh: 最后一个问题。那个你用来写文章的内部写作模型后来怎么样了？感觉那真的很酷，但我们从未真正看到它面世。

turns.en: We're going to get a lot of that spirit into a future model. Again, it's like there's so much stuff happening. We have to make these hard prioritization decisions. I would love a cool writing model, not as much as I would love a cool coding model. And it's what is possible now for coding, for science. That's the thing I'm most excited about. For accelerating all kinds of research, AI and otherwise, for really accelerating the economy. I think that's the right thing for us to most prioritize in terms of new capabilities. But you want a model that can write beautifully because it means it can think very clearly and express that very clearly. That's just useful in normal work.
turns.zh: 我们会把那种精神大量融入未来的模型中。再说一次，有太多事情在发生。我们必须做出这些艰难的优先级决策。我很想要一个酷的写作模型，但不如我想要一个酷的编码模型那么强烈。而现在编码和科学领域是可能的。那是我最兴奋的事情。加速各种研究，包括 AI 和其他领域，真正加速经济。我认为那是我们在新能力方面最应该优先考虑的事情。但你也想要一个能写出优美文字的模型，因为那意味着它能非常清晰地思考并表达出来。这在日常工作中很有用。

transcript.secZh: 内部写作模型与优先级

turns.en: Last question for me. How have conversations been with the broad OpenAI leadership team? You guys are in a position where any single word or sentence you say in any situation can be spun into a headline immediately, and then you have to go on damage control correcting the narrative. But of course the original message is often seen more broadly than the correction. It seems like an interesting challenge.
turns.zh: 最后一个问题。与 OpenAI 整个领导团队的对话如何？你们处于这样一种位置：在任何情况下说的任何一个词或句子都可能立即被曲解成头条，然后你们不得不进行危机公关，纠正说法。但当然，原始信息往往比更正更广泛地传播。这似乎是一个有趣的挑战。

turns.en: It is a strange way to live. I don't know of any private company that has ever been so in the news and under a microscope. At some level it's frustrating. We're squarely in the sights of everybody's anxieties and every competitor trying to take us down, and everybody wondering what will happen with AI to their part of the business or their own lives. There's a lot of plasma looking for an instability to collapse on. In some other sense though, the subjective experience is we are so busy on so much exciting stuff that it often feels like there's this crazy hurricane turning around us, and when we sit it's fairly calm. The media or Twitter goes insane about something one day, they're talking about a crazy meltdown. We're like that is insane. People talk about it all day and then later find out it's wrong, and it seemed like a lot of wasted energy. But we're just like we have this great new model coming. People are building incredible stuff. Companies are transforming. We're trying to figure out how to get more compute and deal with this compute crunch. And we just keep going and we're busy. Then if we open Twitter, pop up our heads and look at the news, it's like wow, that is an insane crazy thing happening completely divorced from reality or 99% divorced from reality. Someone will correct it, but then we get back to work and people flip out again. It is weird to watch when we look outside, but it is less chaotic internally than you would imagine from reading the media reports.
turns.zh: 这是一种奇怪的生活方式。我不知道有哪家私营公司曾如此备受新闻关注和显微镜审视。在某种程度上，这令人沮丧。我们完全处于每个人的焦虑和每个试图打倒我们的竞争对手的视线中，每个人都在想 AI 会对他们的业务或生活产生什么影响。有很多等离子体在寻找不稳定性来坍缩。但从另一个意义上说，主观体验是我们忙于太多令人兴奋的事情，以至于常常感觉周围有一场疯狂的飓风，而我们坐下来时却相当平静。媒体或 Twitter 某天对某件事发疯，他们谈论着疯狂的崩溃。我们觉得那太疯狂了。人们整天谈论它，后来发现是错的，看起来浪费了很多精力。但我们只是觉得我们有一个很棒的新模型即将推出。人们在建造不可思议的东西。公司在转型。我们试图弄清楚如何获得更多算力并应对算力紧张。我们只是继续前进，很忙。然后如果我们打开 Twitter，抬起头看新闻，就会觉得哇，那是一件完全脱离现实或 99% 脱离现实的疯狂事情。有人会纠正它，但然后我们回去工作，人们又发疯了。当我们向外看时，这很奇怪，但内部比你在媒体报告中想象的要平静得多。

transcript.sec: Closing remarks and introduction of Sholto

turns.en: Yep, that makes sense. Well, thank you so much for taking the time to come chat with us. Congrats on the... I'm excited to see the Codex ad. Me, too. Please try it. The app and 53 have been like, I think the coolest thing we've done in a while. Yes. With one prompt, I rebuilt the tbpn.com homepage to look exactly like Berkshire Hathaway and it was just immediate. It was very fun. Interesting choice. Plain text. It was very easy. Immediately one-shot it. Did not really push it to its limits, but I'm having fun. So, thank you so much for coming on the show. We'll talk to you soon. Great to catch up. Goodbye. Cheers.
turns.zh: 是的，有道理。非常感谢你抽出时间来和我们聊天。恭喜……我很期待看到 Codex 广告。我也是。请试试它。这个应用和 53 我觉得是我们一段时间以来做的最酷的事情。是的。用一个提示，我把 tbpn.com 的主页重建得看起来像伯克希尔·哈撒韦，而且立竿见影。非常有趣。有趣的选择。纯文本。非常简单。一次性搞定。没有真正挑战它的极限，但我玩得很开心。非常感谢你上节目。我们很快再聊。很高兴叙旧。再见。干杯。

turns.en: Let me tell you about 11 Labs. Build intelligent real-time conversational agents. Reimagine human technology interaction with 11 Labs. And I'm also going to tell you about Sentry. Sentry shows developers what's broken and helps them fix it fast. That's why 150,000 organizations use it to keep their apps working. And without further ado, we have Sholto Douglas from Anthropic. He's a member of technical staff and he's here in the TV. Sholto, how are you doing?
turns.zh: 让我介绍一下 11 Labs。构建智能实时对话代理。用 11 Labs 重新想象人机交互。我还要介绍一下 Sentry。Sentry 向开发者展示出了什么问题，并帮助他们快速修复。这就是为什么 15 万个组织用它来保持应用运行。闲话少说，我们有来自 Anthropic 的 Sholto Douglas。他是技术团队成员，现在在电视上。Sholto，你好吗？

turns.en: Hey guys, how you doing?
turns.zh: 嘿，大家好，你们好吗？

turns.en: I am doing great. Seems like you're doing better. Tell us the news. What did you release? What happened?
turns.zh: 我很好。看起来你更好。告诉我们新闻。你们发布了什么？发生了什么？

turns.en: All right. Well, once again, we've got a new model. It's really fantastic.
turns.zh: 好的。嗯，又一次，我们有了一个新模型。真的很棒。

turns.en: Uh the camera's a little bit laggy, a bit strange, but um you know, should be good. This model is really, really, really fantastic. So, I think people have been comparing the previous generation of OpenAI and Anthropic models, and they've noticed there's some differences, right? The OpenAI models were a bit better at trying really, really, really hard on tough problems, but the Anthropic models were much faster and so forth. And so, they worked on speed while we worked on making the models much much better at really, really tough problems. And so that's where the model really shines: it's able to think for a really really long time. It's able to expend a lot of test time compute on thinking hard about problems. It's also a massive step towards the coworker that we've been working on. I think I mentioned last time that we're seeing this continuous progression towards models that are as capable at the rest of knowledge work as they are at coding. And I think I'm really really excited about that as well. You can see it's a lot better at computer use. We've got Claude for PowerPoint, claiming to become actually quite capable at Excel. It's not perfect there yet, but huge steps, huge progress.
turns.zh: 呃，摄像头有点卡，有点奇怪，但应该没问题。这个模型真的非常非常棒。我觉得人们一直在比较上一代 OpenAI 和 Anthropic 的模型，他们注意到了一些差异，对吧？OpenAI 的模型在解决难题时更努力一些，而 Anthropic 的模型则快得多，等等。所以，他们专注于速度，而我们则致力于让模型在真正困难的问题上表现更好。这就是这个模型的闪光点：它能够思考很长时间，能够消耗大量的测试时算力来深入思考问题。这也是我们一直在开发的‘同事’（coworker）方向的一大步。我记得上次提到过，我们看到模型在知识工作的其他方面正逐渐变得和编程一样强大。我对此感到非常兴奋。你可以看到它在计算机使用方面好多了。我们有 Claude for PowerPoint，据说在 Excel 上也变得相当不错了。虽然还不完美，但已经是巨大的进步了。

turns.en: Do you have internal benchmarks for things like PowerPoint now?
turns.zh: 你们现在有像 PowerPoint 这样的内部基准测试吗？

turns.en: We have benchmarks for everything that we work on.
turns.zh: 我们做的每件事都有基准测试。

turns.en: But what makes it to the model card?
turns.zh: 但哪些会进入模型卡（model card）呢？

turns.en: What makes it to the model card? Oh, the model card is typically only publicly released benchmarks.
turns.zh: 哪些会进入模型卡？哦，模型卡通常只包含公开发布的基准测试。

turns.en: Because for our internal benchmarks, we usually hold them out for internal testing.
turns.zh: 因为对于内部基准测试，我们通常保留用于内部测试。

turns.en: Oh, sure, sure, sure.
turns.zh: 哦，当然，当然。

turns.en: What's the process for benchmarking a PowerPoint? Because it's not...
turns.zh: 给 PowerPoint 做基准测试的流程是怎样的？因为它不是……

turns.en: I can't talk too much about benchmarking a PowerPoint, but um you know, I'm not sure if you know back a couple of years ago I did work at McKinsey, so you know sometimes maybe they just need...
turns.zh: 我不能过多谈论 PowerPoint 的基准测试，但呃，你知道，我不确定你是否知道几年前我在麦肯锡工作过，所以有时候也许他们只需要……

turns.en: So they show them to you and you say it's good or...
turns.zh: 所以他们给你看，然后你说好或不好？

turns.en: Exactly, good or bad.
turns.zh: 没错，好或不好。

turns.en: Yeah, probably a lot of people involved. Yeah, talk to us about what a difference between 4.5 and 4.6 means. People might be familiar with pre-training, the different buzzwords that go into changing a model. What technically happened at Anthropic over the last few months to enable this?
turns.zh: 是的，可能有很多人参与。跟我们聊聊 4.5 和 4.6 之间的区别意味着什么。人们可能熟悉预训练，以及改变模型的各种流行词。在过去的几个月里，Anthropic 在技术上做了什么来实现这一点？

turns.en: Yes. So, we actually decorrelate the model release versioning from any specific technical details. It's more about the overall capability level that we're excited about. And actually, some like I've been asked by many people why we didn't just name 4.5 as 5 because it was such a step up. And I think maybe we under anticipated. I mean when I was last on here I don't think even I fully appreciated how much of a jump it was from 4 to 4.5. It was great because by the time there was the 4.5 hype cycle we could repost a bunch of clips of you talking about it and people were like wow they covered it today. It's like no we actually had them on the day it launched. And so I'm sure we'll be doing the same thing with 4.6.
turns.zh: 是的。实际上，我们将模型发布的版本号与任何具体的技术细节解耦。它更多是关于我们兴奋的整体能力水平。实际上，很多人问我为什么我们不直接把 4.5 命名为 5，因为它是一个巨大的进步。我想也许我们低估了。我的意思是，上次我在这里的时候，我甚至没有完全意识到从 4 到 4.5 的飞跃有多大。这很好，因为当 4.5 的炒作周期到来时，我们可以重新发布一些你谈论它的片段，人们会惊讶地说‘哇，他们今天报道了’。其实不是，我们是在它发布当天就请了你们。所以我确信我们会对 4.6 做同样的事情。

turns.en: Talk about Task Horizon, the meter survey. Is that important? I obviously it's a public benchmark. We've talked about it before, but is that something that is a different tradeoff to the other? Because I see it as like there's the IQ and then there's how long something can stay focused like the locking in. And is there any tension between those? Do you ever get a model that's like, oh, it's really smart, but it can't stay coherent for that long or vice versa, it does dumber but longer tasks.
turns.zh: 谈谈 Task Horizon，那个米制评估（meter eval）。它重要吗？显然它是一个公开的基准测试。我们之前讨论过，但它和其他基准测试有不同的权衡吗？因为我觉得它就像 IQ 和能保持专注多长时间（比如锁定）之间的区别。这两者之间有矛盾吗？你们有没有遇到过这样的模型：它非常聪明，但无法长时间保持连贯性，或者反过来，它做更笨但更长的任务。

turns.en: I mean, I think the meter eval is possibly the best eval currently out there, right? But you're right in saying that different parts of the chart measure different things. Initially, it was just testing IQ, but then as time goes on, it starts to test things where humans fail at tasks not because they aren't smart enough, but because actually persevering on a task for six, seven, eight hours is really hard. And you're right that these come apart. There's a sort of like models you should almost expect to have superhuman perseverance. But at the same time, maybe context coherence or something like this fights against that. And that's a different axis from raw intelligence or knowledge on given domains.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我认为米制评估可能是目前最好的评估，对吧？但你说得对，图表的不同部分衡量不同的事情。最初，它只是测试 IQ，但随着时间的推移，它开始测试人类失败的任务，不是因为不够聪明，而是因为坚持一项任务六、七、八个小时真的很难。你说得对，这两者是分开的。你几乎可以预期模型会有超人的毅力。但与此同时，也许上下文连贯性之类的东西会与之对抗。这与原始智力或特定领域的知识是不同的维度。

turns.en: Meter eval when I'm referencing AI progress to people. Recently we held this physics conference where we invited a whole bunch of physicists. We did it with Google DeepMind. We invited a whole bunch of physicists to try and convince them that AI was really a big deal. And that was the chart that resonated most deeply with people and I think is just quite conceptually graspable as 'oh okay, things that would take me x hours the models are now capable of'.
turns.zh: 当我向人们提及 AI 进展时，我会用米制评估。最近我们举办了一个物理学会议，邀请了很多物理学家。我们和 Google DeepMind 一起做的。我们邀请了很多物理学家，试图说服他们 AI 真的很重要。而那张图表最能引起人们的共鸣，我认为它在概念上很容易理解，就像‘哦，好吧，那些需要我 x 小时的事情，模型现在也能做到了’。

turns.en: It's an interesting benchmark considering that so many types of knowledge workers will never actually work on a task for seven hours straight. It's like just 'oh I worked on it for 20 minutes and I jumped into a meeting and then I took a look at it again and then I had lunch and then I went for a walk and then I came back I worked on it then I got somebody called, I had to work on something else.' So it's very possible that a lot of people listening to this, maybe outside of some software engineers, have just never even in their career worked on something for six hours straight.
turns.zh: 这是一个有趣的基准测试，考虑到很多类型的知识工作者实际上从来不会连续七小时专注于一项任务。就像‘哦，我工作了 20 分钟，然后去开会，然后又看了一下，然后吃午饭，然后散步，然后回来继续工作，然后有人打电话，我又得做别的事情。’所以很可能很多听众，也许除了某些软件工程师，在他们的职业生涯中从未连续六小时专注于一件事。

turns.en: The flow state.
turns.zh: 心流状态。

turns.en: I mean, you chain things together and so forth. But yes, I agree. It's an interesting benchmark in that respect. And what does even a week-long task mean? Surely that's sort of inherently composable into a year-long work of progress. I mean I certainly don't think of myself as doing a year-long task when I'm at work. I'm doing these day or week-long time horizons usually which feed into a several month-long time horizon.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，你把事情串联起来等等。但没错，我同意。从这个角度来看，这是一个有趣的基准测试。那么，一个为期一周的任务又意味着什么呢？当然，它本质上可以组合成一年长的工作进展。我的意思是，我在工作时肯定不会认为自己是在做一个为期一年的任务。我通常做的是以天或周为单位的时间跨度，这些会汇入几个月的时间跨度。

turns.en: Talk about orchestration. It feels like there's a moment happening. I really like the gas town analogy and the world that he's built there. You see all over, I have four Claude Code instances running at the same time. It feels like there needs to be a new layer of abstraction. How do you think that gets solved? Is now the time to start learning those things? What metaphors will be valuable going forward?
turns.zh: 谈谈编排（orchestration）。感觉一个时刻正在到来。我非常喜欢那个‘煤气镇’（gas town）的类比和他构建的世界。你到处都能看到，我同时运行着四个 Claude Code 实例。感觉需要一个新的抽象层。你认为这如何解决？现在是开始学习这些东西的时候了吗？未来哪些比喻会有价值？

turns.en: Yes. So, right now the agents are still at this point where you need to manually multiplex across them. When I'm working, the stark thing about going from 4 to 4.5 and now 4.6 was that I went from maybe like 10% of my lines of code being written by me to 0% of my lines of code being written by me.
turns.zh: 是的。所以，目前智能体仍然处于需要你手动在它们之间进行多路复用的阶段。当我工作时，从 4 到 4.5 再到现在的 4.6，一个显著的变化是，我写的代码行数从大约 10% 降到了 0%。

turns.en: The thing is I need to actually sit there and constantly switch between these windows to make sure they're on track and give them guidance and feedback. I'm multiplexing maybe five at once when it's going really well. But you still need to be in there in the details. I think the long-term right way to think about this is constantly moving up levels of abstraction. Ultimately you only want to be talking to one agent that synthesizes feedback from models as they come back and say 'I got stuck' or 'I was unclear on this.' That model can act as what I was doing before, and I can take a step back and act at an even higher level of abstraction. It doesn't feel like we ultimately want to be playing Age of Empires or StarCraft with the models. We shouldn't be APM bound. Instead, the models should surface information as you need it, so you can constantly act on whatever the most important thing is and context switch appropriately.
turns.zh: 问题在于，我需要真的坐在那里，不断在这些窗口之间切换，确保它们都在正轨上，并给予指导和反馈。进展顺利时，我可能同时处理五个。但你仍然需要深入细节。我认为长期正确的思考方式是不断向上提升抽象层级。最终，你只想与一个智能体对话，它综合来自模型的反馈，比如‘我卡住了’或‘这个我不清楚’。那个模型可以扮演我之前扮演的角色，而我则可以退后一步，在更高的抽象层级上行动。我们最终应该不想像玩《帝国时代》或《星际争霸》那样操控模型吧？我们不应该受限于每分钟操作数。相反，模型应该在你需要时提供信息，这样你就能持续处理最重要的事情，并恰当地切换上下文。

transcript.sec: Game development and Age of Scaling

turns.en: Speaking of gaming, do you have an update for us? When is the game shipping? Tell us about the game. What inspired this? Give us the backstory and then tell us what's going on.
turns.zh: 说到游戏，你有什么更新吗？游戏什么时候发布？跟我们讲讲这个游戏。是什么启发了你？说说背景故事，然后告诉我们进展如何。

turns.en: Well, I think like many other people, I really wanted to test the limits of the models over the holidays. We saw all that from people saying 'I spent three, four days doing X.' I think that's really where the hype cycle for the most recent generation of models started in some respect, when people got a chance to properly test the limits.
turns.zh: 嗯，我想和很多人一样，我真的想在假期里测试模型的极限。我们看到很多人说‘我花了三四天做某事’。我认为在某种程度上，这正是最新一代模型炒作周期的起点，当人们有机会真正测试其极限时。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: It tells you a lot about adoption and how diffusion takes time because you need to have the space.
turns.zh: 这很能说明采用率的问题，以及扩散需要时间，因为你需要有空间。

turns.en: We need more long weekends.
turns.zh: 我们需要更多长周末。

turns.en: Seriously, that's the bottleneck. Long weekends are the bottleneck.
turns.zh: 说真的，那就是瓶颈。长周末就是瓶颈。

turns.en: GDP.
turns.zh: GDP。

turns.en: Yeah. Good for GDP, I guess. Holidays.
turns.zh: 是啊，对GDP有好处，我猜。假期。

turns.en: Play with coding models.
turns.zh: 玩编码模型。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Sorry. Anyway.
turns.zh: 对对对。抱歉。总之。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Anyway, so Dylan's been getting us all into Age of Empires with these five matches on the downstairs table. And I wanted to see if I could make an 'Age of Scaling' where you build solar panels and data centers, you train AI models and drones and so forth instead of farms and mining. You start in the industrial era, you go all the way up to Carter Chev. I only got like 70-80% done and all the mechanics work, but it turns out it's actually really hard to make a game that's fun. Actually just clicking 'make solar panel' 100 times is not fun. Surprise, surprise. And it's fun for some people. There are those crazy clicker games where you do that and it's just a brain rot game and they're pretty successful. But I think you should stay away from that. Not even Factorio. These are like mobile games that are really, really bad. Anyway, I don't think that aligns with your mission.
turns.zh: 总之，Dylan一直在楼下桌上带我们玩《帝国时代》的五人局。我想看看我能不能做一个‘Scaling时代’，你建造太阳能板和数据中心，训练AI模型和无人机，而不是农场和采矿。从工业时代开始，一直发展到卡特·切夫。我只完成了大概70-80%，所有机制都工作了，但结果发现做一款好玩的游戏真的很难。实际上，点击‘制造太阳能板’100次并不好玩。意外吧。但对有些人来说好玩。有些疯狂的点击游戏就是这样，纯粹是脑残游戏，还挺成功。但我觉得你应该远离那种。甚至不像《异星工厂》。这些是手机游戏，非常非常烂。总之，我觉得那不符合你的使命。

turns.en: There's an art to it.
turns.zh: 这里面有艺术。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: But we talk about the grand geopolitics and strategy of this era we're going into, where the most valuable resources on Earth are, well, basically the economy is in some respects reorienting around compute. And so I wanted to capture some of that dynamic. The late night discussions of San Francisco in a game.
turns.zh: 但我们谈论的是我们即将进入的这个时代的宏大地缘政治和战略，地球上最有价值的资源，嗯，基本上经济在某种程度上正在围绕算力重新定向。所以我想捕捉一些那种动态。旧金山的深夜讨论，浓缩在一个游戏里。

turns.en: Yeah, I love it. How are you processing the work that's being done in world modeling and generative worlds? I know that you're not generating images, but Claude can take in an image and process it, and that has value. You text a screenshot and have them implement it. But what do you think about world models? Is that going to play an important piece, a link in the chain of where you're going?
turns.zh: 是的，我喜欢。你如何看待世界模型和生成式世界方面的工作？我知道你不生成图像，但Claude可以接收图像并处理，这很有价值。你发一张截图，让它实现。但你怎么看世界模型？它会在你前进的道路上扮演重要一环吗？

turns.en: Yes. So, I think there are two different things here. There's the direct path to AGI, which I think is very much coding, and then AI research and general science and so forth. I don't think that requires world models. But I do think that world models are incredibly exciting for a number of other things. I think they're very exciting from a gaming perspective. We all saw those demos are incredible with the genie. It's just truly mind-blowing. And I also think they're probably the unlock to training robotics properly. It feels to me like there was this era of 'oh, we're just going to need to do incredible amounts of behavioral cloning of people operating robots.' And it feels like the scaling laws work, but I don't think it's on the critical path to AGI basically.
turns.zh: 是的。所以，我认为这里有两件不同的事情。一条是通往AGI的直接路径，我认为那主要是编码，然后是AI研究和一般科学等等。我不认为那需要世界模型。但我确实认为世界模型在其他许多事情上非常令人兴奋。从游戏角度来看，它们非常令人兴奋。我们都看到了那些演示，精灵模型令人难以置信，真是令人震惊。而且我认为它们也可能是正确训练机器人的关键。我感觉曾经有一个时代，人们认为‘哦，我们只需要做大量的人类操作机器人的行为克隆。’感觉缩放定律是有效的，但我认为它基本上不是通往AGI的关键路径。

transcript.secZh: 游戏开发与规模化时代

turns.en: Can you unpack the concept of a software-only singularity?
turns.zh: 你能解释一下‘纯软件奇点’这个概念吗？

turns.en: Yes. In this world, it's one where the models are far better at digital tasks than they are at physical ones. And so we see rapid change in the digital world with relatively little change in the physical world. Information and software changes dramatically. This ends up having some pretty weird effects. It means that maybe the drivers of what have been the last couple decades of progress in the economy turn around, get changed very rapidly. I think we'll see that flow on into the physical world, but at a delay. So you get much better at doing chip design. You get much better at training AI models. AI models get a lot faster. Chips get a lot better. The general economy gets a lot more efficient because the information and message passing that is much of the rest of the economy ends up becoming much more efficient. But at the same time, you don't yet have robots providing limitless physical abundance. Science probably progresses really fast up to the degree that you need interaction with labs or larger particle colliders or something like this, and then you go 'okay, well I need to build the robots.' But at the same time, automated labs feel more near-term than unlimited robots in the real world manipulating the earth. I think maybe they actually arrive at similar times. I think you need pretty competent robots for the labs, or at least no one's yet managed to figure out how to automate a lab without turns out there are all these really weird little tasks that require a lot of manual human dexterity that are currently part of biological protocols. A lot of biologists will say 'no, you just actually need something that's capable of human level dexterity.' Maybe not for all experiments, but for enough that it becomes annoying.
turns.zh: 是的。在这个世界里，模型在数字任务上远比在物理任务上出色。因此，我们看到数字世界发生快速变化，而物理世界变化相对较小。信息和软件发生了巨大变化。这最终会产生一些非常奇怪的影响。这意味着过去几十年经济进步的驱动力可能会逆转，发生非常迅速的变化。我认为这会流入物理世界，但会有延迟。所以你在芯片设计上变得更擅长。你在训练AI模型上变得更擅长。AI模型变得更快。芯片变得更好。整体经济变得更高效，因为构成经济其余大部分的信息和消息传递最终变得高效得多。但与此同时，你还没有机器人提供无限的物理富足。科学可能进步得非常快，直到你需要与实验室或更大的对撞机等交互，然后你会说‘好吧，我需要建造机器人。’但与此同时，自动化实验室感觉比在现实世界中操纵地球的无限机器人更近。我认为它们可能实际上在相似的时间到来。我认为实验室需要相当能干的机器人，或者至少还没有人成功弄清楚如何自动化实验室，因为结果发现所有那些奇怪的小任务都需要大量的人类手动灵巧性，这些目前是生物协议的一部分。许多生物学家会说‘不，你实际上需要具备人类水平灵巧性的东西。’也许不是所有实验，但足够多到令人烦恼。

turns.en: And in this software-only singularity, how are you defining singularity? You know, the Kurzweil formulation of more computing power than human brains—that's one equation. There's also just this point beyond which we cannot see. How do you think about singularity in that context?
turns.zh: 在这个纯软件的奇点中，你如何定义奇点？你知道，库兹韦尔提出的计算能力超过人脑的公式——那是一个方程。还有就是这个无法预见的临界点。你在这个背景下如何看待奇点？

turns.en: I think there is a pretty tricky event horizon that I, at least, haven't found anyone that's made incredibly strong or good predictions. It just feels like at that point you have as many digital intelligences or more, and they're as smart or smarter than human intelligences. What does that even mean for the world? It's incredibly hard to predict. It's something we spend a lot of time trying to think about and trying to prepare the world for all the eventualities. But it's difficult to make sort of topline predictions of what exactly that looks like.
turns.zh: 我认为存在一个相当棘手的事件视界，至少我还没发现有人做出过极其有力或准确的预测。感觉在那一刻，你拥有与人类智能数量相当或更多的数字智能，它们与人类智能一样聪明或更聪明。这对世界意味着什么？这非常难以预测。我们花了很多时间思考这个问题，并试图让世界为各种可能性做好准备。但要做出关于具体样子的顶层预测是很困难的。

turns.en: Yeah. In the original Kurzweil formulation, that was almost the definition of the singularity—that you can't make predictions beyond it. So once the predictions break down, then you're there.
turns.zh: 是的。在库兹韦尔最初的表述中，这几乎是奇点的定义——你无法预测超越它的东西。所以一旦预测失效，你就到达了奇点。

turns.en: I mean, I think in the near term, you know, I went down with Dario to his Elon interview in Austin, and I think a lot of the reflection of that in the physical world perhaps is what ends up happening. You do end up basically trying to climb the Kardashev scale and capturing more of the energy of the sun and so forth. But I think that takes some time.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我认为在短期内，我和达里奥一起去奥斯汀参加了埃隆的采访，我认为这在物理世界中的很多反映可能就是最终会发生的事情。你最终基本上是在尝试攀登卡尔达肖夫指数，捕获更多的太阳能量等等。但我认为这需要一些时间。

turns.en: Is your current takeaway we're more chip constrained, energy constrained? What's the biggest bottleneck to AI progress?
turns.zh: 你目前的结论是，我们更受芯片限制还是能源限制？AI 进步的最大瓶颈是什么？

turns.en: I mean, I think right now I agree with Sam's earlier answer that we're more chip constrained in AI progress. But I think it's sort of interesting to roll forward two years and be like, okay, well, if you want 100 gigawatts or, you know, in four years and you want a terawatt, what does that look like? Where do you put that? And this is why Elon's going after the data centers in space, right? He thinks it's going to be the easiest way to get a terawatt in space in 2030. And maybe it's space, maybe it's the, you know, a giant desert somewhere. It's the Atacama desert, the Australian desert, somewhere in Texas, you know, maybe Texas has a lot of solar panels.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我认为目前我同意 Sam 之前的回答，我们在 AI 进步上更受芯片限制。但我觉得有趣的是，向前推两年，你会想，好吧，如果你想要 100 吉瓦，或者四年后你想要 1 太瓦，那会是什么样子？你把它放在哪里？这就是为什么埃隆在追求太空数据中心，对吧？他认为这将是 2030 年在太空中获得 1 太瓦的最简单方式。也许是太空，也许是你知道的某个巨大沙漠——阿塔卡马沙漠、澳大利亚沙漠，或者德克萨斯州的某个地方，也许德克萨斯州有很多太阳能板。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Hard to know.
turns.zh: 很难知道。

transcript.sec: Super Bowl Ad and Compute Constraints

turns.en: Yeah. Jordy, how are you guys thinking about free usage limits ahead of the Super Bowl? It's really tough. I mean, one of the struggles of this is that compute is so constrained.
turns.zh: 是的。Jordy，你们在超级碗之前是如何考虑免费使用限制的？这真的很棘手。我的意思是，其中一个困难是算力非常受限。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Yeah, I noticed you guys didn't say 'download the Claude app.' There was no direct call to action.
turns.zh: 是的，我注意到你们没有说“下载 Claude 应用”。没有直接的行动号召。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, I think the purpose of the ad was very much one for provoking thought and discussion.
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，我认为这个广告的目的很大程度上是为了引发思考和讨论。

turns.en: And it was certainly successful already.
turns.zh: 而且它显然已经成功了。

turns.en: Yeah. Provoke a lot of thought and discussion.
turns.zh: 是的。引发了大量的思考和讨论。

turns.en: And I think you don't need to do an explicit call to action for people to download things or to consider things.
turns.zh: 而且我认为你不需要明确的行动号召让人们去下载或考虑事情。

turns.en: Yeah, that makes sense.
turns.zh: 是的，有道理。

turns.en: But again, it sounds like the GPUs will be on fire Sunday is the expectation.
turns.zh: 但再次，听起来周日 GPU 会火力全开，这是预期。

turns.en: The GPUs will be on fire. As has been the case for the entire industry for the last year. I mean, I think we've all had to make incredibly difficult trade-offs on exactly how compute is used.
turns.zh: GPU 会火力全开。就像过去一年整个行业的情况一样。我的意思是，我认为我们都必须在算力使用方式上做出极其艰难的权衡。

transcript.secZh: 超级碗广告与计算限制

turns.en: Can you zoom in on your experience? Because I feel like everyone's sort of seen rough growth curves for Anthropic on the revenue side or on the tokens generated side or whatever, and it feels exponential but smoothish. And then simultaneously you have these constant worrying about bottlenecks: is there enough capital, are there enough chips, are there enough data centers, is there enough energy, are we in an age of research, are we in a plateau? How have you balanced those two narratives—your experience of smooth growth even though it's exponential with constant fear around something like a cloud hanging over the industry and maybe a slowdown?
turns.zh: 你能详细谈谈你的经验吗？因为我觉得每个人都看到了 Anthropic 在收入或 token 生成等方面的粗略增长曲线，感觉是指数级的但相对平滑。同时，你又不断担心瓶颈：资本够不够，芯片够不够，数据中心够不够，能源够不够，我们是在研究时代还是平台期？你是如何平衡这两种叙事的——你经历的平滑增长（尽管是指数级的）与持续的恐惧（像乌云笼罩行业，可能放缓）？

turns.en: I mean, I think to note on the smooth or exponential growth, one of the things that really blew me away was when SemiAnalysis did that analysis and found that we went from like 2% a month ago or 6 weeks ago to 4% of GitHub commits done by Claude Code. And for the amount of GitHub commits done by Claude Code to double over a few weeks is truly ludicrous. And there's no real visceral way to feel that. You almost feel like it's a number on a screen, but you can't viscerally feel it.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，关于平滑或指数级增长，让我印象深刻的一件事是 SemiAnalysis 的分析发现，我们从一个月前或六周前的约 2% 上升到 4% 的 GitHub 提交是由 Claude Code 完成的。而 Claude Code 完成的 GitHub 提交量在几周内翻倍，这真是荒谬。而且没有真正直观的方式去感受它。你几乎觉得它只是屏幕上的一个数字，但你无法切身感受到。

turns.en: Totally.
turns.zh: 完全同意。

turns.en: For us, we've always had a very, very, very strong conviction. And in many respects, Anthropic is a bet on this being true that scaling is continuing. And that progress continues unabated. So in many respects, the external numbers are only a reflection of the conviction that we've had internally for a long time about how we exactly expect all the trends to go. I mean, I think we're broadly on trend for, like, if you look at situational awareness, I think pretty sure the power and energy and flops predictions are bang on. It kind of feels more like we're just hitting each milestone as we expect.
turns.zh: 对我们来说，我们一直有非常非常强烈的信念。在很多方面，Anthropic 就是押注于 Scaling 持续这一事实。而且进步不会减弱。所以在很多方面，外部数字只是我们长期以来内部信念的反映，即我们确切地预期所有趋势会如何发展。我的意思是，我认为我们大致符合趋势，比如如果你看态势感知，我相当确定关于功率、能量和浮点运算次数的预测是准确的。感觉更像是我们只是按预期达到了每个里程碑。

turns.en: Mhm. And roughly what we expected to have happened has happened so far.
turns.zh: 嗯。到目前为止，大致上我们预期发生的事情已经发生了。

turns.en: In terms of diffusion of the technology, do you think that there's a role for forward deployed engineers to go in and change organizations? We saw some news that OpenAI is hiring a bunch of folks. Obviously a lot of enterprises are using Claude now. But at the same time, that phenomenon of if you don't have a free weekend or a long weekend, you might never get around to implementing it. So having a little bit of extra horsepower and knowledge around the office might actually pull forward capability. Is that something you could see growing at Anthropic?
turns.zh: 在技术扩散方面，你认为前部署工程师是否有角色去改变组织？我们看到一些新闻说 OpenAI 正在招聘很多人。显然现在很多企业都在使用 Claude。但与此同时，如果你没有空闲的周末或长周末，你可能永远没时间实施它。所以在办公室拥有一些额外的能力和知识实际上可能会提前提升能力。你看到 Anthropic 在这方面会增长吗？

turns.en: Yeah, I think it's a great idea. I think it's very clear that people don't know how to hold these things. And also the fact that it's ludicrous: 3 months ago, we didn't have this most recent generation of models. We didn't have 4.5 and like you're meant to adjust your business strategy over the course of the holidays basically because the models are suddenly capable of doing things that they couldn't do right before you enter the end of the year.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为这是个好主意。我认为很明显人们不知道如何把握这些东西。而且事实是这很荒谬：三个月前，我们还没有最新一代的模型。我们没有 4.5，你基本上需要在假期期间调整你的商业策略，因为模型突然能够做它们在年底前无法做到的事情。

transcript.secZh: 前向部署工程师的角色

turns.en: Are you saying we could see a new white collar job creation?
turns.zh: 你是说我们可能会看到新的白领工作被创造出来？

turns.en: Maybe. I mean, I think these are really valuable jobs. There's clearly a hell of a lot of value to be unlocked with them.
turns.zh: 也许吧。我觉得这些工作真的很有价值。显然有很多价值可以通过它们释放出来。

turns.en: We asked Sam this, but I'm interested in your take. Is data the new oil? Or is it the fossil fuel as I said? The more pointed question is: are there organizations out there that have data that's locked away, and it requires a business development deal or an acquisition or some sort of AI leadership to add that capability? Because there are effectively secrets, sometimes trade secrets. I don't know if the Coca-Cola formula is in 4.6 six, but it will be if the Coca-Cola company calls you, right? So how do you think about that question and that phrase, even though it's been beaten to death, maybe it's making a comeback?
turns.zh: 我们问过山姆这个问题，但我对你的看法很感兴趣。数据是新的石油吗？还是像我说的是化石燃料？更尖锐的问题是：有没有一些组织拥有被锁起来的数据，需要商业开发协议、收购或某种AI领导力来解锁这种能力？因为实际上存在秘密，有时是商业机密。我不知道可口可乐的配方是否在4.6六里，但如果可口可乐公司打电话给你，它就会在，对吧？那么你怎么看待这个问题和这个说法，即使它已经被说烂了，也许它正在卷土重来？

turns.en: I think there are two kinds of data, and one of them is dramatically more useful than the other. There's the kind of data which is the analytics a company might have collected in the past, or artifacts of the company's operation, internal documents and so forth. And there's the other kind, which is the actual work that people did to produce those documents, and that's not recorded. So I think to the degree that I'm not certain it's data is the new oil so much as the expertise of people. Models being able to understand and learn from people. You almost want the models to be like an intern in an organization, get coached and feedback, and learn how to do the job. What I'm analogizing to is: as a human, when you join a company, you mostly learn the job from your colleagues rather than from reading all the documents in the company. It's just much more informative, and I broadly expect that to be true of models as well. I expect them to learn in a quite humanlike way from their colleagues.
turns.zh: 我认为有两种数据，其中一种比另一种有用得多。一种是公司过去收集的分析数据，或者公司运营的产物，比如内部文件等等。另一种是人们为制作这些文件所做的实际工作，而这并没有被记录下来。所以我认为，与其说数据是新石油，不如说人的专业知识才是关键。模型能够理解并向人学习。你几乎希望模型像组织里的实习生，接受指导和反馈，学习如何完成工作。我类比的是：作为人类，当你加入一家公司时，你主要是从同事那里学习工作，而不是阅读公司所有的文件。这信息量大得多，我大致认为模型也是如此。我期望它们以相当类人的方式从同事那里学习。

turns.en: Rather than from reading all the documents in the company.
turns.zh: 而不是阅读公司所有的文件。

turns.en: It's just much more informative, and I broadly expect that to be true of models as well. I expect them to learn in a quite humanlike way from their colleagues.
turns.zh: 这信息量大得多，我大致认为模型也是如此。我期望它们以相当类人的方式从同事那里学习。

turns.en: Have you thought about a scenario where a company's revenue goes to zero and all the remaining value is just in their historical data?
turns.zh: 你有没有想过这样一种情况：一家公司的收入归零，所有剩余价值都在他们的历史数据中？

turns.en: Well, I guess in this analogy, it would be the people. I think the values would regress with them.
turns.zh: 嗯，我想在这个类比中，应该是人。我认为价值会随着他们而衰减。

turns.en: Sure. Yeah, that makes sense.
turns.zh: 当然。是的，有道理。

turns.en: When you guys are thinking about new opportunities, verticals, categories, what is the thought process around UI? There are a lot of people building AI native tools today. They functionally look like traditional enterprise software. And it's hard for me to imagine Anthropic building out infinite surface area of traditional software to do things like orchestration. But what is the specific framework when thinking about interfaces for new products?
turns.zh: 当你们考虑新机会、垂直领域、类别时，关于用户界面的思考过程是什么？现在有很多人在构建AI原生工具。它们在功能上看起来像传统的企业软件。我很难想象Anthropic会构建无限的传统软件表面积来做编排之类的事情。但在考虑新产品的界面时，具体的框架是什么？

turns.en: We mostly want to build something that fits in where the humans fit in, and can help interact with you like a colleague. So we want something that can fit in and join in and use the same interfaces that you do, rather than absorbing interfaces. I think that's what companies want.
