If you ask any company in the world, 'Hey, we can get you the best executive in this function in the entire world,' they will usually say we will pay almost any price for that, or an extreme premium. And yet if you sell them a software solution, 'Hey, this software solution can help you do this other thing,' maybe they want to use it, but it's not as enticing as something that can just do the work.
turns.zh: 我们主要想构建一些能融入人类所在之处的东西，能像同事一样与你互动。所以我们想要能融入、加入并使用与你相同的界面的东西，而不是吸收界面。我认为这就是公司想要的。如果你问世界上任何一家公司，‘嘿，我们能给你找来这个职能领域全世界最好的高管，’他们通常会说我们愿意为此付出几乎任何代价，或者一个极高的溢价。然而，如果你卖给他们一个软件解决方案，‘嘿，这个软件解决方案能帮你做另一件事，’也许他们想用，但它不如一个能直接完成工作的东西那么有吸引力。

turns.en: Right? Exactly.
turns.zh: 对吧？完全正确。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Also, the UI evolution seems very underrated. I was processing the OpenAI development and how important mobile was to that. Just this idea: somebody shows up and says, 'You're a busy business executive. You're in meetings and phone calls all day. You're on flights. We got a great software engineer for you, but you got to talk to them over the terminal on your desktop.' It's like, I'm not going to give that many commands because I'm on the road and on my phone. And you see people walking off planes with MacBooks now because they need to get one more prompt in.
turns.zh: 另外，用户界面的演变似乎被严重低估了。我在思考OpenAI的发展，以及移动端对它的重要性。就是这种想法：有人出现说，‘你是个忙碌的高管。你整天开会打电话。你在飞机上。我们给你找了个很棒的软件工程师，但你得通过桌面终端和他们交流。’这就像，我不会下那么多命令，因为我在路上，用手机。现在你会看到人们下飞机时带着MacBook，因为他们需要再输入一个提示。

turns.en: I have done that. I am so guilty of that.
turns.zh: 我做过这种事。我对此深感内疚。

turns.en: And so I imagine there's a lot of work that will be done on the mobile side. How do you think about that?
turns.zh: 所以我想在移动端会有很多工作要做。你怎么看？

turns.en: That's broadly true of all AI interfaces, right? I don't want to look at a screen. I don't want a terminal window or a chat window. I just want to ask my computer to do something, or maybe ask the world around me for something to happen and for that to happen. So I think focusing too much on the interfaces of today versus how would you interact with an incredibly competent colleague is the right thing. I just want to text Claude and be like, 'Hey, can you fix this up for me? Can you help me sort this out or book this trip?' I just want that.
turns.zh: 这大致适用于所有AI界面，对吧？我不想看屏幕。我不想要终端窗口或聊天窗口。我只想让我的电脑做点什么，或者也许让我周围的世界发生点什么。所以我认为过于关注今天的界面，而不是你会如何与一个极其能干的同事互动，才是正确的。我只想给Claude发短信说，‘嘿，你能帮我搞定这个吗？你能帮我解决这个或预订这次旅行吗？’我只想要那样。

turns.en: Yeah. Everyone has that ability to text from a variety of devices. People have AirPods, Apple watches, phones, laptops, tablets. Do you think we need any more new hardware?
turns.zh: 是的。每个人都有能力从各种设备发短信。人们有AirPods、苹果手表、手机、笔记本电脑、平板电脑。你认为我们还需要更多的新硬件吗？

turns.en: I think new hardware is pretty exciting and interesting. I like that people are placing bets on it. I feel like something which can capture a bit more of the context that you have in your day-to-day life and that you can talk to. One thing is that voice is higher bit rate than typing for most people. But at the same time, in most of our environments, it's actually quite annoying to talk to your devices in crowded public spaces or at work. So maybe something that captures that automatically would be great. You've seen those YouTube videos where people subvocalize? That would be cool. Apple just bought a company that looks at skin movements and maybe can relist. There are a number of companies in the space that seem like it's coming, and you'll just be able to plug into that.
turns.zh: 我认为新硬件非常令人兴奋和有趣。我喜欢人们在这方面下注。我觉得有些东西可以捕捉你日常生活中的更多上下文，并且你可以与之交谈。有一点是，对大多数人来说，语音的比特率比打字高。但同时，在大多数环境中，在拥挤的公共场所或工作中与设备交谈实际上相当烦人。所以也许能自动捕捉的东西会很棒。你看过那些人们默读的YouTube视频吗？那会很酷。苹果刚收购了一家观察皮肤运动的公司，也许能重新列出。这个领域有很多公司，看起来它就要来了，你就能接入它。

turns.en: But also my natural environment absorbing more of my context and being able to just talk to you like having speakers around my house rather than having to carry my phone around me. That would be cool. Do you see any world where on-device computation becomes more important? I feel like Anthropic as a whole is so backend heavy, like none of the computation is done on the device. But that could change in theory. I don't know what you think about.
turns.zh: 而且我的自然环境能吸收更多我的上下文，我就能直接跟你说话，比如家里到处都有音箱，而不是必须随身带着手机。那会很酷。你觉得有没有可能设备端计算会变得更重要？我感觉 Anthropic 整体上太偏后端了，所有计算都不在设备上。但理论上这可以改变。我不知道你怎么看。

turns.en: I think our general perspective is that a given level of intelligence gets 10 to 50x cheaper every year. That massively democratizes access to that level of intelligence. It's literally in my Twitter bio: 'intelligence too cheap to meter.' This is in large part one of the things I worked on at Google. One of the ways that happens is that level of intelligence goes on device. But because scaling keeps continuing, there is an exponentially greater set of use cases which the models get applied to. So it's totally plausible that some sub-swarm might exist on your computer, or you might get a laptop with better memory bandwidth that can have little models complete stuff. But at the same time, you also want the much greater intelligence out there planning and farming things out to swarms and really munching on the intensive and intellectually difficult work.
turns.zh: 我认为我们总的观点是，给定水平的智能每年会便宜 10 到 50 倍。这极大地普及了该水平智能的获取。这就在我的 Twitter 简介里：‘智能便宜到无需计量’。这很大程度上是我在 Google 做的工作之一。实现方式之一就是该水平的智能走向设备端。但由于 Scaling 持续进行，模型被应用的用例集呈指数级增长。所以完全有可能你的电脑上存在某个子群，或者你有一台内存带宽更好的笔记本电脑，可以运行小模型完成任务。但同时，你也希望更强大的智能在外面规划、把任务分发给群组，真正处理那些密集且智力上困难的工作。

turns.en: Yeah. Jordy, anything else? Have the market sell-offs due to various Anthropic releases been generally predictable or have there been any that have been surprising?
turns.zh: 嗯。Jordy，还有别的吗？由于 Anthropic 的各种发布导致的市场抛售，通常是可预测的，还是有过令人意外的？

turns.en: I honestly didn't even notice the last one because I was just heads down on the launch. But look, in this case, I think it's a little bit much to ascribe it to an Anthropic launch. There have been legal tools released with AI for many customers that work for lawyers. I don't think this is crazily different to any of those. It's part of a continuing trend that we've seen.
turns.zh: 老实说，我甚至没注意到上一次，因为我当时埋头于发布。但你看，这次我认为把它归因于 Anthropic 的发布有点过头了。有很多面向律师的 AI 法律工具已经发布给许多客户。我不觉得这次跟那些有什么天壤之别。这只是我们看到的持续趋势的一部分。

turns.en: Yep. Makes sense. Well, thank you so much for taking the time to come talk to us on launch day. Great to get the update. Can't wait to talk to you next time. We'll see you. Have a good rest of your day.
turns.zh: 是的。有道理。非常感谢你在发布日抽空来和我们聊天。很高兴听到最新消息。期待下次再聊。再见。祝你今天过得愉快。

transcript.secZh: 设备端计算与智能民主化

transcript.sec: Introduction of Dan Barkella and T1 Energy

turns.en: Let me tell you about Graphite Code review for the Age of AI. Graphite helps teams on GitHub ship higher quality software faster. And I'm also going to tell you about Vanta, automate compliance and security. Vanta is the leading AI trust management platform. And I'm very excited for our next guest. We have Dan Barkella from T1 Energy. He's the chairman and CEO. He's in the green room waiting room. Now he's in the TV. What's going on? How are you doing Dan?
turns.zh: 让我介绍一下面向 AI 时代的 Graphite Code Review。Graphite 帮助 GitHub 上的团队更快地交付更高质量的软件。我还要介绍一下 Vanta，自动化合规与安全。Vanta 是领先的 AI 信任管理平台。我很高兴迎来下一位嘉宾。我们有来自 T1 Energy 的 Dan Barkella。他是董事长兼 CEO。他在休息室等候。现在他出现在屏幕上了。怎么样？你好吗 Dan？

turns.en: Hey, I'm doing great. Thanks for having me.
turns.zh: 嘿，我很好。谢谢邀请。

turns.en: Thanks so much. First time on the show. Would you mind introducing yourself and the company and then I'd love to know about the journey to the company and then I have a bunch of questions about energy but we can start with an introduction if that's okay.
turns.zh: 非常感谢。第一次上节目。你介意介绍一下自己和公司吗？然后我想了解一下你加入公司的经历，之后我有一堆关于能源的问题，但我们可以先从介绍开始，如果没问题的话。

turns.en: Sure. Great. Dan Barcelo, I've been doing energy all my life. A native New Yorker, now a proud resident in Texas. We're building a lot of different energy systems. My journey took me through different parts of the world building oil and gas systems, and now we're really focused on building solar in America. It's an exciting time to be doing it when everyone knows we need a lot of electricity and a lot of energy.
turns.zh: 当然。很好。我是 Dan Barcelo，一辈子都在做能源。土生土长的纽约人，现在是自豪的德州居民。我们在建设很多不同的能源系统。我的职业生涯带我走遍世界各地建设石油和天然气系统，现在我们真正专注于在美国建设太阳能。现在做这件事正是激动人心的时刻，因为每个人都知道我们需要大量电力和能源。

turns.en: Yeah. How did you wind up with T1?
turns.zh: 嗯。你是怎么来到 T1 的？

turns.en: T1 was a company we saw the need for particularly utility scale grid level type of energy systems to be, I don't want to say disrupted, but needed to be impacted in a large way. Whether it's on grid level storage or on solar. We were doing quite a bit on the grid level battery side, and the battery side frankly had a wall of competition: you look left, it's Samsung; you look right, it's LG; you look this way, it's Panasonic; and you got CATL behind you; and then oh my god, here's Tesla. But when you look at solar, it was very different in the United States. It's predominantly by Chinese companies. The American technology of solar was something we kind of almost forgot how to do in the US and build it out. But we had an opportunity to buy assets, by the technology, by the know-how. Now we run it. We bought a 5 gigawatt asset in Texas late '24, and we're building a second part of the plant. So for us it was really about...
turns.zh: T1 是一家我们看到了需求的公司，特别是公用事业规模的电网级能源系统需要，我不想说颠覆，但需要大规模影响。无论是电网级储能还是太阳能。我们在电网级电池方面做了不少，而电池方面坦白说竞争壁垒很高：左边是三星，右边是 LG，这边是松下，后面是宁德时代，然后天哪，还有特斯拉。但当你看向太阳能时，在美国情况非常不同。它主要由中国公司主导。美国的太阳能技术我们几乎忘了如何在美国制造和建设。但我们有机会购买资产，购买技术和专有技术。现在我们运营它。我们在 24 年底在德州购买了一个 5 吉瓦的资产，并且正在建设工厂的第二部分。所以对我们来说，这真的是关于……

turns.en: You should have seen our initial reaction to T1. We saw first interaction with T1 was seeing the video of your facility and John and I were like high five. America's got it. We can build solar at scale. And then of course we figured out the history of the company, but we're excited that it's an American asset now.
turns.zh: 你应该看看我们最初对 T1 的反应。我们第一次接触 T1 是看到你们工厂的视频，我和 John 都击掌庆祝。美国做到了。我们可以大规模制造太阳能。然后当然我们了解了公司的历史，但我们很高兴现在它是美国资产。

turns.en: Yeah. And look, we have a million square foot facility in Dallas that we brought from producing zero. We commissioned that and brought it up to producing over 5 gigawatts in annualized runway in November. So that was a lot of work. A lot of robots in the factory but a lot of humans too.
turns.zh: 是的。你看，我们在达拉斯有一个百万平方英尺的工厂，我们从零产量开始。我们调试了它，并在 11 月将其年化产能提升到超过 5 吉瓦。所以那是很多工作。工厂里有很多机器人，但也有很多人类。

turns.en: Talk about the scale of the operation in the context of overall US solar panel production, because I think people hear 5 gigawatt and maybe they can wrap their head around that, but it's a meaningful percent of overall production, is that right?
turns.zh: 谈谈这个运营规模在美国整体太阳能电池板生产中的背景，因为我认为人们听到 5 吉瓦可能能理解，但这是总产量的一个有意义百分比，对吗？

turns.en: The US is about 50 gigawatts, so this alone is about 10% of American production. It's extremely automated, it has 1,200 people, it's running 24/7 except during those ice storms that people don't know how to deal with in Texas. It's more about the people driving to work than the factory. But it's a sign of significant scale. It's one of the most modern in the world.
turns.zh: 美国大约是 50 吉瓦，所以仅此一项就约占美国产量的 10%。它高度自动化，有 1200 名员工，全天候运行，除了那些德州人不知道如何应对的冰暴期间。更多是人员通勤的问题，而不是工厂本身。但这标志着显著的规模。它是世界上最现代化的工厂之一。

turns.en: How does that 50 gigawatts of capacity in the United States compare to China? Can you lay the sort of geopolitical backdrop and get me up to speed on how much the American government is doing to support this effort?
turns.zh: 美国这 50 吉瓦的产能与中国相比如何？你能介绍一下地缘政治背景，并让我了解美国政府为支持这一努力做了多少吗？

turns.en: China is up to over a thousand gigawatts, 1.2 terawatts of capacity. China's adding from last year probably close to 350 to 400 gigawatts. That's close to the American grid.
turns.zh: 中国已经超过一千吉瓦，1.2 太瓦的产能。中国去年新增的可能接近 350 到 400 吉瓦。这接近美国整个电网的规模。

transcript.sec: China's solar growth and US manufacturing

turns.en: So it's almost like China can add an entire grid.
turns.zh: 所以这几乎就像中国可以增加一整个电网。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Just in solar.
turns.zh: 仅靠太阳能。

turns.en: So the numbers are wild. I don't think China's doing it to lower carbon. I think China's doing it because they need to grow very, very fast. And I think that's what you're seeing the last two or three years in the United States, where solar and storage is also taking 75-80% plus.
turns.zh: 所以这些数字很疯狂。我不认为中国这样做是为了降低碳排放。我认为中国这样做是因为他们需要非常非常快地增长。而且我认为这就是你在过去两三年在美国看到的，太阳能和储能也占据了75-80%以上的份额。

turns.en: You asked about what the US aspects for it. I think the US, both Democrats and Republicans, have done a lot of disincentives and a lot of incentives for advanced manufacturing. Whether it was the IRA in the past or the OOTBA now, those incentives are in place. A lot of tariffs are in place. I think a lot of those create an ability for the US to be on a level playing field with other competitors. Quite simply, I think America lost a lot of its manufacturing muscle. We're building a new facility outside of it's about an hour north of Austin in Rockdale. It used to be an old Alcoa plant. Now it's just a piece of dirt. We're building a 2.1 gigawatt solar facility there that's going to make the cells that go into our plant in Dallas.
turns.zh: 你问到了美国方面的因素。我认为美国，无论是民主党还是共和党，都对先进制造业设置了很多抑制措施和很多激励措施。无论是过去的《通胀削减法案》还是现在的《海外贸易法案》，这些激励措施都已到位。还有很多关税。我认为其中很多措施让美国能够与其他竞争对手公平竞争。很简单，我认为美国失去了很多制造业实力。我们正在奥斯汀以北约一小时车程的罗克代尔建设一个新设施。那里曾经是一个老铝业公司工厂。现在只是一片空地。我们正在那里建设一个2.1吉瓦的太阳能设施，将生产用于我们达拉斯工厂的电池片。

transcript.secZh: 中国太阳能增长与美国制造

turns.en: So, what are the main bottlenecks? Is it purely capital? Because ideally T1 could just copy and paste the Dallas facility a thousand times. But what does it take to actually do that?
turns.zh: 那么，主要瓶颈是什么？纯粹是资本吗？因为理想情况下，T1可以像复制粘贴一样将达拉斯工厂复制一千次。但实际上要做到这一点需要什么？

turns.en: The solar chain is really cool because it has almost four different parts. It's got the digging out silica and making polysilicon rocks. Then you're going into ingots and wafers. Those wafers are also very similar in the semiconductor industry. Then it kind of splits. The high-grade stuff goes to semiconductors. The low-grade stuff goes to solar. Then you take a solar cell plant. The solar cell plant is basically a low-grade semiconductor plant. You're making the cells, making a fab. And then those cells go to the low-tech part, which is in Dallas. Dallas is where I always joke about it's like a 5-gigawatt site with 50 trucks a day bringing in glass. Put in the cells in between and glue them together. And then you got 50 trucks a day going out with glass. So it's like Dan's glass company, we just move them. But the cell part is really interesting and that's where a lot of the advances come from: specialty gases, process equipment, engineers, process equipment that's very specialized. That's the higher-tech part. So for me, it's very interesting as we go backwards and integrate. To answer your question on how do we get more of it, it starts with contracts. We're a smaller company. We're not the scale of what Elon's doing in terms of his announcement with solar. I don't have unlimited capital and funds, nor do I want a lot of dilution as a public company. We need contracts. Those contracts then allow us to get the risk capital that we need to build these plants. But the building part is not the hard part. I'd say the building part in America is more about navigating through certain regulations, navigating through certain delays. Texas, I think, is very conducive. But as long as I have power, as long as I have access to water and we go full recycling on the water, but as we go into water and power and low-cost inputs, we feel we're in a great place in Texas to build.
turns.zh: 太阳能产业链真的很酷，因为它几乎有四个不同的部分。首先是挖掘二氧化硅并制造多晶硅块。然后进入硅锭和硅片。这些硅片在半导体行业也非常相似。然后它分叉了。高等级的材料进入半导体，低等级的材料进入太阳能。然后你有一个太阳能电池工厂。太阳能电池工厂基本上是一个低等级的半导体工厂。你制造电池片，制造一个晶圆厂。然后这些电池片进入低技术部分，也就是达拉斯。达拉斯是我经常开玩笑的地方，就像一个5吉瓦的场地，每天有50辆卡车运进玻璃。把电池片放在中间，然后粘合在一起。然后每天有50辆卡车运出玻璃。所以就像丹的玻璃公司，我们只是搬运它们。但电池片部分真的很有趣，很多进步都来自那里：特种气体、工艺设备、工程师、非常专业的工艺设备。那是高技术部分。所以对我来说，当我们向后整合时，这非常有趣。回答你关于如何获得更多的问题，从合同开始。我们是一家较小的公司。我们没有像埃隆在太阳能方面宣布的那种规模。我没有无限的资本和资金，也不想作为上市公司大量稀释股权。我们需要合同。这些合同让我们能够获得建造这些工厂所需的风险资本。但建造部分并不难。我认为在美国，建造部分更多的是要应对某些法规和某些延误。我认为德克萨斯州非常有利。但只要有电，只要有水，并且我们完全循环用水，但当我们考虑水和电以及低成本投入时，我们觉得在德克萨斯州建造是一个很好的地方。

transcript.secZh: 规模化太阳能制造的瓶颈

turns.en: Who are the main buyers? Like who are you spending the most time with? Or will you actually sell to an Amazon Web Services or Google who's building out a new data center?
turns.zh: 主要买家是谁？比如你花最多时间与谁打交道？或者你实际上会卖给像亚马逊网络服务或谷歌这样正在建设新数据中心的公司吗？

turns.en: We focus on utility scale, and that's capturing the largest utilities, but it's also capturing all the big tech companies that you mentioned. We're usually selling directly through developers that then face those customers. Some of them, for confidentiality, don't want to be disclosed. One of them, Treaty Oak, we recently announced; they're a large developer based in Austin. So I imagine that they'll have a lot of their customers exactly what you just said. The demand for data centers is surreal. The demand for power is surreal. A lot of people know we're waiting for turbines until '29 to '30. So you're not going to get that there. So if you want to start scaling power fast, you need solar and storage right now. So that's a big driver, and that's the bulk of what's driving the demand. Behind that, some smaller utilities are looking to add to the grid and capacity. But by far the other big trend is that as you're running out of spaces to connect power and interconnections, a lot of things are shifting to behind-the-meter or distributed generation. This is where you're building first power islands. You're building data centers with power islands that can later connect to the grid because affordability is a big issue. Nobody wants a data center that's going to raise prices of electricity. The scientist was very vocal about that in Florida: data centers going to bring good jobs and things, but then what are they going to do to electricity prices? So I think it's really important that the industry gets a handle on how energy can also be in an area that's not going to put more stress on the grid.
turns.zh: 我们专注于公用事业规模，这涵盖了最大的公用事业公司，但也涵盖了所有你提到的大型科技公司。我们通常通过开发商直接销售，然后他们面对这些客户。其中一些客户出于保密原因不愿公开。我们最近宣布的Treaty Oak就是其中之一，他们是总部位于奥斯汀的大型开发商。所以我想他们会有很多客户，正如你所说的那样。对数据中心的需求是超现实的。对电力的需求是超现实的。很多人都知道我们等待涡轮机要等到2029到2030年。所以你在那里得不到那个。所以如果你想快速扩大电力规模，你现在就需要太阳能和储能。所以这是一个很大的驱动力，也是推动需求的主要部分。除此之外，一些较小的公用事业公司正在寻求增加电网容量。但到目前为止，另一个大趋势是，随着连接电力和互连的空间越来越少，很多事情正在转向表后或分布式发电。这就是你首先建立电力孤岛的地方。你正在建设带有电力孤岛的数据中心，这些孤岛以后可以连接到电网，因为可负担性是一个大问题。没有人想要一个会提高电价的数据中心。那位科学家在佛罗里达州对此直言不讳：数据中心会带来好工作等等，但然后它们会对电价做什么？所以我认为行业必须掌握如何让能源也能存在于一个不会给电网带来更多压力的区域。

transcript.sec: Future scaling plans and vertical integration

turns.en: So if I restate the current status of the business: G1 is the first facility up and running. G2 you're building. What does the future look like? How far into the future? Obviously, you need the contracts to unlock new plants, but how do you want this to scale? Where else do you want to build? How quickly do you want to copy-paste these facilities?
turns.zh: 所以如果我重述一下业务的当前状态：G1是第一个投入运营的设施。G2你正在建设。未来看起来怎么样？未来多远？显然，你需要合同来解锁新工厂，但你希望如何扩大规模？你还想在哪里建设？你希望多快复制这些设施？

turns.en: If I had the contracts, I'd love to copy-paste Dallas like that. Whether it's six months to a year, those are fast. We've publicly said that we're on track to build G2 this year. And that's already started construction; we started that in December, and we're tracking to continue to build that. So that's our main asset, our main focus. G1's operating, G2 is in build. And then if I go to the next part of the supply chain, we get our polysilicon and our wafers from Corning and Hemlock, so that we've completed the entire chain to be an American chain.
turns.zh: 如果我有合同，我很想那样复制达拉斯。无论是六个月到一年，那些都很快。我们已经公开表示，我们有望在今年建设G2。而且已经开始建设了；我们在12月开始了，并且我们正在继续建设。所以那是我们的主要资产，我们的主要焦点。G1在运营，G2在建。然后如果我进入供应链的下一部分，我们从康宁和赫姆洛克获得多晶硅和硅片，这样我们就完成了整个链条，成为一条美国链条。

turns.en: How do you think about the data centers in space? Elon's notorious for vertically integrating, but there's going to be a lot of players. It's probably a slightly different solar panel than what you might put on the ground, I imagine.
turns.zh: 你怎么看待太空中的数据中心？埃隆以垂直整合而闻名，但会有很多参与者。我想那可能是一种与你可能放在地面上的太阳能板略有不同的太阳能板。

transcript.secZh: 未来扩展计划与垂直整合

turns.en: Is that something where you're interested in talking to folks about a contract to deliver solar panels that would wind up on satellites in space?
turns.zh: 你是否感兴趣与人们讨论一份合同，交付最终用于太空卫星的太阳能板？

turns.en: I'd love to. When you look at silicon-based, probably more on the PERC side than the TOPCon side, there's quite a bit of demand for low Earth orbit in particular, rather than deep space. So silicon wafers do work in space. The question really becomes, are we going to use it for terrestrial demand or space demand? Obviously we're not sending up modules with glass to space, but at the cell level or at the wafer level, we'd love to feed into that as a customer base. Love to do that.
turns.zh: 我很乐意。当你考虑硅基技术，可能更多是PERC而非TOPCon，低地球轨道有相当大的需求，而不是深空。所以硅片在太空中确实有效。问题真正在于，我们是将其用于地面需求还是太空需求？显然我们不会把带玻璃的组件送上太空，但在电池片或硅片层面，我们很乐意将其作为客户群。很乐意这样做。

turns.en: Being a public company CEO can be stressful. What do the short sellers get wrong about T1 Energy these days?
turns.zh: 作为上市公司CEO压力很大。做空者最近对T1 Energy有什么误解？

turns.en: Being an ex buysider and ex sellsider writing research reports, I know the journey. To be a portfolio manager or sellside analyst, you get a lot of things right and a lot wrong. I think for the most part, short sellers like to say, 'Oh, they're not going to make this, or they're not going to do it the right way, compliance with US law, foreign entities concerns.' There's always concerns about the solar industry and misunderstandings about how China's influence is. That's an old story. We're an American company, American public company, New York Stock Exchange listed. We bought an asset and we're running it and building it now. That's one of the biggest parts. The other part, I get stressed because sometimes my IR guy goes, 'Don't read Reddit today.' I'm like, 'Okay, but I want to.' And then someone says, 'Hey, go kill yourself.' Like, Jesus.
turns.zh: 作为前买方和前卖方，写过研究报告，我知道这个过程。成为投资组合经理或卖方分析师，你会做对很多事，也会做错很多。我认为大部分情况下，做空者喜欢说：‘哦，他们做不出来，或者他们不会以正确的方式做，遵守美国法律，外国实体担忧。’太阳能行业总有担忧，对中国影响力的误解。那是个老故事了。我们是一家美国公司，美国上市公司，在纽约证券交易所上市。我们买了一项资产，正在运营和建设。这是最主要的部分之一。另一部分，我会有压力，因为有时我的投资者关系负责人说：‘今天别看Reddit。’我说：‘好吧，但我想看。’然后有人说：‘嘿，去自杀吧。’真是的。

turns.en: Fortunately, some of your fans have come over to our chat and they want to know when are we getting merch? They want T1 merch. Any plans?
turns.zh: 幸运的是，你的一些粉丝来到我们的聊天室，他们想知道我们什么时候有周边？他们想要T1周边。有计划吗？

turns.en: We will put that site up as fast as possible and get the hat and get these things, maybe without this logo here or this one here, but get that there. We're excited to get the merch. We love our shareholders. We're thankful for that. We have a good feeling for all those parts.
turns.zh: 我们会尽快上线那个网站，拿到帽子和其他东西，可能没有这个标志或那个标志，但会弄出来。我们很兴奋推出周边。我们爱我们的股东。我们对此心存感激。我们对所有这些部分感觉良好。

turns.en: I'd love to know the shape of the company. It feels like an American reindustrialization process. How big is the company? It feels like you're a job creator. What roles are you hiring for? If this really scales as I hope it does, what does the shape of the workforce look like?
turns.zh: 我想了解公司的形态。感觉像美国再工业化进程。公司有多大？感觉你在创造就业。你们在招聘什么职位？如果真如我所愿规模化，劳动力形态会是什么样？

turns.en: We're 1,200 already in Dallas. We're going to be 1,500 in Austin. In Austin headquarters we're going to be over 100. If I can do my recruiting call, go to t1energy.com careers. We need engineers. The more rogue you are as an engineer, the better. This has to be a new style of manufacturing. Our corporate policy for expenses is very simple: do not buy anything illegal, either good or service. Two, it's corporate money, be careful with it. Three, we can watch everything you buy with your Ramp card and we do with AI and it's awesome. No one has to do T&E reports. It's just about accountability as people. We just have to build as fast as we can. Throw some of the old rule books out. That's part of the reason we wanted to be in Texas and Austin. There are a lot of engineers there. We need them from the semiconductor sector, from the manufacturing sector, from the robotics sector. It's all about that growth and more contracts, maybe some contracts for space one day. The more contracts we get, the faster we can grow.
turns.zh: 我们在达拉斯已经有1200人。在奥斯汀我们将达到1500人。在奥斯汀总部我们将超过100人。如果我可以做招聘广告，请访问t1energy.com/careers。我们需要工程师。工程师越有闯劲越好。这必须是一种新型制造。我们的费用公司政策非常简单：不购买任何非法物品，无论是商品还是服务。第二，这是公司资金，要小心使用。第三，我们可以通过你的Ramp卡监控所有购买，我们用AI来做，效果很棒。没有人需要做差旅报销报告。这只是关于人的责任感。我们必须尽可能快地建设。扔掉一些旧规则书。这也是我们想留在德克萨斯和奥斯汀的部分原因。那里有很多工程师。我们需要来自半导体行业、制造业、机器人行业的工程师。这一切都关乎增长和更多合同，也许有一天会有太空合同。我们获得的合同越多，增长就越快。

turns.en: That's amazing. Well, thank you so much for everything you're doing. Thanks for taking the time to come chat with us. Congrats on the progress. I'm rooting for you. This is an amazing project.
turns.zh: 太棒了。非常感谢你所做的一切。感谢你抽时间来和我们聊天。祝贺取得的进展。我支持你。这是一个了不起的项目。

turns.en: You should at some point. I think we were live. Between the first time we talked about T1 and the second, the stock was up like 150%.
turns.zh: 你应该在某个时候看看。我想我们当时是直播。从我们第一次谈论T1到第二次，股价涨了大约150%。

turns.en: That's why we're podcasters now. That's why we're the buy side. But yeah, thank you for the work you do.
turns.zh: 这就是为什么我们现在是播客主持人。这就是为什么我们是买方。但谢谢你所做的工作。

turns.en: Thank you so much for taking the time.
turns.zh: 非常感谢你抽出时间。

turns.en: Loud and clear. Merch. That's the prior most priority, right? That's priority number two after re-industrializing America and saving the solar industry, but it's a close second.
turns.zh: 收到。周边。那是首要任务，对吧？那是仅次于再工业化美国、拯救太阳能行业的第二优先事项，但紧随其后。

turns.en: Anyway, thank you so much, Dan. This was fantastic meeting you. You're welcome back anytime. We'll talk to you soon.
turns.zh: 总之，非常感谢你，Dan。见到你太好了。随时欢迎你回来。我们很快再聊。

turns.en: We're very excited to see that. Anyway, let me tell you about Octa first. Octa helps you assign every AI agent a trusted identity. So you get the power of AI without the risk. Secure every agent, secure any agent. And we will go into this Wall Street Journal article about how people are interacting with AI. So it started as an innocent hobby to entertain and distract herself while going through a divorce. Erica Aerson used Anthropic's Claude to build a bot that generates images based on prompts from her and her friends. Soon she promoted Claude to an advisory position in her personal life. It didn't take long for her kids to think she might need a break from her new friend. These days, there are two kinds of people: those who use AI for everything and, well, everyone else. Super users are contracting out their work to groups of agents, amping up their productivity. They're dropping advice from ChatGPT as if the OpenAI bot is their most trusted consigliere, counseling on everything from business strategy to whether or not you should let your friend get a perm. It's an interesting question. Everyone else is wondering if their loved ones may be taking things a bit too far. Maybe some tasks should be reserved for humans. Maybe there's dignity in that. Maybe some of us like the way things are. Aerson says Claude was particularly helpful as she navigated a relationship she suspected wasn't going to go anywhere. He was like, quote, this is Claude talking. 'Hey, I don't think you're going to be able to extract yourself from this if you don't stop talking to this person for a while,' says the Portland, Oregon resident who works in operations for a tech company. When she was later tempted to rekindle contact, Aerson would check in with Claude. 'Stick to your guns,' it told her. AI hasn't been beneficial for every relationship, though. While her kids watch TV, Aerson likes to tinker with Claude. Her sons don't approve. 'It's a waste of her time,' says Grayson, 11. 'She puts a lot of time into AI just to make boring old pictures.'
turns.zh: 我们非常兴奋看到这一点。总之，让我先介绍一下 Octa。Octa 帮助您为每个 AI 智能体分配一个可信身份。这样您就能获得 AI 的力量而无需承担风险。保护每个智能体，保护任何智能体。我们将深入探讨《华尔街日报》那篇关于人们如何与 AI 互动的文章。这一切始于一个无害的爱好，一位名叫 Erica Aerson 的女性在经历离婚时用它来娱乐和分散注意力。她使用 Anthropic 的 Claude 构建了一个机器人，根据她和朋友的提示生成图像。很快，她将 Claude 提升为个人生活中的顾问角色。没过多久，她的孩子们就认为她可能需要和这个新朋友保持距离。如今，有两种人：一种是用 AI 做所有事情的人，另一种是其他人。超级用户将工作外包给一群智能体，大幅提高生产力。他们向 ChatGPT 寻求建议，仿佛 OpenAI 的机器人是他们最信任的顾问，从商业策略到是否应该让朋友烫发，无所不包。这是个有趣的问题。其他人则在想，他们的亲人是否有点过头了。也许有些任务应该留给人类。也许其中有尊严。也许有些人喜欢现状。Aerson 说，在她处理一段她怀疑不会有结果的关系时，Claude 特别有帮助。它说，引用 Claude 的话：“嘿，我认为如果你不暂时停止和这个人说话，你无法从中脱身。”这位在俄勒冈州波特兰市一家科技公司从事运营工作的居民说道。后来当她忍不住想重新联系时，Aerson 会向 Claude 确认。它告诉她：“坚持你的立场。”不过，AI 并非对每段关系都有益。当她的孩子们看电视时，Aerson 喜欢摆弄 Claude。她的儿子们不赞成。11 岁的 Grayson 说：“这是浪费时间。她花很多时间在 AI 上，就为了做无聊的老照片。”

turns.en: Wait, how is she making pictures with Claude?
turns.zh: 等等，她是怎么用 Claude 制作图片的？

turns.en: Yeah, I'm very... They don't have an image model.
turns.zh: 是啊，我很……他们没有图像模型。

turns.en: Must be calling some API under the hood. I don't know.
turns.zh: 肯定是在底层调用某个 API。我不知道。

turns.en: Is she like on Claude Code harnessing Nano Banano Pro?
turns.zh: 她是不是在用 Claude Code 利用 Nano Banano Pro？

turns.en: Maybe she's talking about the SVG images.
turns.zh: 也许她说的是 SVG 图像。

turns.en: Oh, yes. Maybe.
turns.zh: 哦，对。也许吧。

turns.en: No, but there's an image in here that's her image and it looks like a Grok image.
turns.zh: 不，但这里有一张她的图片，看起来像是 Grok 生成的。

turns.en: Maybe she's on Grok. This is an odd article. I'm very confused by this.
turns.zh: 也许她在用 Grok。这篇文章很奇怪。我很困惑。

turns.en: Anderson, age 8, has a different complaint. The technology's accuracy: ChatGPT couldn't correctly identify a Pokemon. Another time he's just like, 'This is unacceptable. Zero on Pokemon bench. I'm selling the stock. It's going to zero.'
turns.zh: 8 岁的 Anderson 有另一个抱怨。技术的准确性：ChatGPT 无法正确识别一个宝可梦。还有一次他说：‘这不可接受。宝可梦基准测试零分。我要卖股票。它要跌到零了。’

turns.en: It's over. It's over.
turns.zh: 完了。完了。

turns.en: It's over, says Anderson, age eight. Another time Anderson struggled to get it to generate a suitable picture of himself. 'Once I only had one leg and once I had three legs,' he said. 'This is so cute,' thinking about this eight-year-old being like, 'This is slop. This is slop,' says an 8-year-old.
turns.zh: 8 岁的 Anderson 说：‘完了。’还有一次，Anderson 很难让它生成一张合适的自己的照片。他说：‘有一次我只有一条腿，还有一次我有三条腿。’想到这个八岁孩子说：‘这是垃圾。这是垃圾。’真可爱。

turns.en: Eric Davik, a startup founder in Brooklyn, uses AI for recipe planning, workouts, business strategy, and vibe coding with his son. The 42-year-old also uploads recordings of his executive coaching meetings to ChatGPT to analyze his progress.
turns.zh: 布鲁克林的初创公司创始人 Eric Davik 使用 AI 进行食谱规划、锻炼、商业策略以及与儿子一起进行氛围编程。这位 42 岁的父亲还将他的高管教练会议录音上传到 ChatGPT 来分析他的进展。

turns.en: I've done some... I asked ChatGPT about a workout plan at one point. I don't go back to it that often. Recipe planning. I'll have it do... I did have it do a deep research report all around where we could go to food.
turns.zh: 我做过一些……我一度问过 ChatGPT 关于锻炼计划的事。我不太经常回去看。食谱规划。我会让它做……我确实让它做了一份关于我们可以去哪里吃饭的深度研究报告。

turns.en: Do you think it's actually good for... Do you think LLMs are good for workout planning? Just taking the... I typically want a workout plan that is... I mean, we don't work out every day and we kind of do a vibes-based approach. I would say...
turns.zh: 你认为它真的适合……你认为大语言模型适合锻炼计划吗？就拿……我通常想要一个锻炼计划是……我的意思是，我们不是每天都锻炼，我们有点随性。我会说……

turns.en: Try to push it hard and not... We're usually talking about technology and business during our workouts.
turns.zh: 试着努力推动，而不是……我们通常在锻炼时谈论技术和商业。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: But historically, let's say when I was starting to work out, I would find a plan, but I wanted it to be from a specific person that was making that plan with an intention around it for somebody in that state, which was maybe like I've been weight training for six months at the time.
turns.zh: 但历史上，比如说当我刚开始锻炼时，我会找一个计划，但我希望它来自一个特定的人，那个人为处于某种状态的人制定计划，比如我当时已经进行了六个月的重量训练。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, there's a huge...
turns.zh: 是的。是的。是的，有一个巨大的……

turns.en: And so if you just take... The reason I ask is like I've had terrible luck doing recipes, getting recipes from LLMs, because I think it just takes the average of every recipe for a certain item on the internet and just kind of gives it to you, and you can end up with like... I think we tried to make waffles once and it just... the waffle was clearly the recipe was just wrong because they were really runny, you know, it just wasn't working. It was like it had botched the different...
turns.zh: 所以如果你只是拿……我问的原因是，我在从大语言模型获取食谱方面运气很差，因为我认为它只是取了互联网上某个菜品的所有食谱的平均值，然后直接给你，结果可能……我记得我们试过一次做华夫饼，结果……华夫饼的食谱明显是错的，因为面糊太稀了，你知道，就是不行。就像是搞砸了不同的……

turns.en: Well, you haven't used the latest models. You throw Codex 5.3 at that.
turns.zh: 嗯，你没用最新的模型。你用 Codex 5.3 试试。

turns.en: Yeah, but it's obviously not an... It's waffle bench needs to be... It's not an IQ thing. It's just... we're creating waffle bench. We're moving the goalposts. Needs to be able to make waffles. Great waffles. Anyway, let me tell you about Cognition. They're the makers of Devin, the AI software engineer. Crush your backlog with your personal AI engineering team.
turns.zh: 是啊，但这显然不是……华夫饼基准测试需要……这不是智商问题。只是……我们在创建华夫饼基准测试。我们在移动目标。它必须能做华夫饼。好华夫饼。总之，让我介绍一下 Cognition。他们是 AI 软件工程师 Devin 的创造者。用你的个人 AI 工程团队解决积压工作。

turns.en: Anyway, the wife of Eric, the startup founder, says... his wife is reluctant to use AI, especially for any writing-related task. A freelance journalist, she doesn't believe the technology can replicate her 20 years of professional experience. Her husband's use can annoy her, like when she asked him to write an important email to her son's school. As she read it over, something seemed off. 'I was like, this is basically the same paragraph repeated three times,' says the 45-year-old. She asked him, 'Did ChatGPT do this?' She says, 'He admitted to it.' She's probably not lying. He says, 'I don't remember the specific instance.' I love how they're just like cross-interviewing.
turns.zh: 总之，初创公司创始人 Eric 的妻子说……他的妻子不愿使用 AI，尤其是任何与写作相关的任务。作为一名自由记者，她不相信这项技术能复制她 20 年的专业经验。她丈夫的使用方式有时会惹恼她，比如当她让他给儿子的学校写一封重要的邮件时。她读了一遍，感觉有些不对劲。这位 45 岁的女士说：‘我当时想，这基本上就是同一段话重复了三遍。’她问他：‘这是 ChatGPT 写的吗？’她说：‘他承认了。’她可能没撒谎。他说：‘我不记得具体是哪次了。’我喜欢他们这种交叉采访的方式。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: It's very funny because I leaned the complete opposite way on preschool applications once. I was like, 'Okay, I know that everyone else is going to be using AI slop, so I need to write extra conversationally.' So I'm in the application being like, 'Hey, what's up? My name's John.' Just completely organically writing like flow of state. Then I was like, 'I probably need to polish this up a little bit, but I'm definitely not giving it the LLM flavor because I think that's going to be a tell for a lot of people on the other side reading these.' Another thing is that people are getting sick of this slop. But what do you think, Tyler?
turns.zh: 这很有意思，因为我在幼儿园申请这件事上完全反其道而行之。我当时想：‘好吧，我知道其他人都会用AI垃圾，所以我得写得特别口语化。’于是我在申请里写：‘嘿，你好吗？我叫约翰。’完全自然地写，像意识流一样。然后我又想：‘我可能需要稍微润色一下，但我绝对不会让它有LLM的味道，因为我觉得对面读这些的人很多都能看出来。’另外，人们已经开始厌倦这种垃圾了。你怎么看，Tyler？

turns.en: Yeah, I just don't resonate with this article at all. Over Christmas break, I think I onboarded my dad to Claude Code. I'm like, 'Yeah, you need to be using the best model. Why? You haven't tried 4 or 5 yet. What are you doing?'
turns.zh: 是啊，我完全不认同这篇文章。圣诞假期的时候，我让我爸用上了Claude Code。我说：‘对，你得用最好的模型。为什么？你还没试过4或5呢。你在干嘛？’

turns.en: Yeah. But what was the killer use case? Did you tell him, 'Write software, get on GitHub'? Did you just get him set up?
turns.zh: 是啊。但杀手级用例是什么？你告诉他‘写软件，上GitHub’了吗？你只是帮他设置好了？

turns.en: Just like anything. Any task, just have Claude do it and it'll probably work.
turns.zh: 就是任何事。任何任务，让Claude去做，它大概就能搞定。

turns.en: I mean, I don't cook much, so I don't know about the recipe stuff. But the funny thing is that there are recipes. You can just take a recipe book and open it. You can just Google a recipe. But you're like, 'I need Claude Code to work for six hours to get me a recipe for waffles.' That's not actually the promise. That's not actually advancing. The advancing is like, 'Build me a piece of software that will generate me a new waffle recipe based on what's happening in the news and what my kids are into, and generate the images.' Do a lot more that would take a long time. You're not actually going to save time if you're using an LLM for something that's Googleable.
turns.zh: 我是说，我不怎么做饭，所以我不太了解食谱那方面。但好笑的是，食谱是现成的。你可以直接拿一本食谱翻开，也可以直接谷歌搜一个食谱。但你却说：‘我需要Claude Code工作六个小时，给我弄个华夫饼食谱。’这其实不是它的承诺，也不是进步。进步是：‘给我建一个软件，能根据新闻动态和我孩子的喜好生成新的华夫饼食谱，还能生成图片。’做更多需要很长时间的事情。如果你用LLM做谷歌就能搞定的事，其实并不会节省时间。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: I don't know. It's just we're in a really weird moment right now where the models are good at so many things. They are also incredibly confident even when they're wrong. Sometimes you're making... I had a friend that got a ticket for dictating a text into their phone while driving. They got pulled over and were given a ticket. They were trying to figure out, 'Am I going to get a point on my license for this infraction?' And the model was saying one thing. But I've had other experiences very recently with models where they get it completely wrong. So I was like, 'Okay, you kind of got an indication.' Good thing you had me. You texted me and I was like, 'I know more than ChatGPT about this.'
turns.zh: 我不知道。只是我们现在处在一个非常奇怪的时刻，模型在很多事情上都很擅长。它们即使错了也极其自信。有时候你会……我有个朋友因为开车时用手机口述短信被开了罚单。他被拦下并收到了罚单。他想弄清楚：‘这次违规会不会扣分？’模型说了一件事。但我最近也有其他经历，模型完全搞错了。所以我想：‘好吧，你算是有个提示。’幸好你有我。你发短信给我，我说：‘关于这个，我比ChatGPT知道得多。’

turns.en: Anyway, let me tell you about public investing for those who take it seriously. Stocks, options, bonds, crypto, treasuries, and more with great customer service. And our first guest of the lightning round is here. Should we bring in Mandy Fields, the CFO of Elf Beauty? Welcome to the show.
turns.zh: 总之，让我来介绍一下认真对待公共投资的人。股票、期权、债券、加密货币、国债等等，还有优质的客户服务。闪电轮的第一位嘉宾来了。我们请进Elf Beauty的首席财务官Mandy Fields吗？欢迎来到节目。

turns.en: What's happening? Hello. Thanks for having me.
turns.zh: 怎么了？你好。谢谢邀请。

turns.en: Great to meet you. Would love a quick introduction on yourself since it's your first time.
turns.zh: 很高兴见到你。既然你是第一次来，请简单介绍一下自己。

turns.en: Sure. So, yeah, I'm Mandy Fields. I'm chief financial officer at Elf Beauty. I've been here seven years, my 28th consecutive quarter of reporting the results for Elf Beauty.
turns.zh: 好的。我是Mandy Fields，Elf Beauty的首席财务官。我在这里工作了七年，连续第28个季度报告Elf Beauty的业绩。

turns.en: Amazing. Amazing. And what is the latest? What's the news?
turns.zh: 太棒了。太棒了。最近有什么消息？

turns.en: Well, the news is we just reported another tremendous quarter. 38% net sales growth, 79% adjusted EBITDA. Oh, hey. Yeah, I love that. And so we're really having a lot of fun over here at Elf Beauty. And that's the results.
turns.zh: 消息是，我们刚刚报告了又一个出色的季度。净销售额增长38%，调整后EBITDA增长79%。哦，嘿。是的，我喜欢这个。我们在Elf Beauty真的玩得很开心。这就是结果。

turns.en: What's driving that right now? Right now I feel like there's so much uncertainty around the strength of the consumer broadly. There are so many different indicators. You can look at government data and see one thing that might give you one signal, and then you look at quarters like you guys are putting up and it says something else. But what are you guys seeing?
turns.zh: 现在是什么在推动这个？现在我觉得消费者整体实力存在很大的不确定性。有这么多不同的指标。你可以看政府数据，得到一个信号，然后看你们这样的季度业绩，又说明别的东西。但你们看到了什么？

turns.en: Yeah, well, it really comes down to three things. Our value proposition, and so 75% of our portfolio on the ELF brand is at $10 or less, and that really speaks to that consumer seeking value. You pair that with our incredible innovation, the marketing engine that we have who, by the way, I heard that you all are going to be at the big game Super Bowl ads. So congratulations on that. We will be as well.
turns.zh: 是的，这归结为三件事。我们的价值主张，ELF品牌75%的产品组合价格在10美元或以下，这确实迎合了追求价值的消费者。再加上我们不可思议的创新和营销引擎，顺便说一句，我听说你们都要在超级碗广告中亮相。恭喜你们。我们也会参加。

turns.en: Thank you. I think our buy will be at a smaller scale than yours. I'm assuming.
turns.zh: 谢谢。我想我们的投入规模会比你们小。我猜。

turns.en: I saw that. I saw yours was about $50,000, something like that.
turns.zh: 我看到了。我看到你们的大约是5万美元，差不多这样。

turns.en: Something like that. Yeah. But anyway, I want to get into the Super Bowl too, but continue.
turns.zh: 差不多吧。是的。不过，我也想聊聊超级碗，但请继续。

turns.en: So, and then we also have the incredible addition of Road to our net sales performance. And so, Road is an acquisition that we did. We closed in August. That's Hailey Bieber's brand. And it has just had tremendous performance. And so, you take all of those things together, that's how you get to that 38% net sales growth for the quarter.
turns.zh: 然后，我们还新增了Road品牌，它对我们的净销售额表现贡献巨大。Road是我们收购的品牌，8月份完成交易。那是Hailey Bieber的品牌。它的表现非常出色。所以，把这些加在一起，就得到了季度净销售额38%的增长。

turns.en: How is the marketing mix changing over the last few years? I mean, Meta had a blowout quarter. It seems like ads are performing very well on that platform. The influencer economy is still growing. How much... what does the mix of marketing spend look like?
turns.zh: 过去几年营销组合是如何变化的？我是说，Meta的季度业绩非常出色。广告在那个平台上表现很好。网红经济仍在增长。营销支出的组合是什么样的？

turns.en: Yeah, so our marketing spend is pretty well balanced. We allocate 24 to 26% of our net sales to marketing and digital. And we are there on every platform where our community is present. And that has really been a key differentiator with ELF. I think that we are so close to our community. We keep our ear to the ground, and so that really comes through in our innovation and the marketing campaigns that we decide to put forward are really culturally relevant and connected to our communities.
turns.zh: 是的，我们的营销支出相当均衡。我们将净销售额的24%到26%分配给营销和数字渠道。我们在社区所在的每个平台都有布局。这确实是ELF的一个关键差异化因素。我认为我们与社区非常贴近。我们保持敏锐的洞察力，这体现在我们的创新中，我们决定推出的营销活动真正具有文化相关性，并与我们的社区紧密相连。

turns.en: And how are you thinking about the agent commerce question? Is this something that you need to do anything special for, or does it just naturally happen? It's still so early, but these things in AI, they take off so fast.
turns.zh: 那么你们如何看待智能体商务这个问题？这是需要你们做特殊准备的事情，还是它会自然发生？现在还为时过早，但AI领域的事情发展得非常快。

turns.en: I think it is still early. We're making sure that we have the backend correct so that we can participate in agentic commerce. But, you know, AI is just one of those things where this is kind of one of those technologies that can help on both the revenue side as well as the efficiency side.
turns.zh: 我认为现在还为时过早。我们正在确保后端正确，以便能够参与智能体商务。但你知道，AI就是这样一种技术，它既能在收入端也能在效率端提供帮助。

turns.en: And so, I think it's pretty incredible from that perspective. Is it possible to build a direct-to-consumer brand from scratch these days without a huge celebrity partner? It feels like more and more of the deals that I see that actually go the distance, get to big revenue numbers, they got somebody attached.
turns.zh: 从这个角度看，我觉得非常了不起。如今从头打造一个直接面向消费者的品牌，没有大牌明星合作可能吗？我感觉我看到越来越多真正成功、收入可观的交易，背后都有人撑腰。

turns.en: Yeah, it's interesting that you say that because I think that's actually pretty rare for a celebrity brand to make it to this side. Like if I think about Haley's brand, Road, you know, it's very rare to see a company go to $200 million in sales in less than three years on 10 products. I mean, you usually are seeing multiple products, multiple SKUs, categories to get there, and so I think what we found in Road is pretty rare.
turns.zh: 嗯，你这么说很有意思，因为我觉得名人品牌能做到这个地步其实相当罕见。比如想到 Haley 的品牌 Road，你知道，很少看到一家公司靠 10 个产品在不到三年内销售额达到 2 亿美元。通常你需要多个产品、多个 SKU、多个品类才能达到这个水平，所以我认为我们在 Road 上取得的成就非常难得。

turns.en: Yeah. Well, congratulations on the progress. Thank you so much. I wanted to, before you jump off, I wanted to ask, as CFO, how you think about Super Bowl ads. I'm sure this isn't your first rodeo. What kind of goes into it? You know, there's so many different ways to approach a Super Bowl ad, but what's your logic going into it or kind of framework?
turns.zh: 是的。祝贺你们取得的进展。非常感谢。在你离开之前，我想问一下，作为 CFO，你怎么看待超级碗广告？我相信这不是你第一次经历了。这背后需要什么考量？你知道，超级碗广告有很多不同的方式，但你的逻辑或框架是什么？

turns.en: Yeah. So, Super Bowl ads are really about building awareness behind your brand. And so this is not our first rodeo. I think our first time was four years ago. We came with an ad with Jennifer Coolidge. It was a lot of fun. This year we had Melissa McCarthy as the actress in our Super Bowl ad. It's fantastic. So I really think it's about building brand awareness. If I think back, you know, five, six years, our overall awareness was around 13% as a brand, and now we're over 40%. And I credit that to a lot of the collaborations and the things like Super Bowl that we've done over the years.
turns.zh: 是的。超级碗广告实际上是为了提升品牌知名度。这不是我们第一次做。我记得第一次是四年前，我们和 Jennifer Coolidge 一起拍了一支广告，非常有趣。今年我们请了 Melissa McCarthy 出演超级碗广告，效果很棒。所以我认为这主要是为了建立品牌知名度。回想五六年前，我们的品牌整体知名度大约只有 13%，现在超过了 40%。这要归功于我们多年来所做的许多合作和像超级碗这样的活动。

turns.en: So, measurement.
turns.zh: 那么，衡量标准呢？

turns.en: Yeah. So, your CMO and your marketing team are not coming to you and saying, 'Cool, I'll give you the budget, but I want to see a move in topline the next week.' It's more about...
turns.zh: 是的。所以，你的 CMO 和营销团队不会来找你说，‘好的，我给你预算，但我希望下周就看到营收增长。’这更像是……

turns.en: I always say that I always want to see... like show me the money now.
turns.zh: 我总说我一直想看到……比如现在就给我看钱。

turns.en: Yeah. Okay. But in fact, it is sowing the seeds for a longer term investment. There are other things that we look at that are more direct revenue drivers. But Super Bowl investments like that are for the long term.
turns.zh: 是的。好吧。但实际上，这是为长期投资播下种子。我们还有其他更直接的收入驱动因素。但像超级碗这样的投资是为了长远。

turns.en: What's last question for me? What's your M&A outlook for the next couple years? I'm sure in a perfect world there'd be a new Road every single year that you could roll in, but obviously that's not the way. Great brands don't get started every single day or even year sometimes. So how are you thinking about it?
turns.zh: 最后一个问题。你对未来几年的并购前景怎么看？我敢肯定，在理想情况下，每年都会有一个新的 Road 可以纳入，但显然不是这样。伟大的品牌不是每天甚至每年都能诞生的。那么你是怎么考虑的呢？

turns.en: Well, we have a great portfolio of brands with us today, and so that's really going to be our focus on the existing portfolio and building those brands out even further, whether it be E.L.F. in cosmetics and skincare, Naturium, or Road. We have some really great brands in our portfolio, and so that's what we're going to be focused on. If somewhere along the way we found another Road, we'd be happy to bring it into the portfolio, but staying focused on what we have for now.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们目前拥有一个很棒的品牌组合，所以我们的重点将放在现有组合上，进一步打造这些品牌，无论是 E.L.F. 的化妆品和护肤品、Naturium 还是 Road。我们的组合中有一些非常优秀的品牌，这就是我们将专注的方向。如果在这个过程中我们发现了另一个 Road，我们会很乐意将其纳入组合，但眼下会专注于我们已有的品牌。

turns.en: Makes a ton of sense. Awesome. Well, thank you so much for taking the time. Congrats. Excited to see the ad on Sunday.
turns.zh: 非常有道理。太棒了。好的，非常感谢你抽出时间。祝贺你。很期待周日看到广告。

turns.en: Yeah, very excited. We'll talk to you soon. Yeah, congrats on the quarter. Cheers.
turns.zh: 是的，非常兴奋。我们很快再聊。是的，祝贺你们这个季度的表现。干杯。

turns.zh: 我完全同意。我发现，如你所知，我正在托管一处新房产，我和一个非常热衷马匹的人聊了聊，他们详细介绍了这处房产能养什么样的马，还给我讲了大致情况。我希望为……做出贡献，提高这些数字是每个人的责任，对吧？光靠一个人是不够的……

That's not the real number, but there's a lot of parking lots. What's going to happen to them when we don't need them because of autonomous cars? Stables. Yep. You take your Waymo or your autonomous Uber into the city. You hop on a steed and you go from place to place. Excellent execution, J. Excellent execution.
turns.zh: 我是说，Uber 的 CEO Dara Khosrowshahi 昨天来节目说，城市里 75% 的土地是停车场之类的。那不是真实数字，但确实有很多停车场。当我们因为自动驾驶汽车不再需要它们时，它们会变成什么？马厩。是的。你坐 Waymo 或自动驾驶 Uber 进城，然后跳上一匹马，从一个地方到另一个地方。执行得好，J。执行得好。

turns.zh: 我确实喜欢马。我也喜欢 Gusto，因为马有 Gusto，这个统一的薪资、福利和人力资源平台专为中小型企业打造。TVPN。它是 TVPN 的支柱。

turns.en: According to The Information, upcoming avocado model from Meta is referenced as the most capable model to date internally.
turns.zh: 据 The Information 报道，Meta 即将推出的 avocado 模型在内部被称为迄今为止最强大的模型。

turns.en: Mhm.
