turns.en: I, this doesn't tell you that much.
turns.zh: 我，这并不能说明太多。

turns.en: It doesn't. It's the most powerful iPhone ever. We get it. We get it. Like, but not even that. It's like this is like some... you know, like they could make the most capable model for Meta. Yeah.
turns.zh: 确实不能。就像‘史上最强大的 iPhone’一样。我们懂。我们懂。但甚至不是那样。这就像……你知道，他们可以为 Meta 打造最强大的模型。是的。

turns.en: Could be the least capable if you compare it to the other lab. So...
turns.zh: 如果与其他实验室相比，它可能是最弱的。所以……

turns.en: Yeah. But it's the same thing that happens at WWDC where you stand on stage and say it's the most powerful iPhone yet. It has the most battery life of an iPhone ever. And it's like, well, if it was a less powerful iPhone, you probably wouldn't launch it. But it's very exciting to see that they're making progress and they're sending out memos and they're leaking to The Information and their excitement is building. And I think that I do think that we are going to get something pretty powerful. And based on the team, everything like avocado is a good name, and based on the team and the compute and the money, like they're going to jump to the frontier. There's just no question that they'll be really, really close to frontier.
turns.zh: 是的。但这和 WWDC 上发生的事情一样，你站在台上说这是有史以来最强大的 iPhone，电池续航最长。但问题是，如果它不那么强大，你可能根本就不会发布它。但看到他们取得进展、发送备忘录、向 The Information 泄露消息、兴奋感不断升温，这非常令人兴奋。而且我认为我们确实会得到一些相当强大的东西。基于团队，像 avocado 这样的名字不错，基于团队、算力和资金，他们会跃居前沿。毫无疑问，他们会非常非常接近前沿。

turns.en: We got to go back to the horses. AI in the X chat says they have two horses doing their part. So, thank you.
turns.zh: 我们得回到马的话题。X 聊天中的 AI 说他们有两匹马在尽自己的一份力。所以，谢谢。

turns.en: Oh, thank you, Owl. And full circle, get those numbers up. Don't just say, 'Oh, I'm doing my part.' You got to be growing exponentially. You got to go to four, then 8, 16, 32, etc.
turns.zh: 哦，谢谢你，Owl。回到原点，提高这些数字。不要只说‘哦，我在尽自己的一份力’。你必须指数级增长。你必须到 4，然后 8、16、32，等等。

turns.en: Okay. Just on the meta model thing for a second.
turns.zh: 好的。就 Meta 模型的事再说一句。

turns.en: Historically, food-based models with food in the name have done really well, right? You have Nano Banana. Yes. You have Strawberry. Strawberry. Yep. That's all I can think of. But I mean, Avocado, it's a good sign. Yeah. Okay. Okay, it's good. It's good. Wasteland Capital a black pill. Yes. Tech has now entered a liquidation spiral where people like hedge funds are forced to sell good cheap accelerating assets like semis to cover their losses and expensive decelerating high PE like SaaS and AI victims. AI victims. Not sure how long this will take to play out. I don't know. It's been a brutal week. We needed the red suits this week. We did. But fortunately, our guest who's in person has a very special suit that I'm very excited to show you after our next guest who is in the green room waiting. Last post. Joe Weisenthal says, 'The selloff in everything that is not canned soup is relentless.' How's Caterpillar doing? Probably doing pretty well. Campbell is up today. I love it. Up 6% in the last 5 days. Flight to safety. Flight to Yeah. Buy some soup. Flight of soup. Buy some soup. Gemini 3 Pro, Google's most intelligent model yet. State-of-the-art reasoning, next level vibe coding, and deep multimodal understanding. Our next guest is in the green room waiting. We have Ivan from Daytona. Great name. Great name. We love Daytona.
turns.zh: 历史上，名字里带食物的模型表现都很好，对吧？有 Nano Banana。对。有 Strawberry。Strawberry。没错。我就想到这些。但我觉得，Avocado 是个好兆头。是啊。好吧，好吧，挺好的。Wasteland Capital 是个黑丸。是的。科技行业现在进入了清算螺旋，人们比如对冲基金被迫抛售优质廉价加速资产如半导体来弥补亏损，以及昂贵减速高市盈率的资产如 SaaS 和 AI 受害者。AI 受害者。不知道这要持续多久。我不知道。这周很残酷。我们需要这周的红色西装。确实。但幸运的是，我们的现场嘉宾有一套非常特别的西装，我很期待在下一个嘉宾之后展示给你们看，他正在休息室等着。最后一条，Joe Weisenthal 说：“除了罐头汤，一切都在无休止地抛售。”卡特彼勒表现如何？可能还不错。Campbell 今天上涨了。我喜欢。过去5天涨了6%。避险。对。买点汤。汤的避险。买点汤。Gemini 3 Pro，谷歌最智能的模型。最先进的推理，下一级的氛围编码，以及深度多模态理解。我们的下一位嘉宾正在休息室等着。我们有来自 Daytona 的 Ivan。好名字。好名字。我们喜欢 Daytona。

turns.en: All right, we got to talk about this because you had one of my favorite interactions on X. This was like a month ago. You remember you said something like 'I'm working late on a Sunday' and somebody commented like 'Why are you even doing that? Nobody knows who you are' and Ivan was like 'That's exactly why.' That's so good. Amazing. That's a great response.
turns.zh: 好了，我们得聊聊这个，因为你在 X 上有一个我最喜欢的互动。大概一个月前。你记得你说了类似‘我周日工作到很晚’的话，然后有人评论说‘你为什么要这么做？没人认识你’，然后 Ivan 说‘这正是原因。’太棒了。精彩。真是个绝妙的回应。

turns.en: Yeah, so it was basically the reason was like, it was just before the holidays and everyone was talking about 'Oh, no one took a break during the summer, so we're all taking breaks in winter' like founders and investors or whatever. And I'm thinking, are we serious? This is like a once in a generation opportunity and if you go rest, you won't probably make it, right? And I just tweeted that and then to your point, someone was like 'I've never heard of you' like exactly that's why we have to work. So yeah.
turns.zh: 是的，基本上原因是，就在假期前，大家都在说‘哦，夏天没人休息，所以我们冬天都休息’，比如创始人和投资者之类的。我在想，我们是认真的吗？这就像一代人只有一次的机会，如果你去休息，你可能就撑不下去了，对吧？我就发了那条推文，然后就像你说的，有人说‘我从没听说过你’，这正是为什么我们必须工作。所以是的。

turns.en: Well hopefully people hear about you now. Why don't you introduce yourself and the company?
turns.zh: 希望现在人们能知道你了。不如介绍一下你自己和公司吧？

turns.en: Sure. So I'm one of the co-founders and CEO of Daytona. Daytona is on a mission basically to give every single agent a computer or a sandbox as it's popularly called, right? And so the team has been working together, the founders for almost 20 years. So we're quite old and we've always been in for people. Everything from screwing together servers to creating orchestrators and whatever.
turns.zh: 当然。我是 Daytona 的联合创始人兼 CEO。Daytona 的使命基本上是给每个智能体一台电脑，或者俗称的沙盒，对吧？团队已经一起工作，创始人们差不多20年了。所以我们年纪不小了，而且一直致力于为人们服务。从组装服务器到创建编排器等等。

turns.en: Interesting. Orchestrators. Tell me more about where you're seeing the orchestration market going. I'm a big fan of GAS Town and the metaphors and it feels like it could be the hot new trend, but I don't know if it's too early or where we'll see all that go.
turns.zh: 有意思。编排器。跟我说说你觉得编排市场会怎么发展。我是 GAS Town 和那些隐喻的忠实粉丝，感觉这可能是个热门新趋势，但我不确定是不是太早了，或者我们会看到它走向何方。

turns.en: I mean trends keep changing as you've seen, like every two months things change. But one thing is for sure, and this is what I mean, I love what GAS Town is doing and all these others, which is basically the ability to spin up multiple of these agents. Each of these agents needs a computer. And I don't think we stop here with like two or four or 10 or 50, like this will go exponential. And so that's why we're doing what we're doing. So we really love what Steve is doing at GAS Town and all these other people that are creating this.
turns.zh: 我的意思是趋势一直在变，就像你看到的，每两个月事情就变了。但有一件事是肯定的，这就是我的意思，我喜欢 GAS Town 和其他所有人在做的事情，基本上就是能够快速启动多个这样的智能体。每个智能体都需要一台电脑。而且我不认为我们会停在两个、四个、十个或五十个，这会是指数级的增长。所以这就是我们正在做的。我们真的很喜欢 Steve 在 GAS Town 做的事情，以及所有其他创造这一切的人。

transcript.secZh: 编排市场与智能体需求

turns.en: Talk more about where the product is, the evolution, where the team is, kind of the current state of the company and where you want to go.
turns.zh: 再多谈谈产品的情况、演变、团队的情况、公司的现状以及你们想去哪里。

turns.en: Sure. So the company's almost 3 years old, but we actually hard pivoted and fired all our customers and everything and we decided, so yeah that was a hard one. And we saw what was coming. We weren't actually first but we did see that agents would need these computers and so we actually rebuilt the product from zero, launched it 8 months ago. And since then, the number of customers has been insane. The growth has been insane. We have everything from YC companies, growth companies, Fortune 100 companies, everyone building agents either internally or as products are using us or someone like us. So yeah.
turns.zh: 当然。公司差不多三年了，但我们实际上进行了艰难的转型，解雇了所有客户等等，我们决定，是的，那很艰难。我们看到了即将发生的事情。我们其实不是第一个，但我们确实看到了智能体需要这些电脑，所以我们从零重建了产品，8个月前发布。从那以后，客户数量疯狂增长。增长非常疯狂。我们有各种客户，从 YC 公司、成长型公司到财富100强公司，每个在内部或作为产品构建智能体的人都在使用我们或类似我们的服务。所以是的。

turns.en: Talk about why an agent needs a computer. There's been this debate over OpenClaw, formerly Claude Book, around like, oh, should you host it virtually or should you run it on a MacBook Mini? And if you do run it locally, that's a security risk. But then you talk to the people and they're like, okay, yeah, you're running in the cloud, but you still gave it access to your email. And so like you kind of let the fox in the hen house. What does it mean to actually provision a computer versus just APIs to other compute resources?
turns.zh: 谈谈为什么智能体需要一台电脑。关于 OpenClaw（前身为 Claude Book）一直有争论，比如，你应该虚拟托管它还是应该在 MacBook Mini 上运行？如果你在本地运行，那有安全风险。但然后你和人们聊，他们会说，好吧，你在云端运行，但你仍然给了它访问你邮件的权限。所以就像你把狐狸放进了鸡窝。实际配置一台电脑与仅仅提供其他计算资源的 API 相比，意味着什么？

turns.en: Yeah, exactly. So I think that's the most interesting part where originally agents were like these RAG-based systems. They could connect to APIs and they can usually consume information, analyze it, give you some feedback and fire up some tasks. But if you actually think about that, if that is a way a digital knowledge worker would work, the two of you wouldn't have laptops in front of you, right? It's not that ideal. It's messy. You have to download things. You have to install things. You have to analyze things. And if we think of AI agents as digital knowledge workers, it actually makes super sense that they do need these computers. The OpenClaw or whatever it's called right now. And Claude Code and whatnot. I can't, there's like three names. OpenClaw. OpenClaw. Yeah. So this just shows and everyone buying Mac Minis. I think this just made people understand why it needs computers.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。所以我认为最有趣的部分是，最初智能体就像是基于 RAG 的系统。它们可以连接到 API，通常可以消费信息、分析信息、给你反馈并启动一些任务。但如果你仔细想想，如果那是数字知识工作者工作的方式，你们俩就不会面前有笔记本电脑了，对吧？那并不理想。很混乱。你必须下载东西。你必须安装东西。你必须分析东西。如果我们把 AI 智能体视为数字知识工作者，那么它们确实需要这些电脑就非常有道理了。OpenClaw 或者现在叫什么。还有 Claude Code 等等。我记不清，好像有三个名字。OpenClaw。OpenClaw。是的。所以这正好表明，每个人都在买 Mac Mini。我认为这让人们明白了为什么需要电脑。

transcript.secZh: 为何智能体需要计算机

transcript.sec: Agent Use Cases: Code, Browser, RL

turns.en: But if you look at our use cases and what people build, we basically have three: code and command execution, computer and browser use, and RL. The first one, like the canonical example—these aren't our customers, I'll just say the highest brands so people know—it's like if you think of Lovable, you as a human type with Lovable and it spins up a sandbox to write the code, preview the code, and run the code. Lovable has how many users? It needs hundreds of thousands of these sandboxes to be run so their agents can get there. The second one is if you need your agent to do QA testing of Booking.com's website, it needs a computer to be able to run the browser to do these things. The newest use case for us is RL environments. I think Dylan Patel was a guest yesterday on your show, and we're doing a conference next week, so he's one of our guests.
turns.zh: 但如果你看看我们的用例和人们构建的东西，我们基本上有三个：代码和命令执行、计算机和浏览器使用，以及强化学习。第一个，典型的例子——这些不是我们的客户，我只说最高端的品牌让大家知道——就像你想到 Lovable，你作为人类在 Lovable 上输入，然后它启动一个沙盒来编写代码、预览代码并运行代码。Lovable 有多少用户？它需要数十万个这样的沙盒来运行，这样他们的智能体才能工作。第二个是，如果你需要你的智能体对 Booking.com 的网站进行 QA 测试，它需要一台计算机来运行浏览器以完成这些操作。对我们来说最新的用例是强化学习环境。我想 Dylan Patel 昨天是你的节目嘉宾，我们下周要举办一个会议，所以他也是我们的嘉宾之一。

turns.en: He's going.
turns.zh: 他会去的。

turns.en: Yeah, he's going. So at the Chase Center, we rented out the Chase Center to do the conference called Compute. That's amazing. So you're very invited if you guys want to join.
turns.zh: 是的，他会去。我们在 Chase Center 租了场地，举办一个叫 Compute 的会议。太棒了。所以如果你们想参加，非常欢迎。

turns.en: And so he has this article about RL environments and how most of the models have now over the last 18 months become better because of post-training and spinning up these RL environments. And these companies to spin up these RL environments, they need not only fast computers or sandboxes, they need them in concurrency. So they need 100,000 or 200,000 to be spun up in an order of like 3 minutes, right? This is not a trivial thing and not something that you can really take off the shelf. So when you think about why agents need computers, when you really think about the market of AI agents and software, that's sort of where that fits in.
turns.zh: 他有一篇文章关于强化学习环境，以及过去18个月里大多数模型如何因为后训练和启动这些强化学习环境而变得更好。这些公司要启动强化学习环境，不仅需要快速的计算机或沙盒，还需要并发能力。所以他们需要在3分钟内启动10万或20万个实例，对吧？这不是一件小事，也不是现成就能搞定的。所以当你思考为什么智能体需要计算机，当你真正思考AI智能体和软件的市场时，这就是它的定位。

turns.en: I want Dylan Patel doing the Lil Yachty walk out that he's made fake AI versions of it so many times and now he's at the Chase Center, 1500 builders there. You got to get the Lil Yachty playing so he can do the walk out. I saw First Mark in the chat. Give us the news. What happened?
turns.zh: 我希望 Dylan Patel 做 Lil Yachty 的出场秀，他做了很多次假的AI版本，现在他在 Chase Center，有1500个开发者在那里。你得放 Lil Yachty 的音乐让他走红毯。我在聊天里看到 First Mark 了。告诉我们新闻。发生了什么？

turns.en: Break down.
turns.zh: 详细说说。

turns.en: Yes, we raised a $24 million Series A.
turns.zh: 是的，我们筹集了2400万美元的A轮融资。

turns.en: Boom. Congratulations.
turns.zh: 哇。恭喜。

turns.en: Thank you so much. Thank you so much.
turns.zh: 非常感谢。非常感谢。

turns.en: Yeah, very impressive progress and I'm sure you'll be back on very soon and have a great time at the conference pulling together 1500 people. Not easy.
turns.zh: 是的，非常令人印象深刻的进展，我相信你很快就会回来，祝你在会议上玩得开心，聚集1500人。不容易。

turns.en: We're excited. Thanks so much. We'll talk so much. Great to meet you, Evan. Have a good rest of your day. Goodbye.
turns.zh: 我们很兴奋。非常感谢。我们聊了很多。很高兴见到你，Evan。祝你今天愉快。再见。

turns.en: And without further ado, we've been keeping our next guest waiting. He's live in the TBP Ultra Dome. It's Scott. You tell him he's suited up. Thank you so much for coming. Look at this suit. Is that game worn? Someone's got to bring some swag to the show, you know. No quarter zips here.
turns.zh: 事不宜迟，我们让下一位嘉宾久等了。他在 TBP Ultra Dome 现场。是 Scott。你告诉他他穿西装了。非常感谢你的到来。看看这套西装。是比赛穿过的吗？总得有人给节目带点范儿来，你知道的。这里没有半拉链。

turns.en: No half-ass custom suits only.
turns.zh: 只有不马虎的定制西装。

turns.en: Actually, yeah. What's the origin of the suit?
turns.zh: 实际上，是的。这套西装是怎么来的？

turns.en: No, Shinasty. I got to prep.
turns.zh: 不，Shinasty。我得准备一下。

turns.en: No, you guys got your sponsors. I got my Shinesty.com. They make all my custom suits. By the way, it's a shame the sports card company Fleer isn't still in business. Fleer. Remember Fleer sports cards?
turns.zh: 不，你们有你们的赞助商。我有我的 Shinesty.com。他们做我所有的定制西装。顺便说一句，可惜体育卡公司 Fleer 已经不在了。Fleer。还记得 Fleer 体育卡吗？

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 记得。

turns.en: Because then they could have called this the Fleer Ultra Dome.
turns.zh: 因为那样他们就可以把这个叫做 Fleer Ultra Dome 了。

turns.en: Yeah. Been giving out gold medallions. I had to do this for Dylan. Dylan needed a clear. He's a big fan. Yeah. How'd you meet Dylan?
turns.zh: 是啊。一直在发金牌。我得为 Dylan 做这个。Dylan 需要一个清晰的。他是铁粉。是的。你怎么认识 Dylan 的？

turns.en: Dylan and I met at HQ.
turns.zh: Dylan 和我在 HQ 认识的。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: When he came on board, I was hosting it. He came on board as a partnerships guy.
turns.zh: 他加入的时候，我是主持人。他是作为合作伙伴负责人加入的。

turns.en: Absolute wizard. He is.
turns.zh: 绝对的奇才。他是。

turns.en: You guys are so lucky. We have him.
turns.zh: 你们太幸运了。我们有他。

turns.en: We are with Savvy. If we were just a little earlier market, maybe we could have hired him first, but yeah.
turns.zh: 我们在 Savvy 有他。如果我们市场稍微早一点，也许我们就能先雇到他，但算了。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. You guys, we'll poach him one day. I'm pretty sure.
turns.zh: 是的。是的。你们，我们总有一天会挖走他的。我很确定。

turns.en: Yeah. Introduce that'll be a good talent work. That'll be a good day.
turns.zh: 是啊。介绍那会是很好的人才工作。那会是美好的一天。

turns.en: And then yeah. Introduce Savvy, get us up to speed, and then of course we'll go back and talk a lot about HQ.
turns.zh: 然后是的。介绍一下 Savvy，让我们了解最新情况，然后我们当然会回头聊很多关于 HQ 的事。

turns.en: Sure. Yeah. So Dylan and I met at HQ where if viewers aren't familiar, was this live interactive mobile game show viral phenomenon back in 2017. You guys play?
turns.zh: 当然。是的。所以 Dylan 和我在 HQ 认识的，如果观众不熟悉的话，那是2017年一个直播互动手机游戏秀的病毒式现象。你们玩过吗？

turns.en: Oh yeah. Of course.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。当然。

turns.en: All right. All right.
turns.zh: 好吧。好吧。

turns.en: I never won. I don't think I ever.
turns.zh: 我从没赢过。我觉得我从没赢过。

turns.en: Neither did I, man.
turns.zh: 我也没有，老兄。

turns.en: Oh yeah. Well, you were playing insider trading.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。好吧，你在玩内幕交易。

turns.en: It's tough though. Honestly, part of the journey to Savvy is taking a lot of the learnings and insights from HQ, which was a tremendous success at first, viral international hit, but when you think about it and in hindsight, you go, 'Oh, maybe it was a flawed product because it was this really impossible game to win, right? Very few people actually won it.' A lot of very smart people, including Stephen Colbert when I did his show, he told me behind the curtain, he didn't do this on camera, he goes, 'I played your game a couple times. Didn't win. Stop playing.' And it's like, 'Oh, interesting.' Because if you're a smart person, you feel losing over and over and over. It's not a good feeling. So from that perspective, it was maybe not the best because yeah, if you play Fortnite or Call of Duty and you're not doing well, they will pair you with other people that are at your skill ceiling. Like skill-based matchmaking happens in most games. You go to chess.com, you'll be matched with someone at your level. So you can get a couple wins under your belt. With HQ, it just gets more and more competitive.
turns.zh: 不过很难。老实说，通往 Savvy 的旅程的一部分是吸取了 HQ 的许多经验教训，HQ 一开始非常成功，病毒式国际热门，但当你回想起来，事后看来，你会想，‘哦，也许这是一个有缺陷的产品，因为它是一个几乎不可能赢的游戏，对吧？很少有人真正赢了。’很多非常聪明的人，包括 Stephen Colbert，当我上他的节目时，他在幕后告诉我，他没有在镜头前说，他说，‘我玩过你的游戏几次。没赢。不玩了。’然后我就想，‘哦，有意思。’因为如果你是一个聪明人，你会感觉一次又一次地输。这不是好感觉。所以从这个角度看，也许不是最好的，因为如果你玩《堡垒之夜》或《使命召唤》表现不好，他们会把你和技能上限相近的人配对。就像大多数游戏都有基于技能的匹配。你去 chess.com，你会和水平相当的人配对。这样你就能赢几场。而 HQ 只会越来越有竞争性。

turns.en: Exactly.
turns.zh: 正是。

turns.en: And then the company obviously has to raise the bar or else everyone wins.
turns.zh: 然后公司显然必须提高门槛，否则每个人都赢。

turns.en: Yes. And iterating on the products, adding features, adding new formats. They just never quite got there. So with Savvy, one of the takeaways is we want to make this game something that everybody can play all the way through. Because with HQ, if you got out on question two out of 12, you were done. That's it.
turns.zh: 是的。而且迭代产品、添加功能、添加新格式。他们就是没能做到。所以对于 Savvy，一个要点是我们想让这个游戏成为每个人都能从头玩到尾的东西。因为在 HQ，如果你在12题中的第2题就出局了，你就完了。就是这样。

turns.en: Yeah, that's right. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。是的。

turns.en: So now you could play all five rounds of Savvy, which are word puzzles. We thought about trivia, but I think AI has put a kaibosh on that because it's just too easy with these bots, man.
turns.zh: 所以现在你可以玩 Savvy 的全部五轮，都是字谜。我们考虑过 trivia，但我觉得 AI 已经把它扼杀了，因为用这些机器人太容易了，老兄。

turns.en: Yeah. Somebody would have just like the biggest bot farm in the world just trying just winning every single time.
turns.zh: 是啊。有人会拥有世界上最大的机器人农场，每次都试图赢。

turns.en: I mean, they were already doing that eight years ago. So just imagine what you can do now.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，他们八年前就已经在这么做了。所以想象一下你现在能做什么。

transcript.secZh: 智能体用例：代码、浏览器、强化学习

turns.en: You guys were dealing with bots.
turns.zh: 你们当时在对付机器人。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, I don't know if it was AI back then, but there were Discord farms and bot people. I'm not a tech guy, so I don't really know how that stuff works. But with Savvy, we're doing word puzzles. It's basically a live Wordle meets Connections. I don't know if you play those games.
turns.zh: 是啊。我的意思是，我不知道当时是不是AI，但确实有Discord农场和机器人玩家。我不是技术专家，所以不太懂那些东西。但Savvy做的是文字谜题，基本上就是现场版的Wordle加上Connections。不知道你玩不玩这些游戏。

turns.en: So it's a version, our version of that. Five rounds, timed rounds. You can score points.
turns.zh: 所以它是我们自己的版本。五轮，限时回合，可以得分。

turns.en: And the key differentiator: you're playing against me.
turns.zh: 关键的区别在于：你在和我对战。

turns.en: Yeah. It's you.
turns.zh: 对，就是你。

turns.en: It's the host. The host plays too. So imagine HQ if I was asking the questions and answering them, and if you answered more than me, you won. Imagine if that's how HQ works. That's how Savvy works.
turns.zh: 是主持人。主持人也参与。所以想象一下HQ，如果是我在提问并回答，而你答得比我多就赢了。想象HQ那样运作。Savvy就是这样。

turns.en: So if you score more points than me, if you solve the puzzles quicker, you beat the host, you're a hostbuster.
turns.zh: 所以如果你得分比我高，解谜更快，你就打败了主持人，你就是“主持人终结者”。

turns.en: A hostbuster.
turns.zh: 主持人终结者。

transcript.sec: Day in the life and move to LA

turns.en: What's a day in the life like then? Right now.
turns.zh: 那你现在的一天是怎样的？

turns.en: Well, I just moved to LA last week. So I'm trying to get my day in the life. I was here a few years ago. New York guy before, New York my whole life. I spent about 21 to 24 in LA, moved back to New York for some relationships, and came back here for my new relationship, my new commitment.
turns.zh: 嗯，我上周刚搬到洛杉矶。所以还在适应新生活。几年前我在这里待过。之前是纽约人，一辈子都在纽约。21到24岁左右在洛杉矶，因为一些感情关系搬回纽约，现在又为了新的感情关系——我的新承诺——回来了。

turns.en: This is because frankly the time...
turns.zh: 这主要是因为时间……

turns.en: Married to the game. I'm not married to anyone else but this game. I'm committed like Kurt Russell to Goldie Hawn. That level of commitment.
turns.zh: 和这个游戏结婚了。我没和别人结婚，只和这个游戏。我的承诺就像Kurt Russell对Goldie Hawn那样。

turns.en: Arizona iced tea 99 cents. That commitment. That's my commitment.
turns.zh: Arizona冰茶99美分。那种承诺。那就是我的承诺。

turns.en: But I'm here for a week, so I really haven't gotten my day-in-the-life routine yet. I walk the dog in the morning. I'm still getting up early with that time zone difference. Having my coffee, making breakfast, doing a great job.
turns.zh: 但我才来一周，所以还没有固定的日常。早上遛狗，因为时差还是早起。喝咖啡，做早餐，做得不错。

turns.en: Stay on New York time.
turns.zh: 保持纽约时间。

turns.en: I thought about that. It might happen. You guys are up early for the show. So it actually helps, kind of staying in there.
turns.zh: 我想过。可能会这样。你们为了节目起得很早，所以保持纽约时间其实有帮助。

turns.en: We do the workout, go get breakfast, prep the show, go live at 11. How important is going live at a particular time, the iteration, any of this recording? How are you thinking about rotating in other hosts, your workflow and involvement? Because I feel like the unlock is the talent, is the host.
turns.zh: 我们锻炼、吃早餐、准备节目，11点直播。在特定时间直播、迭代、录制这些有多重要？你怎么考虑引入其他主持人、你的工作流程和参与度？因为我觉得关键就是人才，就是主持人。

turns.en: For sure. We're going to definitely find other hosts. We have to. I already talked today about doing a gig in Miami on March 18th. So we're going to have other hosts, other shows. That's all going to happen. But look, this is so cool being here, first of all, watching you guys grow the way you have. I've been following along and watching. It's very cool to see.
turns.zh: 当然。我们肯定会找其他主持人。必须的。我今天还谈了一个3月18日在迈阿密的演出。所以我们会找其他主持人，做其他节目。这些都会发生。但首先，来到这里看着你们这样成长真的很酷。我一直关注着，看着你们发展。

turns.en: There's a lot of HQ in here.
turns.zh: 这里有很多HQ的影子。

turns.en: There's a lot of HQ. There's some Clubhouse. There's a lot of different stuff that has been tried, and we sort of pieced it back together.
turns.zh: 有很多HQ，也有一些Clubhouse。有很多不同的尝试，我们把它重新拼凑起来。

turns.en: And you realize the importance of cadence, of going live every day. For you guys, having that long show. For us, a short burst of tight attention span, but a whole different thing. We're playing a game.
turns.zh: 你意识到节奏的重要性，每天直播。对你们来说，是长节目。对我们来说，是短时间高专注的爆发，但完全是另一回事。我们在玩游戏。

turns.en: For example, we were doing beta shows weekly in New York. And I knew going in, weekly isn't going to really... never do anything weekly. Always strongly.
turns.zh: 比如，我们之前在纽约每周做测试节目。我一开始就知道，每周一次不行……永远不要每周做任何事。要坚决。

turns.en: I like that.
turns.zh: 我喜欢这个说法。

turns.en: So we started doing daily shows. Now we go live five times a week, Sunday through Thursday, school nights, at 9:00 p.m. Eastern. Last Thursday we had 1,200 peak concurrent, about 1,500 total entered. Last night, less than a week later, 3,000. So we've doubled week over week.
turns.zh: 所以我们开始做每日节目。现在每周直播五次，周日至周四，上学日的晚上，东部时间晚上9点。上周四我们有1200人同时在线峰值，总共约1500人参与。昨晚，不到一周后，有3000人。所以每周翻倍增长。

turns.en: There you go. No monetization?
turns.zh: 不错。没有变现吗？

turns.en: We sold one t-shirt on Teespring. Got three bucks for that. I think we made four cents on TikTok live simulcast. So we are post-monetization, post-revenue. But I'm bootstrapping this thing.
turns.zh: 我们在Teespring上卖了一件T恤，赚了三美元。在TikTok直播同步播出上大概赚了四美分。所以我们处于后变现、后收入阶段。但我在自力更生做这件事。

transcript.secZh: 日常生活与搬到洛杉矶

turns.en: I was going to ask: you talked about some of the product challenges with HQ. If the average user is losing every single night, that's rough. But what about from a business standpoint? It also feels like HQ was in this position where once it had real venture dollars behind it, you just have to go for scale. And when you're bootstrapping, as long as the business is sustainable, there's no reason to stop. You can reach a point where you're like, okay, we've hit a ceiling, and that's okay. Maybe there is no ceiling, maybe there is, but it doesn't really matter either way.
turns.zh: 我正想问：你提到了HQ的一些产品挑战。如果普通用户每晚都输，那很糟糕。但从商业角度看呢？感觉HQ一旦有了风险投资，就必须追求规模。而当你自力更生时，只要业务可持续，就没有理由停止。你可以达到一个点，觉得好吧，我们碰到了天花板，那也没关系。也许没有天花板，也许有，但无论如何都不重要。

turns.en: Yeah. I don't have a very sanguine view about VC culture based on my experience with HQ. You give a bunch of young people a lot of money and just say go scale a company, go lead a team, go manage people. Not very many people can go compete with Facebook, go compete with Google. But even just personal business sense: most of these people didn't go to business school. Anyway, my point is we have a philosophy here where we're really trying to bring as much as we can to this as a full team. I'm putting my money in. One of my other co-founders is putting money in. We are really trying to commit to doing this ourselves for as long as we can. We did take a couple meetings with some early-stage seed guys just to kind of validate the product. I won't tell you to grow.
turns.zh: 是的。根据我在HQ的经验，我对VC文化并不乐观。你给一群年轻人很多钱，然后说去扩大公司规模，去领导团队，去管理人。没多少人能去和Facebook、Google竞争。但即使只是个人商业头脑：这些人大多没上过商学院。总之，我的观点是，我们这里的理念是真正作为一个完整团队尽我们所能。我自己投钱，另一位联合创始人也投钱。我们真的在努力尽可能长久地自己做这件事。我们确实和几个早期种子轮的人开了几次会，只是为了验证产品。我不会告诉你要扩张。

turns.en: The other question is: is this almost more suitable for someone who is in the entertainment industry, not thinking about this as a tech company or an AI company, but more about the values in the talent, the values in the community, the values in the product?
turns.zh: 另一个问题是：这是否更适合娱乐行业的人，不把它看作科技公司或AI公司，而是更看重人才的价值、社区的价值、产品的价值？

turns.en: Yeah. I feel like in Hollywood and entertainment, it's very much calculus of: hey, if we can spend $20 million to make this movie and it makes $80 million, that's a win. And in VC, if a company says we're raising $20 million and we only ever want to make $80 million, then we're opening it up and moving on to this sequel maybe, which also is insane to me because $60 million is not bad. I'll take a quarter of that. So no, I mean, listen, I get it. There's a whole ecosystem of the investor. And we are certainly open to it.
turns.zh: 是的。我觉得在好莱坞和娱乐业，逻辑是：嘿，如果我们花2000万美元拍这部电影，赚了8000万，那就是胜利。而在VC界，如果一家公司说我们融资2000万，只想赚8000万，那我们就开放它，也许继续做续集，这对我来说也很疯狂，因为6000万并不差。给我四分之一就行。所以不，我的意思是，我理解。有一个完整的投资者生态系统。我们当然对此持开放态度。

turns.en: How do you originally get the job at HQ?
turns.zh: 你最初是怎么得到 HQ 那份工作的？

turns.en: Oh, that was an audition, man. An audition. So, yeah, even though it was a tech company, they held full auditions.
turns.zh: 哦，那是试镜，老兄。试镜。所以，是的，尽管那是一家科技公司，他们还是举行了完整的试镜。

turns.en: How did you see it? Like a talent casting website or a Craigslist ad?
turns.zh: 你是怎么看到的？比如人才选角网站还是 Craigslist 广告？

turns.en: It was purely through a friend. A guy I used to work with at The Onion.
turns.zh: 纯粹是通过一个朋友。一个我以前在《洋葱报》一起工作过的人。

turns.en: Let's give it up for networking.
turns.zh: 为人脉网络鼓掌。

turns.en: Oh, I love the networking. Yes.
turns.zh: 哦，我喜欢人脉网络。是的。

turns.en: The Onion. You worked there?
turns.zh: 《洋葱报》。你在那里工作过？

turns.en: So, in 2008, I interned there. And this guy Nick was the photo editor and we became friends, you know, a couple of young guys, funny guys hanging out in New York. And we stayed friends throughout, on Facebook mostly. But I got my roommate a job at the company that was HQ. It turns out Nick had put out a message on Facebook saying, 'Hey, I'm looking for an animator.' My roommate was an animator. So Russ got the job there.
turns.zh: 所以，2008 年我在那里实习。这个叫 Nick 的人是图片编辑，我们成了朋友，你知道，几个年轻人，有趣的家伙，在纽约一起玩。我们一直保持朋友关系，主要是在 Facebook 上。但我帮我室友在后来成为 HQ 的那家公司找了份工作。结果 Nick 在 Facebook 上发消息说：‘嘿，我在找一个动画师。’我室友就是动画师。所以 Russ 得到了那份工作。

turns.en: And then lo and behold, a few months later, Nick calls me and goes, 'You know, we're casting a host for this game show on your phone.' I was about to move to LA. This was 2017. I already moved out of my apartment in Brooklyn. I was ready to go. And I'm like, look, I guess I'll do one more audition. I've failed every audition. Just flunked them left and right for shows like Broad City and Search Party. I remember there's a Delta commercial with Noah Synagar. I was going to take my shirt off and I was excited to do that.
turns.zh: 然后你瞧，几个月后，Nick 打电话给我说：‘你知道，我们正在为这个手机上的游戏节目选主持人。’我当时正要搬到洛杉矶。那是 2017 年。我已经搬出了布鲁克林的公寓。我准备好了要走。我想，好吧，我再试一次镜。我每次试镜都失败。在《Broad City》和《Search Party》这样的节目上左右碰壁。我记得有一个 Delta 广告，和 Noah Synagar 一起。我本来要脱掉衬衫，我很兴奋能那样做。

turns.en: You were dice.
turns.zh: 你当时很冒险。

turns.en: I failed all those auditions and this is the one audition I got, the last one I took, and it changed my life. And what can I say? It just... but you know, you mentioned the whole media and tech hybrid, and that's what made HQ very unique, but also what makes Savvy unique.
turns.zh: 我所有那些试镜都失败了，而这是我得到的唯一一次试镜，我参加的最后一个，它改变了我的生活。我还能说什么呢？只是……但你知道，你提到了整个媒体和科技的混合体，这正是 HQ 非常独特的原因，也是 Savvy 独特的原因。

turns.en: Look, HQ had interest from NBC, Disney, Fox, also Facebook, also the venture capital, you know, Founders Fund put in 15 million. But very few companies have that type of dual interest and attraction from those major spheres of influence and money. And it's about managing those types of investment parts, but also just managing the product and understanding that when you're doing a live interactive game show on a phone, on your own app, just like what you guys are doing in terms of building your own network, you've got to think about the technology involved. Look at what's happening with streaming and everything, but it's a show. It's entertainment. And you guys are doing it all, just like we're doing it all. It's exciting. It's very heavy. Could be scary at times. Very few people are doing it. So, give ourselves some credit for doing the media-tech hybrid.
turns.zh: 听着，HQ 曾引起 NBC、迪士尼、福克斯的兴趣，还有 Facebook，以及风险投资，你知道，Founders Fund 投了 1500 万。但很少有公司能同时从这些主要影响力和资金领域获得这种双重兴趣和吸引力。这关乎管理这些投资部分，但也关乎管理产品，并理解当你在手机上、在自己的应用上做一个直播互动游戏节目时，就像你们在建立自己的网络方面所做的那样，你必须考虑所涉及的技术。看看流媒体等正在发生的事情，但这是一个节目。这是娱乐。而你们在做所有这一切，就像我们也在做所有这一切一样。这很令人兴奋。非常沉重。有时可能很可怕。很少有人在做这件事。所以，为我们自己做的媒体科技混合体点赞。

turns.en: They said it was impossible. How soon do you think Zuck will clone Savvy?
turns.zh: 他们说过这是不可能的。你觉得扎克伯格多久会克隆 Savvy？

turns.en: I would love for him to try.
turns.zh: 我很希望他试试。

turns.en: Someone else, break down how you process that.
turns.zh: 其他人，说说你怎么看待这件事。

turns.en: Listen, man. I mean, they say flattery is the... I forget what they say.
turns.zh: 听着，老兄。我的意思是，他们说奉承是……我忘了他们怎么说。

turns.en: Imitation is the sincerest form of flattery.
turns.zh: 模仿是最真诚的奉承。

turns.en: We like to say imitation just sucks.
turns.zh: 我们喜欢说模仿就是烂。

turns.en: Imitation, especially imitation crap.
turns.zh: 模仿，尤其是模仿的垃圾。

turns.en: It's for losers.
turns.zh: 那是给失败者的。

turns.en: Those garbage California rolls.
turns.zh: 那些垃圾加州卷。

turns.en: It's disgusting and despicable. Then you should be ashamed for imitating me.
turns.zh: 这令人作呕且卑鄙。那么你应该为模仿我感到羞耻。

turns.en: And you say this not flattered at all.
turns.zh: 而且你说这根本不感到荣幸。

turns.en: Right. Exactly. And nobody at CNBC would have any issue with that. But look, we all imitate. We all remix each other. But that's the thing. It's like what you guys are doing. It's like, oh, I don't need a job on CNBC. I can create my own damn CNBC. Make your own.
turns.zh: 对。完全正确。而且 CNBC 没有人会有意见。但你看，我们都模仿。我们都互相混搭。但问题就在这里。就像你们正在做的。就像，哦，我不需要 CNBC 的工作。我可以创建自己的 CNBC。自己做。

turns.en: CNBC. Of course you do.
turns.zh: CNBC。你当然可以。

turns.en: But I honestly would have never even thought like, oh, I should apply for a job there. I don't even know how to get into that world. It just comes naturally.
turns.zh: 但我老实说，我甚至从未想过，哦，我应该去那里申请工作。我甚至不知道如何进入那个世界。这很自然。

turns.en: You're right. If you asked John and I even a year and a half ago, hey, would you ever think about could we do business television? We'd be like, let's have a podcast with two microphones, just talk to each other, and it turned into a TV show kind of but streamed online.
turns.zh: 你说得对。如果你问约翰和我，甚至一年半前，嘿，你们有没有想过我们能不能做商业电视？我们会说，让我们做一个有两个麦克风的播客，只是互相聊天，然后它变成了一种电视节目，但在线直播。

transcript.sec: Commitment to Savvy and online backlash

turns.en: You know, someone asked me when I was hosting HQ, I got interviewed and they said, 'Well, where do you want to be in 5 years? What's the next move for you?' And I go, 'I'll be here for 20 years if this thing stays on. This is the future.' Unfortunately, it didn't last very long. But with Savvy, I am in this to win this for the long haul. My skin's in the game. My face is again front and center. For better or worse, I don't think America 100% loves this face, but enough people do.
turns.zh: 你知道，有人在我主持 HQ 时问我，我接受采访，他们说：‘那么，你五年后想在哪里？你的下一步是什么？’我说：‘如果这东西继续下去，我会在这里待 20 年。这是未来。’不幸的是，它没有持续很久。但对于 Savvy，我是在长期投入，志在必得。我投入了心血。我的脸再次出现在最前面和中心。无论好坏，我不认为美国 100% 喜欢这张脸，但足够多的人喜欢。

turns.en: What was chat back like that?
turns.zh: 聊天反馈怎么样？

turns.en: You don't have to like... chat can be a little rough, you know.
turns.zh: 你不必喜欢……聊天可能有点粗暴，你知道。

turns.en: We've been there. We've been banned by all sorts. Just today, earlier, I guess word leaked that Sam was coming on. We promoted it everywhere.
turns.zh: 我们经历过。我们被各种平台封禁过。就在今天早些时候，我猜消息泄露说 Sam 要来。我们到处宣传了。

turns.en: Well, no. I'm saying it leaked to the part of the internet where there are a lot of people that are really frustrated that 40, the model from OpenAI, is being deprecated. A lot of people fell in love with the model, have a very close relationship with the model. And so, we basically couldn't even look at the chat at one point because there was a message every second from somebody saying...
turns.zh: 嗯，不是。我是说它泄露到了互联网的一个角落，那里有很多人对 OpenAI 的模型 40 被弃用感到非常沮丧。很多人爱上了这个模型，与它有着非常亲密的关系。所以，我们基本上有一段时间甚至不敢看聊天，因为每秒都有人发消息说……

turns.en: I didn't realize how big that army is. I've seen a couple posts. I thought maybe it was fake AI-generated or something. They were real and they had points to make.
turns.zh: 我没意识到那个军团有多大。我看到过一些帖子。我以为可能是假的 AI 生成的之类的。他们是真实的，而且他们有观点要表达。

turns.en: Yeah. The problem with people who can't... yeah, fall in love with a fashion model.
turns.zh: 是啊。问题在于那些不能……是的，爱上一个时尚模特的人。

turns.en: Fall in love with AI models and it's a whole different... we have like a really core community that's really positive and everyone gives each other feedback. The core group is really really...
turns.zh: 爱上AI模型，这完全是另一回事……我们有一个非常核心的社区，非常积极，大家互相反馈。核心群体真的非常……

turns.en: Of course no, I mean I'm just screwing around. Obviously every chat's going to have a few trolls in there, but no, we welcome everyone. And there really are like HQ, the community of HQ was so tremendous. These HQies, I mean millions of people playing.
turns.zh: 当然不，我只是在开玩笑。显然每个聊天里都会有几个捣乱的人，但不会，我们欢迎所有人。而且HQ的社区真的非常庞大。这些HQ玩家，我的意思是数百万人都在玩。

turns.en: I've heard from people who got married because they met someone playing HQ. You know?
turns.zh: 我听说过有人因为玩HQ认识而结婚的。你知道吗？

turns.en: Yeah, strange families coming.
turns.zh: 是啊，奇妙的家庭组合。

turns.en: How many people of the early user base for Savvy are just people that were waiting for it to be reborn in some way?
turns.zh: Savvy的早期用户中有多少是那些等待它以某种方式重生的人？

turns.en: A bunch. I mean, what we noticed was again, live interactive, not a lot of people are doing it and platforms aren't built for it. So even on TikTok and Twitch, when people are going live, they're finding hacks to make interactive gaming, but no one's doing this one-to-one interactive, the host versus the audience, streamer versus audience. Very unique. And that's kind of the thesis behind building our platform: we want to make this streamer-to-audience interaction that no one else is doing, live appointment-based games, cash prizes, the whole shebang.
turns.zh: 很多。我的意思是，我们注意到的是，直播互动，没多少人在做，平台也不是为此而建的。所以即使在TikTok和Twitch上，人们直播时也在找办法做互动游戏，但没人做这种一对一的互动，主持人对观众，主播对观众。非常独特。这基本上就是我们搭建平台的理念：我们想做这种别人没做过的主播与观众的互动，直播预约制游戏，现金奖励，全套服务。

turns.en: So how do you think about syndication and marketing like turning the show that's live interactive? That's a very high bar. I mean, we see it with like the number of people that show up live and watch the whole show is way lower than the people that watch our RSS feed or YouTube videos or shorts or clips. And so, how do you take something that you got to be there, but if you see it, you might be like, I want to be there for the next one.
turns.zh: 那么你怎么看待联合播出和营销，比如把直播互动的节目做成那样？门槛很高。我的意思是，我们看到，现场观看完整节目的人数远低于观看我们RSS订阅或YouTube视频、短视频或片段的人数。那么，你怎么把一个需要现场参与的东西，如果别人看到了，可能会想，下次我要去现场？

turns.en: Exactly. Well, that's the marketing, right? I mean, we haven't spent any money on marketing. It's really just been my social media posts that have gotten us to this 3,000 people or so.
turns.zh: 没错。这就是营销，对吧？我的意思是，我们没花一分钱在营销上。全靠我的社交媒体帖子，才让我们有了这3000人左右。

turns.en: That's great. And then of course, you know, coming on shows like this. I'm hoping we get the TBPN bump tonight.
turns.zh: 那很好。当然，你知道，上这种节目。我希望今晚能带来TBPN的流量增长。

turns.en: Oh yeah, let's do it.
turns.zh: 哦，是的，来吧。

turns.en: I want to crack that 3500. We get to 3500.
turns.zh: 我想突破3500。我们到3500。

turns.en: If it happens, we're taking credit. We're taking credit. You should happen.
turns.zh: 如果实现了，我们就邀功。我们邀功。你应该实现。

turns.en: You should. I mean, how about even Sam Altman's trolls will take you to...
turns.zh: 你应该。我的意思是，就连Sam Altman的黑粉都会把你带到……

turns.en: Tell them that Foro is hosting 40. A dangerous proposition forever and can never never stop.
turns.zh: 告诉他们Foro在主持40。一个永远危险且永不停歇的提议。

turns.en: Wait, so you touched on monetization earlier, but is this the kind of thing you get... if 10,000 people, 20,000, 100,000 some days showing up every single night, they would happily pay some amount of money for that experience, right?
turns.zh: 等等，你之前提到了变现，但这是那种……如果每天有1万、2万、10万人每晚都来，他们会很乐意为此付费，对吧？

turns.en: Absolutely. Again, to bring it back to Dylan, who was the one responsible for getting those deals on board, I mean, we had ultimately we were doing million-dollar deals for single shows.
turns.zh: 绝对。再说回Dylan，他是负责促成那些交易的人，我的意思是，我们最终为单场节目做了百万美元的交易。

turns.en: Yeah. But why wouldn't a player just subscribe too? So you have...
turns.zh: 是啊。但为什么玩家不直接订阅呢？所以你有……

turns.en: We have subscriptions.
turns.zh: 我们有订阅。

turns.en: Paid subscriptions. So you have that, because I think if you're running...
turns.zh: 付费订阅。所以你有这个，因为我觉得如果你在运营……

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, I can just imagine if you have 100,000 people that play the game all the time, why would they not play? There's a lot of value there.
turns.zh: 是啊。我的意思是，我可以想象如果你有10万人一直在玩游戏，他们为什么不玩呢？那里有很多价值。

turns.en: No. Yeah. We're going to do subscriptions. We're rolling that out for our season one premiere March 1st.
turns.zh: 不。是的。我们要做订阅。我们将在3月1日第一季首播时推出。

turns.en: Subscriptions and of course sponsorships could be a part of that, too. But no, absolutely. I mean, you know, when we see people on Twitch donating $10 a month to their streamers for nothing really in return, it's like well if we can offer... I mean it's just donate, it's like Patreon, it's support. No, it's support and it's an amazing community of support out there from fans to artists, right, fans to content creators. So we don't have that mechanism yet, but when we roll out the subscription, it's like hey, if you love Savvy, if you love rock with me, throw us some cash and then we'll give you your digital rewards, your customizations, skins, exclusive games, though maybe higher prize.
turns.zh: 订阅当然，赞助也可以成为其中的一部分。但没错，绝对。我的意思是，你知道，当我们看到人们在Twitch上每月给主播捐10美元却几乎没什么回报时，就好像如果我们能提供……我的意思是，这只是捐赠，就像Patreon，是支持。不，这是支持，而且外面有一个了不起的支持社区，从粉丝到艺术家，对吧，粉丝到内容创作者。所以我们还没有那个机制，但当我们推出订阅时，就像，嘿，如果你喜欢Savvy，如果你喜欢和我一起摇滚，给我们一些钱，然后我们会给你数字奖励、定制、皮肤、独家游戏，也许还有更高的奖金。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Talk to me about a million-dollar HQ show. Like what does that feel like for you? What's an example? Walk me through sort of like how those big shows, those big monetization moments came together.
turns.zh: 是啊。是啊。跟我聊聊百万美元的HQ节目。对你来说感觉如何？举个例子？带我回顾一下那些大型节目、那些大的变现时刻是如何实现的。

turns.en: How they came together was we were able to get a million people playing the game live. If you get a million live devices, and it turns out, guys, if Dylan only knew this when we were making those deals, it probably could have doubled money because later on we hired Nielsen, the ratings company, to come in and do a study. And they put like a 1.7% multiple on our viewers. So, frankly, people are looking over your shoulder. Exactly. If there's one person holding a phone, maybe there's an office all playing together, family playing together. So, honestly, two and a half million connected devices was our peak with The Rock when he hosted. That was a Warner Brothers sponsored event. It was sponsored for Warner Brothers for a movie to promote Rampage, I believe was the movie at the time. Instant classic, modern classic Rampage, of course.
turns.zh: 它们是如何实现的呢？我们能让一百万人同时在线玩游戏。如果你有一百万台在线设备，结果发现，伙计们，如果Dylan在做那些交易时知道这一点，可能钱能翻倍，因为后来我们请了尼尔森评级公司来做研究。他们给我们的观众乘了一个1.7的倍数。所以，坦白说，有人在你背后看。没错。如果一个人拿着手机，可能整个办公室一起玩，一家人一起玩。所以，老实说，250万台连接设备是我们的峰值，当时是The Rock主持的。那是华纳兄弟赞助的活动，是为了推广一部电影，我记得当时是《狂暴巨兽》。当然是经典，现代经典《狂暴巨兽》。

turns.en: Okay. Of course, still still getting paid.
turns.zh: 好的。当然，还在赚钱。

turns.en: Still talk about that movie.
turns.zh: 还在谈论那部电影。

turns.en: Two but 2.5 million connected devices really. Yeah. Close to like 5 million viewers.
turns.zh: 但250万台连接设备是真的。是啊。接近500万观众。

turns.en: And then what's your interaction with the Warner Brothers team on that? Like are you stopping... Yeah. During the show, are you just overlays, graphics? Are you stopping doing ad reads? It's like what do they want to really make that pop for a million bucks?
turns.zh: 那么你和华纳兄弟团队在那次合作中是如何互动的？比如，你们会停下来……是的。在节目期间，你们只是叠加画面、图形吗？你们会停下来做广告口播吗？他们想要什么才能让那一百万美元花得值？

turns.en: It was, I mean, we showed the trailer. We played the trailer in the lobby and we definitely talked about it. Probably wrote a whole script around it. I think we did a Ready Player One deal, right? Maybe some questions that are linked, some questions linked to it. A lot of graphical integration, the UX UI, but I think we wore like the Ready Player One goggles even. That kind of stuff. And live product reads are going to be part of Savvy as well. I mean, again, we're taking the live podcast ecosystem of ads, as you guys well know, you guys are like NASCAR drivers over here.
turns.zh: 是这样的，我们播放了预告片。我们在大厅里播放了预告片，而且我肯定提到了它。可能围绕它写了整个脚本。我想我们做了《头号玩家》的合作，对吧？可能有一些相关的问题。很多图形整合，用户体验和界面，但我认为我们甚至戴了《头号玩家》的眼镜。诸如此类。直播产品口播也将成为Savvy的一部分。我的意思是，再次，我们借鉴了直播播客的广告生态系统，你们很清楚，你们就像这里的NASCAR车手。

turns.en: Oh, yeah.
turns.zh: 哦，是啊。

turns.en: Do you think that energy or compute will be a bigger bottleneck?
turns.zh: 你认为能源还是算力会是更大的瓶颈？

turns.en: Great question. And I don't know what either one of those words means. No. Um, you know, yeah, it's going to get expensive, but I expect you guys to train your own foundation models, our own rep.
turns.zh: 好问题。我不知道这两个词是什么意思。不。嗯，你知道，是的，它会变得昂贵，但我希望你们训练自己的基础模型，我们自己的代表。

turns.en: We got a foundation model right here. AI. You're a foundation model of a man.
turns.zh: 我们这里就有一个基础模型。AI。你是一个人的基础模型。

turns.en: I'm a foundational element.
turns.zh: 我是一个基础元素。

turns.en: I'm a fundamental aspect of consciousness live on your screen. You know, real humans are going to be a scarce commodity in the near future. So, how are you thinking about building the team?
turns.zh: 我是你屏幕上活生生的意识基本面向。你知道，在不久的将来，真人会变成稀缺商品。那么，你打算怎么组建团队？

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, it's important obviously. I'm CEO, CMO, co-founder technically. So we've got our COO Josh, our CTO Ben, and CEO Johan. Johan and Ben are based in Europe. Actually most of our team is in Europe, we're distributed across the globe. They're doing a lot of the hiring decisions there for the key roles. Backend stuff we're looking, but we are looking for animators, social media manager, community manager is key. And really we're looking for just the 'be do have' way of thinking. We just want people who are going to be their best, bring their best self to the job. If they're doing that, then they're going to have success and we're going to do well.
turns.zh: 是的，这显然很重要。我严格来说是CEO、CMO兼联合创始人。我们有COO Josh、CTO Ben和CEO Johan。Johan和Ben在欧洲。实际上我们大部分团队都在欧洲，分布在全球各地。他们在那里负责很多关键岗位的招聘决策。我们在找后端人才，但也在找动画师、社交媒体经理，社区经理是关键。我们真正想要的是那种‘成为、行动、拥有’思维方式的人。我们只希望人们能全力以赴，把最好的自己带到工作中。如果他们能做到，他们就会成功，我们也会做得很好。

turns.en: Yeah. Did you get any inbound from companies that wanted to dip their toe in live? PostHQ people that want to like... What was the PostHQ sort of idea maze for you?
turns.zh: 是的。有没有公司主动联系你，想涉足直播领域？HQ之后的人想做什么？HQ之后，你的想法迷宫是什么样的？

turns.en: Oh, it was wild, man.
turns.zh: 哦，太疯狂了，老兄。

turns.en: Because I imagine you could just go and say, 'I'm going to be an actor. Put me in a TV show or whatever. There's game shows you compete in that.' There's also a lot of tech people that were aware of you. How did you navigate that?
turns.zh: 因为我想你可以直接说，‘我要当演员，把我放进电视剧或者别的什么里。有游戏节目你可以参加。’还有很多科技圈的人知道你。你是怎么应对的？

turns.en: Well, I mentioned the failed auditions before. I'm not much of an actor, it turns out, so I wasn't going to go that route.
turns.zh: 嗯，我之前提到过失败的试镜。结果证明我不太会演戏，所以我不打算走那条路。

turns.en: Play yourself well. I do play myself and that's all I really need to know how to play, just me.
turns.zh: 演好你自己。我确实演我自己，这就是我需要知道怎么演的全部，就我自己。

turns.en: The way I look at it is like there were probably two dozen people that contacted me over the last seven years. I had this baseball show, I went from HQ to this baseball show called 'Change Up' on Zone. Supposed to be a three-year deal. COVID killed that. The major leagues became the force majeure leagues, is the joke I like to make. They killed it because baseball went on hiatus. There was no need for the show and they used it as an opportunity to pull the plug. Brutal. So I lost that gig. But look, I had like dozens of people reaching out about all sorts of things. I even tried to start a company back in 2019, 2020 pre-pandemic with a guy and that had its issues. But the thing about these guys who hit me up, it was Benjamin and Johan, these two randomly on Twitter. They messaged me over a year ago now and said, 'Hey, we have this idea.'
turns.zh: 我看待这件事的方式是，过去七年大概有二十多人联系过我。我做过一个棒球节目，从HQ去了Zone频道的一个叫‘Change Up’的棒球节目。本来应该是三年合约。新冠毁了它。大联盟变成了不可抗力联盟，这是我喜欢开的玩笑。他们砍掉了它，因为棒球停摆了。节目不需要了，他们借机拔掉了插头。太残酷了。所以我丢了那份工作。但是你看，有几十个人因为各种事情联系我。我甚至在2019年、2020年疫情前尝试和一个家伙创办公司，但出了问题。但关键是，联系我的这些家伙，是Benjamin和Johan，两个在Twitter上随机的人。他们一年多前给我发消息说，‘嘿，我们有个想法。’

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Interaction host versus streamer. And I'm like, I've heard a lot of pitches. I took the meeting. They showed me their demo. I was like, these guys are like 27, 28 years old, and I've met with lots of people, lots of ideas, some fly-by-night stuff, some really earnest people, but just with the right team, right product. These guys seem like they had the experience with mobile gaming. That's what they pitched me on. They go, 'We work at mobile gaming companies here in Europe.' That's what we need. So they know the retention mechanics. They don't necessarily have to have done the exact same thing, but having done something at scale, the interaction, the engagement, all these things. Something else lacking from HQ, by the way, I don't think a single person on the team had mobile gaming experience or Hollywood production experience. It was wild.
turns.zh: 互动主持人对主播。我当时想，我听过很多推销。我参加了会议。他们给我看了他们的演示。我想，这些家伙大概27、28岁，我见过很多人，很多想法，有些是昙花一现的东西，有些是非常真诚的人，但只是需要合适的团队、合适的产品。这些人似乎有移动游戏的经验。他们就是用这个来推销的。他们说，‘我们在欧洲的移动游戏公司工作。’这正是我们需要的。所以他们了解留存机制。他们不一定非得做过完全相同的事情，但做过大规模的事情，互动、参与度，所有这些。顺便说一句，HQ还缺少一样东西，我不认为团队里有任何一个人有移动游戏经验或好莱坞制作经验。这太疯狂了。

turns.en: No one had any...
turns.zh: 没有人有任何……

turns.en: Wild. No, seriously. Yeah.
turns.zh: 疯狂。不，说真的。是的。

turns.en: Does anyone here have experience any...
turns.zh: 这里有人有任何经验吗……

turns.en: I mean very talented people. I'm not taking away, but coming from Twitter, coming from Uber, coming from big tech companies. But I really don't think, maybe I'm wrong, some engineers maybe, but mobile game is a very specific field and again entertainment and Hollywood media is very specific. So I brought that entertainment side of things, these guys have the mobile gaming tech side of things. We've married together. Our CEO Josh is kind of the glue guy who's really keeping the company running and taking care of all that, and also our production manager helping set up the streams. He just did a whole overhaul of our new studio here in LA. Shout out Forever Dog Studios. Our version of the Ultra Dome. But no, I mean it takes as you know.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，他们非常有才华。我不是贬低他们，但他们来自Twitter、Uber、大型科技公司。但我真的不认为，也许我错了，可能有些工程师有，但移动游戏是一个非常具体的领域，而娱乐和好莱坞媒体也非常具体。所以我带来了娱乐方面，这些人有移动游戏技术方面。我们结合在了一起。我们的CEO Josh有点像粘合剂，他真正维持着公司运转，处理所有事情，还有我们的制作经理帮助设置直播。他刚刚彻底翻新了我们在洛杉矶的新工作室。向Forever Dog Studios致敬。我们的Ultra Dome版本。但是不，我的意思是，这需要时间，你知道的。

turns.en: Which one's this?
turns.zh: 这是哪个？

turns.en: That's a dog panting studio.
turns.zh: 那是一个狗喘气的工作室。

turns.en: You said Forever Dog. Forever dogs. We play dogs.
turns.zh: 你说了Forever Dog。永远的狗。我们扮演狗。

turns.en: I like that. Okay. Forever dog. I don't...
turns.zh: 我喜欢这个。好的。永远的狗。我不……

turns.en: There we go. The dog. Forever dog.
turns.zh: 这就对了。狗。永远的狗。

turns.en: Thinking of my guy right now. Just left my dog at home.
turns.zh: 现在正想着我的家伙。刚把我的狗留在家里。

turns.en: But no, man. It's a unique entity what we're doing. What I say 'we' because we got you guys are doing it too. How are you?
turns.zh: 但是不，老兄。我们做的事情是一个独特的实体。我说‘我们’是因为你们也在做。你怎么样？

turns.en: I love media. I love that. It's a business that doesn't exist if you don't show up and make it every single day. And that scares a lot of people. And sure, there are downsides to that. A lot of people want to say, the idea of SaaS, it's beautiful. You build this thing one time, you sell it, passive income. It's not passive. I'm talking about UGC, right? And this is the classic dilemma for me because what HQ did amazingly, the most innovative aspect of HQ was the fact that it was an app that worked for 15 minutes a day.
turns.zh: 我喜欢媒体。我喜欢这一点。这是一个如果你不每天出现并创造它就不存在的生意。这吓到了很多人。当然，这也有缺点。很多人想说，SaaS的想法很美。你一次性构建这个东西，然后卖掉，被动收入。它不是被动的。我说的是UGC，对吧？这对我来说是经典的困境，因为HQ做得最令人惊叹、最具创新性的方面是，它是一个每天只运行15分钟的应用程序。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And it was not UGC. It was PGC, producer generated. There was a single show, a single purpose on this customized platform. What a concept, right?
turns.zh: 而且它不是UGC。它是PGC，由制作人生成。在这个定制平台上只有一个节目、一个目的。多么棒的概念，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, because you could say, well, let's open up to everybody. And the funny thing is the twist on this is that HQ derived from a previous iteration of an app called Hype. So the founders, they had a company called Intermediate Labs, and it was about building apps. They built some kind of dance app for Instagram. They built an app called Hype and it was 'we're going to allow people to make their own talk shows from their bedrooms, give them templates, give them music, almost like TikTok talk show thing.' And I don't know, maybe a few thousand people downloaded it. One woman was doing a trivia show from her bedroom. And they saw the show, they're like, 'Let's just do our own show.' And that's how they got the idea for HQ Trivia. So they tried UGC at first. It wasn't working.
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，因为你可能会说，好吧，让我们向所有人开放。有趣的是，转折在于HQ源自一个之前版本的应用程序叫Hype。所以创始人有一家公司叫Intermediate Labs，专注于构建应用程序。他们为Instagram构建了一个舞蹈应用。他们构建了一个叫Hype的应用，内容是‘我们将允许人们在自己的卧室里制作自己的脱口秀，给他们模板，给他们音乐，几乎像TikTok脱口秀一样。’我不知道，也许有几千人下载了它。一个女人在她的卧室里做了一档问答节目。他们看到了这个节目，就想，‘让我们自己做节目吧。’这就是他们得到HQ Trivia想法的原因。所以他们一开始尝试了UGC。但行不通。

turns.en: The PGC... Yeah. The challenge is it's a very small market of people. Even Twitch is still so power law driven that if you take away the top 30 creators, the platform is probably worthless.
turns.zh: PGC……是的。挑战在于这是一个非常小众的市场。即使是Twitch也仍然如此受幂律驱动，如果你去掉前30名创作者，这个平台可能一文不值。

turns.en: It's very true.
turns.zh: 非常正确。

turns.en: So, we are basically gatekeeping the creators on our platform. Very, very highly curating it is probably a better way to say it. Curating it to just me right now, but we'll open up if you guys gatekeeping.
turns.zh: 所以我们基本上是在把关平台上的创作者。更准确地说，是高度精选。目前只精选了我自己，但如果你们把关，我们会开放。

turns.en: Gate is a negative word. Can't say gatekeeping anymore. But you guys, I want to bring you guys on the show.
turns.zh: Gate 是个负面词。不能再提 gatekeeping 了。但你们，我想请你们上节目。

turns.en: You'll co-host with me. We'll do cross promo. We know how it works.
turns.zh: 你来和我一起主持。我们做交叉推广。我们都懂。

turns.en: The app is in the app store and I want to read this review. It's amazing. 'HQ done right. This app is so much fun. I've missed the live game shows of the 2010s and this app delivers on the best of those. Not only is Quizdaddy back, but there's also new features that reward you just for playing the game. I'm excited to see how Savvy grows from here, but they're off to a promising start. Five stars.'
turns.zh: 应用已经在应用商店里了，我想读一下这条评论。太棒了。'HQ 做得对。这个应用太有趣了。我很怀念 2010 年代的直播游戏节目，这个应用重现了那些最好的部分。不仅 Quizdaddy 回来了，还有新功能让你仅仅玩游戏就能获得奖励。我很期待看到 Savvy 从这里如何成长，但他们已经有了一个充满希望的开端。五星。'

turns.en: Sounds like ChatGPT.
turns.zh: 听起来像 ChatGPT 写的。

turns.en: That's very nice. I mean, the feedback has been phenomenal. The community has been phenomenal.
turns.zh: 这很好。我的意思是，反馈非常棒。社区也非常棒。

turns.en: We sponsor a suit.
turns.zh: 我们赞助一套西装。

turns.en: Yeah. Do you want your own C? I'll connect.
turns.zh: 是啊。你想要自己的 C 吗？我来联系。

turns.en: No, no, no. I want to do a TVPN suit that you wear during the game.
turns.zh: 不，不，不。我想要一件你在游戏时穿的 TVPN 套装。

turns.en: Oh, I like that. Oh, you had stickers. And what about I'll give you some stickers here on your console.
turns.zh: 哦，我喜欢这个。哦，你有贴纸。那我给你一些贴纸放在你的主机上吧。

turns.en: Yeah, for sure. I'll wear a TVPN.
turns.zh: 当然。我会穿 TVPN 的。

turns.en: Let's get the custom TVPN suit.
turns.zh: 我们来定制 TVPN 套装。

turns.en: Love it. Congratulations.
turns.zh: 太棒了。恭喜。

turns.en: I'm super excited for you. It sounds like you have a fantastic team taking the founder the right way. Our friend Jeremy Gon talks about this idea of everyone is like pre or post-fall. There are people that are doing really well and yet they haven't been deeply humbled by life yet, they never suffered a setback. You've gone through that now. Daddy's eaten a helping of humble pie or two over the years.
turns.zh: 我为你超级兴奋。听起来你有一个很棒的团队，以正确的方式带领创始人。我们的朋友 Jeremy Gon 谈到一个观点，每个人要么是坠落前要么是坠落后的状态。有些人做得很好，但还没有被生活深深谦卑过，从未遭遇挫折。你现在已经经历了。Daddy 这些年来吃过一两块谦卑的馅饼。

turns.en: It tastes pretty good, actually.
turns.zh: 实际上味道还不错。

turns.en: More than your fair share. Look, I can't complain about anything. I've been so blessed even these last seven years of wandering the desert to get to this point. But I'm so excited for this launch. Truly, if we can get the TBPN community on board tonight, download the app, play savvy.live, on board the cameras, play savvy.live, Android, Apple tonight at 6:00 p.m. Pacific, 9:00 p.m. Eastern. Let's get over 3,000 concurrents. And I'm going to give you guys credit.
turns.zh: 比你应得的还多。听着，我没什么可抱怨的。即使过去七年像在沙漠中流浪，我也非常幸运能走到这一步。但我对这个发布感到非常兴奋。真的，如果我们能让 TBPN 社区今晚加入，下载应用，玩 savvy.live，打开摄像头，玩 savvy.live，安卓和苹果，今晚太平洋时间下午 6 点，东部时间晚上 9 点。让我们超过 3000 同时在线。我会给你们记功。

turns.en: Well, I think it's time to plant the bomb and get out of here. Thank you so much for coming on. We'll close out the show with you on here. Leave us five stars on Apple Podcast and Spotify and the newsletter tbpn.com. Yeah, we did have range. We went all over. It's fun. Sam to Sam to Savvy.
turns.zh: 好了，我想是时候埋下炸弹然后离开了。非常感谢你来做客。我们将以你在这里结束节目。请在 Apple Podcast 和 Spotify 上给我们五星好评，还有订阅 tbpn.com 的新闻通讯。是的，我们确实涉猎广泛。我们聊了很多。很有趣。从 Sam 到 Sam 到 Savvy。

turns.en: Nice book.
turns.zh: 好书。

turns.en: It's good stuff. Living legends.
turns.zh: 好东西。活着的传奇。

turns.en: Yeah. And we will be back tomorrow at Laughlin joining. We have a bunch of other fun folks joining the show and be a great day. Have a great rest of your day. Afternoon and evening. We love you.
turns.zh: 是的。我们明天会在 Laughlin 回来加入。我们还有一群有趣的人加入节目，会是很棒的一天。祝你今天剩下的时间愉快。下午和晚上都好。我们爱你。

turns.en: Nice work, brothers. I'll see you on the next one.
turns.zh: 干得好，兄弟们。下次见。
